topics.title: Synapses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT303 - MEDICAL PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Synapses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **synapse** is a highly specialized, sub-microscopic anatomical structure that permits the unidirectional or bidirectional transfer of a signal (electrical impulse or chemical messenger) from one neuron to another neuron, or from a neuron to an effector cell (such as a muscle fiber or glandular cell). The term was first introduced by the Nobel laureate physiologist **Sir Charles Scott Sherrington** in **1897**, derived from the Greek word *synaptein*, meaning "to clasp or to bind together."

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme PCBMT303 (Medical Physics)**, a synapse is fundamentally treated as a **bio-electrochemical transducer** — a device that converts the electrical action potential of a presynaptic neuron into a chemical signal (neurotransmitter release) and then back into an electrical signal (postsynaptic potential) in the target cell.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):** Synapses form the foundational unit of the **nervous system signal processing**. A typical adult human brain contains approximately **100 trillion (10¹⁴)** synapses, making them the principal substrate for all neural computation, learning, and memory.

## 1.2 Classification of Synapses

Synapses are broadly classified into two principal categories based on their mechanism of signal transmission:

| Type | Transmission Mode | Speed | Directionality | Presence in Humans |
|------|------------------|-------|----------------|-------------------|
| **Electrical Synapse** | Direct ionic current flow via gap junctions | Ultra-fast (≈0.1 ms) | Mostly bidirectional | Rare; found in cardiac muscle, smooth muscle, retina, CNS glial cells |
| **Chemical Synapse** | Neurotransmitter release across synaptic cleft | Slower (0.5–2 ms delay) | Strictly unidirectional | Predominant; forms >99% of synapses in the human CNS |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Letter" Model

Imagine a postal system between two cities separated by a river (the **synaptic cleft**):

- **Electrical Synapse = A Telephone Call:** Two adjacent houses are connected by a physical wire (gap junction channel). Electricity (ionic current) flows instantaneously from one house to the other. There is no "package" — only continuous current.
- **Chemical Synapse = A Postal Letter:** Person A (presynaptic terminal) writes a message on a letter (neurotransmitter), places it in a sealed envelope, and drops it across the river. The mail carrier (diffusion + reuptake mechanisms) delivers it to Person B (postsynaptic receptor). The message can only travel one way (unidirectional), and there is a finite delivery time (synaptic delay).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** The **synaptic cleft** in a chemical synapse is only **20 to 40 nanometers (nm)** wide. For perspective, a human hair is approximately **80,000 nm** in diameter. This tiny gap is precisely why the chemical signal must be **amplified** via neurotransmitter-receptor binding to overcome signal attenuation.

## 1.4 Why Synapses Are Central to Medical Physics

From a biophysical standpoint, synapses are **non-linear signal-processing elements** that perform the following critical operations:

1. **Signal Integration** — Summation of multiple inputs (spatial and temporal).
2. **Signal Filtering** — Allowing only specific frequencies of action potentials to pass.
3. **Signal Amplification** — A single presynaptic vesicle release can open thousands of postsynaptic receptors.
4. **Plasticity** — Modification of synaptic strength, the cellular basis of **learning and memory (Hebbian Theory)**.
5. **Pathophysiology** — Dysfunction of synapses underlies epilepsy, myasthenia gravis, Parkinson's disease, and depression.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Chemical Synapse (The Tripartite Synapse)

The modern understanding (post-2000) describes a synapse as a **tripartite structure** comprising three functional components:

1. **Presynaptic Terminal (Axon Terminal / Bouton):**
   - Contains **synaptic vesicles** (typically 40–50 nm diameter) filled with neurotransmitter molecules.
   - Houses **voltage-gated Calcium (Ca²⁺) channels** clustered in specialized regions called **"active zones."**
   - Possesses mitochondria to supply ATP for vesicle recycling and ion pump operation.

2. **Synaptic Cleft:**
   - The extracellular fluid-filled space of width **20–40 nm**.
   - Contains enzymes (e.g., **acetylcholinesterase**) for terminating neurotransmitter action.
   - Houses cell-adhesion molecules (neuroligin, neurexin) that maintain structural integrity.

3. **Postsynaptic Membrane:**
   - Contains **receptor proteins** (ionotropic or metabotropic).
   - The region beneath the membrane, called the **Postsynaptic Density (PSD)**, is a protein-rich scaffold containing receptors, scaffolding proteins (PSD-95), and signaling enzymes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tripartite vs. Quadpartite Synapse:** Modern neuroscience (post-2010) recognizes a **quadripartite synapse** that also includes **astroglial cells (astrocytes)** which actively modulate synaptic transmission by reuptake of neurotransmitters (especially glutamate via EAAT transporters) and release of **gliotransmitters** (e.g., ATP, D-serine, glutamate).

## 2.2 Mechanism of Chemical Synaptic Transmission — Step-by-Step Logic

The transmission of a signal across a chemical synapse follows a strict **eight-step** biophysical cascade:

| Step | Event | Biophysical/Biochemical Detail |
|------|-------|-------------------------------|
| **1** | **Action Potential Arrival** | The depolarization wave (≈ +30 mV) reaches the presynaptic terminal. |
| **2** | **Voltage-Gated Ca²⁺ Channel Opening** | The depolarization opens **P/Q-type and N-type Ca²⁺ channels**. |
| **3** | **Ca²⁺ Influx** | Extracellular Ca²⁺ concentration is ≈ **2 mM** (high), while intracellular is ≈ **100 nM** (10⁴-fold gradient). Ca²⁺ rushes in down its electrochemical gradient. |
| **4** | **Vesicle Docking & Fusion** | Ca²⁺ binds to **synaptotagmin** (the Ca²⁺ sensor), triggering **SNARE-protein-mediated vesicle fusion** with the presynaptic membrane. |
| **5** | **Neurotransmitter Release** | Vesicles release their contents into the cleft via **exocytosis**. |
| **6** | **Diffusion Across Cleft** | Neurotransmitter molecules diffuse across the 20–40 nm cleft in <0.1 ms. |
| **7** | **Receptor Binding** | Neurotransmitter binds to postsynaptic receptors, opening ion channels. |
| **8** | **Postsynaptic Potential (PSP) Generation** | Ion flow creates either an **EPSP** (depolarization) or **IPSP** (hyperpolarization). |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute values in equations below use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the vertical pipe character `\|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | KTU Application |
|---|------------------|------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Nernst Equation (Equilibrium Potential)** | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$ | $E_{ion}$ in volts; $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); $T$ in Kelvin; $z$ = ion valence; $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol | Calculates reversal potential for ions (e.g., $E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV, $E_K \approx -90$ mV) |
| 2 | **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation** | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln \dfrac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}$ | $V_m$ in volts; $P$ = permeability coefficient | Computes resting membrane potential considering multiple ion permeabilities |
| 3 | **Synaptic Delay** | $t_{delay} = t_{Ca} + t_{vesicle} + t_{diffusion} + t_{receptor}$ | Total delay ≈ **0.5 to 2.0 ms** | Distinguishes chemical from electrical synapses |
| 4 | **EPSP Amplitude (Simplified Cable Equation)** | $V(x) = V_0 \, e^{-x/\lambda}$ | $\lambda$ = length constant (typically 0.1–1.0 mm in neurons) | Predicts voltage decay along dendritic arbor |
| 5 | **Time Constant of Membrane** | $\tau_m = R_m \cdot C_m$ | $R_m$ = membrane resistance (Ω·cm²); $C_m$ ≈ 1 µF/cm² | Determines temporal summation |
| 6 | **Quantal Release Equation** | $m = n \cdot p$ | $m$ = mean quantal content; $n$ = number of release sites; $p$ = release probability (0 to 1) | Statistical description of neurotransmitter release (del Castillo-Katz) |
| 7 | **Conductance Change During PSP** | $I_{syn} = g_{syn}(V_m - E_{syn})$ | $g_{syn}$ = synaptic conductance; $E_{syn}$ = reversal potential | Ohm's law for synaptic current |
| 8 | **Spatial Summation** | $V_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i(x_i)$ | Sum of individual EPSPs arriving at the axon hillock | Determines if threshold is reached |
| 9 | **Temporal Summation** | $V(t) = V_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau_m}$ | Time-decay of postsynaptic potential | Two EPSPs arriving in <$\tau_m$ will sum |
| 10 | **Calcium Current Driving Force** | $I_{Ca} = g_{Ca}(V_m - E_{Ca})$ | $E_{Ca} \approx +120$ mV (using Nernst with $[Ca]_{out} = 2$ mM) | Determines rate of vesicle fusion |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Medical Utility

- **Neuroprosthetics & Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI):** Modern BCIs (e.g., Neuralink, BrainGate) record synaptic activity using **microelectrode arrays** that sense local field potentials generated by synaptic currents.
- **Pharmacology:** Over **25%** of all prescription drugs target synaptic receptors (e.g., SSRIs, benzodiazepines, anesthetics).
- **Diagnostic Imaging:** **fMRI (functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging)** detects the **BOLD (Blood-Oxygen-Level-Dependent) signal**, which is a direct consequence of synaptic activity-induced increases in cerebral blood flow (neurovascular coupling).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Deriving the Synaptic Current Using Ohm's Law for Ion Channels

### Objective
To derive an expression for the postsynaptic ionic current generated by a single ion channel population following neurotransmitter binding.

### Starting Point
We treat an open ion channel as a **variable resistor** governed by Ohm's law:

$$I = g \cdot \Delta V$$

where $g$ is the conductance (in Siemens, S) and $\Delta V$ is the deviation of the membrane potential from the channel's equilibrium (reversal) potential.

### Step 1: Define the Driving Force
The driving force on a given ion is the difference between the instantaneous membrane potential $V_m$ and the Nernst equilibrium potential $E_{ion}$ for that ion:

$$\text{Driving Force} = V_m - E_{ion}$$

For example, if $V_m = -70$ mV (resting) and $E_{Na} = +60$ mV, then:

$$V_m - E_{Na} = -70 - 60 = -130 \text{ mV}$$

This large negative value means Na⁺ will flow **into** the cell (since positive ions flow from high to low potential inside the cell's frame of reference).

### Step 2: Define Synaptic Conductance
The synaptic conductance $g_{syn}(t)$ is **time-varying**. After neurotransmitter release, it follows an approximately **alpha function**:

$$g_{syn}(t) = g_{max} \cdot \frac{t}{\tau} \cdot e^{1 - t/\tau}$$

where $g_{max}$ is the peak conductance and $\tau$ is the time-to-peak (typically **1–5 ms** for fast ionotropic receptors like AMPA).

### Step 3: Combine Using Ohm's Law
The postsynaptic synaptic current is therefore:

$$I_{syn}(t) = g_{syn}(t) \cdot (V_m(t) - E_{syn})$$

Substituting the alpha function:

$$I_{syn}(t) = g_{max} \cdot \frac{t}{\tau} \cdot e^{1 - t/\tau} \cdot (V_m(t) - E_{syn})$$

### Step 4: Convert to Membrane Voltage Change
By the **cable equation** for a uniform cylindrical dendrite, the local voltage response is related to the current by:

$$C_m \frac{\partial V}{\partial t} = -I_{syn}(t) - I_{leak} + \frac{V}{R_a}$$

For a small perturbation $V_{PSP}$ from rest, the leak term linearizes, and we obtain the **passive membrane response**:

$$V_{PSP}(t) = \frac{I_{syn}(t)}{g_{leak}} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)$$

with $\tau_m = R_m C_m$.

### Step 5: Magnitude Estimation
Typical numerical values for a single excitatory synapse on a hippocampal pyramidal neuron:
- $g_{max} \approx 500$ pS
- $E_{syn} = E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV
- $V_m = -70$ mV at rest
- $g_{leak} \approx 5$ nS

Thus, the peak EPSP amplitude is approximately:

$$V_{EPSP} = \frac{0.5 \text{ nS} \times (-130 \text{ mV})}{5 \text{ nS}} = -13 \text{ mV of depolarization}$$

This is well below the ≈ **+15 to +20 mV** needed to reach threshold, confirming that **multiple EPSPs must summate** to trigger an action potential.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Deriving the Synaptic Delay

The total synaptic delay $t_{delay}$ is the sum of all sequential kinetic events:

$$t_{delay} = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4 + t_5$$

| Symbol | Event | Typical Magnitude |
|--------|-------|-------------------|
| $t_1$ | Ca²⁺ channel opening latency | ≈ 100 µs |
| $t_2$ | Ca²⁺ diffusion to vesicle | ≈ 50 µs |
| $t_3$ | Vesicle fusion (SNARE zippering) | ≈ 200 µs |
| $t_4$ | Neurotransmitter diffusion across cleft | ≈ 50 µs |
| $t_5$ | Receptor binding + channel gating | ≈ 200 µs |
| **Total** | | **≈ 600 µs = 0.6 ms** |

### Numerical Calculation:

$$t_{delay} = 100 + 50 + 200 + 50 + 200 = 600 \text{ µs}$$

The experimentally measured value of **0.5–2.0 ms** matches this estimate within one order of magnitude, validating the model. This delay is the principal biophysical reason why chemical synapses are slower than electrical synapses (≈ 0.1 ms).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Excitatory and Inhibitory Synapses

| Parameter | Excitatory Synapse (EPSP) | Inhibitory Synapse (IPSP) |
|-----------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Typical Neurotransmitter** | Glutamate, Acetylcholine (at NMJ) | GABA, Glycine |
| **Receptor Class** | Ionotropic: AMPA, NMDA, Kainate; Metabotropic: mGluR1-8 | Ionotropic: GABA$_A$, Glycine; Metabotropic: GABA$_B$ |
| **Ion Permeated** | Na⁺, K⁺ (and Ca²⁺ for NMDA) | Cl⁻ (inflow) or K⁺ (outflow) |
| **Reversal Potential $E_{syn}$** | ≈ **0 mV** (mixed Na⁺/K⁺) | ≈ **-70 mV** (Cl⁻ equilibrium) |
| **Effect on $V_m$** | **Depolarization** (toward 0 mV) | **Hyperpolarization** (more negative) or **shunting inhibition** |
| **Amplitude** | 0.1 – 5 mV | 0.1 – 5 mV |
| **Driving Force at Rest** | $\vert V_m - E_{syn} \vert = \vert -70 - 0 \vert = 70$ mV | $\vert V_m - E_{syn} \vert = \vert -70 - (-70) \vert = 0$ mV initially, but shunting reduces $R_m$ |
| **Reversal Potential Calculation** | Nernst for Na⁺/K⁺ mixture (GHK equation required) | Nernst for Cl⁻: $E_{Cl} = -\frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{[Cl^-]_{out}}{[Cl^-]_{in}}$ |
| **Threshold to Action Potential** | Brings $V_m$ closer to ≈ -55 mV | Moves $V_m$ away from threshold |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Calculate the equilibrium (Nernst) potential for the Cl⁻ ion at body temperature (37 °C = 310 K), given that $[Cl^-]_{out} = 120$ mM and $[Cl^-]_{in} = 4$ mM.

**Solution:**

The Nernst equation is:

$$E_{Cl} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[Cl^-]_{out}}{[Cl^-]_{in}}$$

Since Cl⁻ has valence $z = -1$, substituting all values:

$$E_{Cl} = \frac{(8.314)(310)}{(-1)(96485)} \ln \left(\frac{120}{4}\right)$$

Step 1: Compute the prefactor:

$$\frac{(8.314)(310)}{(-1)(96485)} = \frac{2577.34}{-96485} = -0.02671 \text{ V}$$

Step 2: Compute the log ratio:

$$\ln(30) = 3.401$$

Step 3: Multiply:

$$E_{Cl} = -0.02671 \times 3.401 = -0.0908 \text{ V} = -90.8 \text{ mV}$$

**Interpretation:** The Cl⁻ equilibrium potential is approximately **-90 mV**, which is more negative than the typical resting potential of -70 mV. Therefore, when GABA$_A$ receptors open Cl⁻ channels, Cl⁻ flows into the cell, hyperpolarizing it toward -90 mV — this constitutes **inhibition**.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Chemical Synapse

The diagram below illustrates the eight-step biophysical cascade of synaptic transmission with all component interactions mapped. Node identifiers are alphanumeric and all labels use clean uppercase text to comply with Mermaid compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[STEP 01: ACTION POTENTIAL ARRIVES AT PRESYNAPTIC TERMINAL]:::step
    B[STEP 02: MEMBRANE DEPOLARIZATION OPENS VOLTAGE GATED CA2+ CHANNELS]:::step
    C[STEP 03: CA2+ ION INFLUX DOWN ELECTROCHEMICAL GRADIENT]:::step
    D[STEP 04: CA2+ BINDS SYNAPTOTAGMIN ON SYNAPTIC VESICLES]:::step
    E[STEP 05: SNARE PROTEIN MEDIATED VESICLE FUSION WITH PRESYNAPTIC MEMBRANE]:::step
    F[STEP 06: NEUROTRANSMITTER EXOCYTOSIS INTO SYNAPTIC CLEFT 20 TO 40 NM]:::step
    G[STEP 07: DIFFUSION AND BINDING TO POSTSYNAPTIC RECEPTORS]:::step
    H[STEP 08: GENERATION OF EPSP OR IPSP IN POSTSYNAPTIC NEURON]:::step
    I[TERMINATION: REUPTAKE BY TRANSPORTERS AND ENZYMATIC DEGRADATION]:::term
    J[ASTROCYTE MODULATION: GLUTAMATE UPTAKE VIA EAAT AND GLIOTRANSMITTER RELEASE]:::glial

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    F -.-> J
    G -.-> J

    classDef step fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef term fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef glial fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

```mermaid
graph LR

    end

    subgraph CHEMICAL_SYNAPSE_BLOCK
        C1[PRESYNAPTIC TERMINAL]:::chem --> C2[CA2+ ENTRY TRIGGERS VESICLE RELEASE]:::chem
        C2 --> C3[NEUROTRANSMITTER DIFFUSION ACROSS CLEFT]:::chem
        C3 --> C4[POSTSYNAPTIC RECEPTOR BINDING]:::chem
        C4 --> C5[POSTSYNAPTIC POTENTIAL GENERATION]:::chem
    end
```

```mermaid
graph TD
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]
**Define a synapse. Distinguish between electrical and chemical synapses in terms of transmission speed, directionality, and synaptic cleft width.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A synapse is a specialized junction that allows the transmission of signals between two neurons or between a neuron and an effector cell. Sherrington coined the term in 1897.

| Feature | Electrical Synapse | Chemical Synapse |
|---------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Transmission Speed** | Ultra-fast (≈ 0.1 ms) | Slower (0.5–2.0 ms) |
| **Directionality** | Mostly bidirectional | Strictly unidirectional |
| **Cleft Width** | ≈ 2–4 nm (gap junction pore) | 20–40 nm (true extracellular gap) |
| **Mechanism** | Direct ionic current flow | Neurotransmitter-mediated |

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for comparison table)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]
**What is meant by synaptic delay? Mention its typical value and the principal biophysical reason for its existence in chemical synapses.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Synaptic delay is the finite time interval between the arrival of an action potential at the presynaptic terminal and the generation of a postsynaptic potential. It arises due to the sequential cascade of Ca²⁺ channel opening, Ca²⁺ diffusion, vesicle fusion (SNARE zippering), neurotransmitter diffusion across the cleft, and receptor binding. The total delay is approximately **0.5 to 2.0 ms** in chemical synapses, whereas electrical synapses have a delay of only ≈ **0.1 ms**. This delay is principally due to the **chemical exocytosis step** (vesicle fusion ≈ 200 µs) and the **neurotransmitter diffusion time** across the 20–40 nm cleft.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for value + 1 for biophysical reason)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Nernst/GHK distinction (Loss: 2 marks):** Many students incorrectly apply the Nernst equation for a mixed-ion channel (e.g., at a glutamate synapse). The correct framework is the **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) voltage equation**, which accounts for multiple ion permeabilities.
> 2. **Confusing EPSP and Action Potential (Loss: 1 mark):** An EPSP is a **graded, decremental, sub-threshold** potential, NOT an action potential. It is **analog** in nature, while the action potential is **all-or-nothing** and **digital**.
> 3. **Omitting the unit conversions in numerical problems (Loss: 1 mark):** Always convert temperature to Kelvin and concentrations to consistent molar units before applying the Nernst equation.
> 4. **Missing the quantal release framework (Loss: 1 mark):** In questions about neurotransmitter release, mention the **del Castillo-Katz quantal hypothesis** ($m = n \cdot p$) for full credit.
> 5. **Forgetting the tripartite/quadripartite synapse model (Loss: 1 mark):** Modern KTU questions often expect awareness that synapses are not just two-cell structures; **astrocytes** actively modulate transmission.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** With a neat block diagram, describe the structure of a chemical synapse. Explain the step-by-step mechanism of chemical synaptic transmission emphasizing the role of Ca²⁺ ions.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Derive the Nernst equation from first principles. Using the derived equation, calculate the equilibrium potential for K⁺ at 37 °C given $[K^+]_{out} = 4$ mM and $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM.

---

**Model Solution to Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Structure of a Chemical Synapse (3 marks):**
A chemical synapse consists of three principal parts:
1. **Presynaptic Terminal (Axon Bouton):** Contains mitochondria, synaptic vesicles (40–50 nm) filled with neurotransmitter, and active zones with voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels.
2. **Synaptic Cleft:** 20–40 nm wide, filled with extracellular fluid, contains degradative enzymes (e.g., acetylcholinesterase) and cell-adhesion molecules.
3. **Postsynaptic Membrane:** Bears receptor proteins (ionotropic or metabotropic) and the postsynaptic density (PSD) protein scaffold.

A diagram showing these components should be drawn (refer to Section 4.1 above).

**Mechanism of Transmission (4 marks):**
- **Step 1:** Action potential reaches the presynaptic terminal, causing depolarization to ≈ +30 mV.
- **Step 2:** Depolarization opens **voltage-gated P/Q-type and N-type Ca²⁺ channels**.
- **Step 3:** Ca²⁺ flows into the terminal down a 10⁴-fold electrochemical gradient ($[Ca]_{out} = 2$ mM, $[Ca]_{in} = 100$ nM).
- **Step 4:** Ca²⁺ binds to **synaptotagmin** (the Ca²⁺ sensor on synaptic vesicles), triggering **SNARE-mediated exocytosis**.
- **Step 5:** Neurotransmitter (e.g., glutamate) is released into the cleft and diffuses to the postsynaptic membrane.
- **Step 6:** Neurotransmitter binds receptors, opening ion channels and producing an **EPSP** (depolarization) or **IPSP** (hyperpolarization).
- **Step 7:** Neurotransmitter is terminated by reuptake, enzymatic degradation, or diffusion.

**Role of Ca²⁺ (highlighted in bold within the explanation above):** Ca²⁺ acts as the **essential trigger** for neurotransmitter release. Removal of extracellular Ca²⁺ abolishes synaptic transmission completely, demonstrating its obligate role.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Derivation of the Nernst Equation (4 marks):**

The Nernst equation is derived from the **electrochemical equilibrium condition** — the state where the chemical potential gradient is exactly balanced by the electrical potential gradient, with **zero net flux** of the ion.

Consider an ion X with charge $z$ at temperature $T$. The chemical potential (Gibbs free energy per mole) is:

$$\mu = \mu^0 + RT \ln[X]$$

At equilibrium, the difference in electrical potential energy ($zFV$) plus the difference in chemical potential between outside and inside must equal zero:

$$zF(V_{in} - V_{out}) + RT \ln \frac{[X]_{in}}{[X]_{out}} = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$V_{in} - V_{out} = -\frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[X]_{in}}{[X]_{out}}$$

Defining $E_{ion} = V_{in} - V_{out}$ (the Nernst equilibrium potential):

$$\boxed{E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}}$$

**Numerical Calculation for K⁺ (3 marks):**

Given: $T = 37\,°C = 310$ K, $[K^+]_{out} = 4$ mM, $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM, $z = +1$.

Step 1: Compute the prefactor:

$$\frac{RT}{zF} = \frac{(8.314)(310)}{(1)(96485)} = \frac{2577.34}{96485} = 0.02671 \text{ V}$$

Step 2: Compute the log ratio:

$$\ln \frac{[K^+]_{out}}{[K^+]_{in}} = \ln \left(\frac{4}{140}\right) = \ln(0.02857) = -3.555$$

Step 3: Multiply:

$$E_{K} = (0.02671)(-3.555) = -0.0949 \text{ V} \approx -95 \text{ mV}$$

**Answer:** The K⁺ equilibrium potential is approximately **-95 mV**, in excellent agreement with the experimentally measured value.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the concepts of Excitatory Postsynaptic Potential (EPSP) and Inhibitory Postsynaptic Potential (IPSP). Discuss the mechanisms of spatial and temporal summation at a typical neuron.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Compare the structural and functional features of electrical and chemical synapses in a comprehensive table. State two specific locations in the human body where electrical synapses are physiologically important.

---

**Model Solution to Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**EPSP and IPSP Definitions (3 marks):**

- **EPSP (Excitatory Postsynaptic Potential):** A transient, graded depolarization of the postsynaptic membrane that **increases** the probability of an action potential. It is typically mediated by **glutamate** acting on **AMPA** (Na⁺/K⁺ permeable) or **NMDA** (Na⁺/Ca²⁺/K⁺ permeable) receptors. The reversal potential is approximately **0 mV**.

- **IPSP (Inhibitory Postsynaptic Potential):** A transient, graded hyperpolarization (or shunting) of the postsynaptic membrane that **decreases** the probability of an action potential. It is typically mediated by **GABA** (GABA$_A$ receptor, Cl⁻ permeable) or **glycine**. The reversal potential is approximately **-70 mV** (E$_{Cl}$).

**Spatial Summation (2 marks):**
When **multiple presynaptic neurons** fire simultaneously and release neurotransmitter at **different dendritic locations**, their EPSPs propagate electrotonically toward the axon hillock and **algebraically add** at the trigger zone. If the summed depolarization exceeds threshold (≈ -55 mV), an action potential is generated.

**Temporal Summation (2 marks):**
When a **single presynaptic neuron** fires **multiple action potentials in rapid succession**, the resulting EPSPs overlap in time before the membrane potential returns to rest. If the inter-spike interval is shorter than the membrane time constant $\tau_m$ (typically 10–50 ms), the EPSPs summate. The condition for summation is:

$$\Delta t_{spikes} < \tau_m$$

where $\tau_m = R_m C_m$.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Feature | Electrical Synapse | Chemical Synapse |
|---------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Cleft Width** | 2–4 nm (gap junction) | 20–40 nm |
| **Structural Basis** | Connexon protein channels (connexin-36) | Pre- and postsynaptic membranes with vesicles and receptors |
| **Mechanism** | Direct ionic current flow | Neurotransmitter release and receptor binding |
| **Speed** | ≈ 0.1 ms | 0.5–2.0 ms |
| **Directionality** | Mostly bidirectional | Strictly unidirectional |
| **Synaptic Delay** | Negligible | Pronounced (Ca²⁺-dependent) |
| **Fatigability** | Non-fatiguing | Fatigues with sustained activity |
| **Plasticity** | Limited | High (basis of learning/memory) |
| **Signal Type** | Analog (graded current) | Either analog (EPSP/IPSP) or digital (action potential) |
| **Prevalence in CNS** | Rare (<1%) | Predominant (>99%) |
| **Example** | Cardiac intercalated discs | Neuromuscular junction, cortical pyramidal synapses |

**Two Physiological Locations of Electrical Synapses (2 marks):**
1. **Cardiac Muscle Intercalated Discs:** Gap junctions between cardiac myocytes allow rapid, synchronized electrical coupling, enabling the heart to contract as a functional syncytium. Located in the intercalated discs, they contain **connexin-43**.
2. **Hippocampus and Retina (CNS):** Electrical synapses between inhibitory interneurons (e.g., hippocampal basket cells) and in the retinal AII amacrine cells synchronize network oscillations. Connexin-36 is the principal gap junction protein.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 PCBMT303 — Synapses**

- [ ] **Sherrington coined the term "synapse" in 1897.** It is the functional contact point between two excitable cells.
- [ ] The human brain contains ≈ **10¹⁴ (100 trillion) synapses** — the principal substrate of cognition.
- [ ] **Chemical synapses predominate** (>99% in human CNS); **electrical synapses** are rare and use **connexin gap junctions**.
- [ ] **Synaptic cleft width:** 20–40 nm (chemical) vs. 2–4 nm (electrical).
- [ ] **Synaptic delay** ≈ **0.5–2.0 ms** in chemical synapses; the principal bottleneck is the **Ca²⁺-dependent SNARE-mediated exocytosis step**.
- [ ] **Ca²⁺ entry** is the **obligate trigger** for neurotransmitter release; the extracellular/intracellular Ca²⁺ concentration ratio is ≈ **20,000 : 1**.
- [ ] **SNARE proteins** (synaptobrevin, syntaxin, SNAP-25) mediate vesicle fusion; **synaptotagmin** is the Ca²⁺ sensor.
- [ ] **EPSP** is a **graded, sub-threshold, decremental depolarization**; **IPSP** is a graded hyperpolarization (or shunting inhibition).
- [ ] **Spatial summation** = simultaneous inputs at different locations; **temporal summation** = rapid sequential inputs at the same location.
- [ ] **Membrane time constant** $\tau_m = R_m C_m$ governs temporal summation; typical value 10–50 ms in neurons.
- [ ] **Nernst Equation:** $E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$; use this for single-ion equilibrium potentials.
- [ ] **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz Equation:** Use this for **multi-ion** membranes (e.g., postsynaptic membranes with mixed Na⁺/K⁺/Cl⁻ permeability).
- [ ] **Quantal release hypothesis** (del Castillo-Katz, 1954): $m = n \cdot p$ where $m$ = mean quantal content, $n$ = number of release sites, $p$ = release probability.
- [ ] **Modern synapse model is tripartite or quadripartite** — astrocytes modulate transmission via **EAAT transporters** and **gliotransmitter** release.
- [ ] **Diseases of synaptic dysfunction:** Myasthenia gravis (NMJ ACh receptor autoantibodies), Lambert-Eaton syndrome, epilepsy, Parkinson's disease, depression.
- [ ] **Key pharmacological agents targeting synapses:** SSRIs (serotonin reuptake), benzodiazepines (GABA$_A$ allosteric modulation), botulinum toxin (cleaves SNAP-25 → blocks ACh release).
- [ ] **Remember the directionality rule:** Chemical synapses are **strictly unidirectional** (pre → post); electrical synapses are **mostly bidirectional**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical & Chemical Synapses, Excitatory & Inhibitory Synapses, Synaptic Potentials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT303 - MEDICAL PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electrical & Chemical Synapses, Excitatory & Inhibitory Synapses, Synaptic Potentials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Synapse — The Fundamental Junction of the Nervous System

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A *synapse* is a highly specialized, anatomically and functionally defined junction that permits the **unidirectional or bidirectional** transfer of an electrical or chemical signal from one neuron (the *presynaptic neuron*) to another neuron, muscle cell, or glandular cell (the *postsynaptic cell*). The term, coined by Sir Charles Sherrington in 1897, is derived from the Greek *synaptein* meaning "to clasp or bind together." Synapses are the fundamental computational units of the nervous system and are the primary sites of neural integration, plasticity, and information processing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The human central nervous system contains approximately **10¹⁵ synapses** (≈ 1 quadrillion). Each neuron typically receives input from **1,000 to 10,000** presynaptic terminals, making synaptic transmission the most vital biophysical process studied in medical physics for understanding neurological function, disease, and pharmacology.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Telecommunications Relay"

Think of a synapse as a **telecommunications relay tower** between two cities (neurons):

- An **electrical synapse** is like a *direct fiber-optic cable* splice — the signal passes **instantaneously** and **bidirectionally** with no relay station in between.
- A **chemical synapse** is like a *radio broadcast station* — the message is encoded, transmitted through the air (the *synaptic cleft*), decoded by a receiver antenna (the *postsynaptic receptor*), and is strictly **one-way (unidirectional)**. There is a small but measurable **broadcast delay**.

### 1.3 The Two Principal Classes of Synapses

| Property | Electrical Synapse | Chemical Synapse |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Junction Type | Gap Junction (Connexon) | Synaptic Bouton + Cleft |
| Signal Carrier | **Ionic current** (direct) | **Neurotransmitter** molecules |
| Direction | Usually **bidirectional** | Strictly **unidirectional** |
| Synaptic Delay | **< 0.1 ms** (virtually none) | **~ 0.3 – 0.5 ms** |
| Speed | **Ultra-fast** | **Slower** but modifiable |
| Plasticity | **Low** (fixed gain) | **High** (learning, memory) |
| Location of Major Function | CNS synchronization | **> 99% of all synapses** |
| Standard Examples | Cardiac muscle, retinal amacrine cells, hippocampal interneurons | Neuromuscular junction, cortical pyramidal neurons |

### 1.4 The Two Functional Subclasses of Chemical Synapses

> [!NOTE]
> **Excitatory vs. Inhibitory Synapses** are defined not by the *anatomical structure* but by the **physiological effect on the postsynaptic membrane potential**.

- **Excitatory Synapse** → produces an **Excitatory Postsynaptic Potential (EPSP)** → membrane **depolarization** → raises probability of action potential.
- **Inhibitory Synapse** → produces an **Inhibitory Postsynaptic Potential (IPSP)** → membrane **hyperpolarization** or **shunting** → lowers probability of action potential.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** The same neurotransmitter (e.g., acetylcholine) can be **excitatory at one synapse** (neuromuscular junction — skeletal muscle) and **inhibitory at another** (cardiac muscle — vagal innervation). Therefore, classification is determined by the **postsynaptic receptor**, not the neurotransmitter identity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Electrical Synapse — Operational Mechanism (Stepwise)

Electrical synapses operate through **gap junctions**, which are clusters of transmembrane channels called **connexons**. Each connexon is composed of **6 connexin protein subunits** forming a hexameric cylinder.

1. **Connexon Assembly:** Two hemichannels (one from each cell membrane) dock end-to-end across an intercellular gap of only **2 – 4 nm** (vs. **20 – 40 nm** for chemical synapses).
2. **Channel Aperture:** The resulting aqueous pore has a diameter of **~ 1 – 2 nm**, large enough for ions, second messengers (IP₃, cAMP), and small molecules up to **~ 1 kDa**.
3. **Current Flow:** Ionic current from the depolarized presynaptic cell flows directly through the pore into the postsynaptic cell, governed by **Ohm's Law**: $I = g_{gj} \cdot (V_{pre} - V_{post})$, where $g_{gj}$ is the gap-junction conductance.
4. **Bidirectionality:** Since the channel has no rectification gate, current can flow in **either direction** depending on the voltage gradient.
5. **Voltage Sensitivity:** Some connexons (e.g., Cx36 in neurons) exhibit *voltage-dependent gating*, allowing modulation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the CNS uses both types:** Electrical synapses provide **synchronization** (e.g., the pyloric network of the lobster, cortical interneuron networks for gamma oscillations 30–80 Hz), while chemical synapses provide the **modulation, plasticity, and integration** required for learning and memory.

### 2.2 Chemical Synapse — Operational Mechanism (Stepwise)

The chemical synapse is the dominant synapse type in the human nervous system. Its operation follows a precise sequence:

1. **Synthesis:** Neurotransmitters (e.g., glutamate, GABA, ACh) are synthesized in the presynaptic cytoplasm or terminal.
2. **Storage:** Packaged into **synaptic vesicles** (~ 40 nm diameter) each containing **~ 5,000 – 10,000** transmitter molecules.
3. **Depolarization Arrival:** An action potential invades the presynaptic terminal.
4. **Ca²⁺ Influx:** Depolarization opens **voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels (P/Q- and N-type)**, raising local [Ca²⁺]ᵢ from **~ 100 nM (resting)** to **~ 10 – 100 μM**.
5. **Vesicle Docking & Fusion:** The Ca²⁺ binds **synaptotagmin** on vesicles, triggering SNARE-protein-mediated exocytosis into the cleft within **~ 0.2 ms**.
6. **Diffusion Across Cleft:** Transmitter traverses the **20 – 40 nm** cleft by free diffusion (Brownian motion, mean transit time **~ 0.1 – 0.5 μs**).
7. **Receptor Binding:** Binds postsynaptic **ionotropic** (fast, ligand-gated) or **metabotropic** (slow, G-protein coupled) receptors.
8. **Ion Channel Opening:** Postsynaptic conductance changes → EPSP or IPSP generated.
9. **Termination:** Transmitter is cleared by **reuptake (transporters)**, **enzymatic degradation (AChE, MAO)**, or **diffusion**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight — The "Synaptic Delay":** The total delay of **~ 0.3 – 0.5 ms** is dominated by **Ca²⁺-dependent vesicle fusion**, not diffusion. This delay is the *rate-limiting step* of chemical transmission and explains why reflexes have a measurable reaction time even when axon conduction is fast.

### 2.3 Excitatory Synapse & EPSP — Deep Mechanism

At an excitatory synapse, the postsynaptic receptor (e.g., AMPA, NMDA for glutamate; nicotinic for ACh) opens a non-selective **cation channel** permeable primarily to **Na⁺** (and to a lesser extent K⁺, Ca²⁺).

- The **reversal potential** $E_{rev}$ for a non-selective cation channel is approximately **~ 0 mV**.
- Since resting $V_m \approx -70$ mV, current flows *inward* (Na⁺ enters), causing **depolarization** to a value closer to 0 mV.
- The result is an **EPSP** — a transient, graded depolarization of amplitude **+0.1 to +5 mV**, lasting **~ 10 – 50 ms**.

**NMDA Receptors — A Special Case:**
NMDA receptors are unique in that they require **simultaneous**:
- **Glutamate binding** (ligand gating), AND
- **Postsynaptic depolarization** (to relieve Mg²⁺ block).

This makes NMDA receptors **coincidence detectors** — the molecular basis of Hebbian learning ("neurons that fire together, wire together").

### 2.4 Inhibitory Synapse & IPSP — Deep Mechanism

At an inhibitory synapse, the postsynaptic receptor (e.g., GABA$_A$ or GABA$_B$ for GABA; glycine receptor) opens channels selective for **Cl⁻** (GABA$_A$, glycine) or **K⁺** (GABA$_B$, some muscarinic).

- The **Cl⁻ reversal potential** $E_{Cl} \approx -70$ mV (at physiological gradients).
- The **K⁺ reversal potential** $E_K \approx -90$ mV.
- Opening Cl⁻ channels at a resting $V_m = -70$ mV: Cl⁻ flows *inward* (since $[Cl^-]_i$ is low), but this stabilizes $V_m$ near $E_{Cl}$ and **clamps the membrane**, opposing depolarization.
- Opening K⁺ channels: K⁺ flows *outward*, driving $V_m$ toward $E_K = -90$ mV (**hyperpolarization**).
- The result is an **IPSP** — a transient hyperpolarization of amplitude **-1 to -5 mV**, lasting **~ 10 – 50 ms**.

**Shunting Inhibition:**
A special form of inhibition where the opening of Cl⁻ channels *decreases the membrane resistance* ($R_m \downarrow$) so dramatically that excitatory currents are "shunted away" — this is **silent** on a voltage recording but powerfully suppresses excitability.

### 2.5 The Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation

The resting membrane potential is governed by the relative permeabilities of the membrane to Na⁺, K⁺, and Cl⁻:

$$
V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)
$$

At body temperature **T = 310 K** (37 °C), $\frac{RT}{F} \ln 10 \approx 61.5$ mV, so:

$$
V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)
$$

### 2.6 Nernst Equation — Reversal Potential for a Single Ion

For any ion X with charge $z$:

$$
E_X = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\!\left(\frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}\right) = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}\right) \text{ mV at 37 °C}
$$

| Ion | Typical Plasma [Out] | Typical [In] | $E_X$ (mV) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| K⁺ | 4.5 mM | 150 mM | **−94 mV** |
| Na⁺ | 145 mM | 15 mM | **+60 mV** |
| Cl⁻ | 110 mM | 4 mM | **−90 mV** (sign-flipped for anions) |
| Ca²⁺ | 1.2 mM | 100 nM | **+125 mV** |

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Standard Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $E_X = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}\right)$ | Nernst (reversal) potential for ion X | mV |
| 2 | $V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\sum P_{cat}[Cat]_o + \sum P_{an}[An]_i}{\sum P_{cat}[Cat]_i + \sum P_{an}[An]_o}\right)$ | GHK resting potential | mV |
| 3 | $I_{ion} = g_{ion} \cdot (V_m - E_{ion})$ | Ionic current (Ohmic approximation) | nA |
| 4 | $I_{syn} = g_{syn}(t) \cdot (V_m(t) - E_{rev})$ | Synaptic current (driving-force model) | nA |
| 5 | $\Delta V = \frac{I_{syn} \cdot R_m}{1 + R_m \cdot g_{syn}}$ | Synaptic potential (EPSP/IPSP amplitude) | mV |
| 6 | $\tau_m = R_m \cdot C_m$ | Membrane time constant (decay of PSP) | ms (≈ 10 ms) |
| 7 | $\lambda = \sqrt{\frac{r_m}{r_i}}$ | Space constant (spatial decay) | mm (≈ 1 mm) |
| 8 | $V_{EPSP,total} = \sum_{i} w_i \cdot V_{EPSP,i}$ | Temporal + Spatial summation | mV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The **driving-force equation** $I = g \cdot (V_m - E_{rev})$ is identical in form to the equation governing a **field-effect transistor (FET)** in analog electronics, where $g$ is the transconductance and $V_m - E_{rev}$ is the overdrive voltage. This is why **neuromorphic engineering** uses transistor arrays to emulate synaptic function.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation (from First Principles of Thermodynamics)

The Nernst equation is derived by equating the **electrochemical potential** of an ion on both sides of a permeable membrane. At equilibrium, the net flux is zero, meaning the **electrical work** exactly balances the **chemical work** of concentration-gradient diffusion.

**Step 1 — Define chemical potential difference (per mole of ions):**
The chemical work required to move one mole of ions from side *i* (inside) to side *o* (outside) against a concentration gradient is:

$$
\Delta \mu_{chem} = RT \ln\!\left(\frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}\right)
$$

This expression arises from the Boltzmann distribution, where R is the universal gas constant (8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹) and T is absolute temperature (kelvin). The chemical potential is fundamentally a logarithmic function of concentration.

**Step 2 — Define electrical potential work:**
The electrical work required to move one mole of a monovalent ion ($z = +1$) across a membrane whose inside is negative by $V_m$ volts (so outside is more positive) is:

$$
\Delta W_{elec} = z \cdot F \cdot V_m
$$

Here $F$ is Faraday's constant (96,485 C·mol⁻¹), and $z$ is the ion's valence.

**Step 3 — Apply the equilibrium condition:**
At electrochemical equilibrium, the two work terms are equal and opposite:

$$
zFV_m = -RT \ln\!\left(\frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}\right)
$$

The negative sign indicates that the electrical force opposes the diffusional force at equilibrium.

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_m$:**

$$
V_m = -\frac{RT}{zF} \ln\!\left(\frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}\right) = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\!\left(\frac{[X]_i}{[X]_o}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Convert natural log to log₁₀:**
Using $\ln x = 2.3026 \log_{10} x$:

$$
V_m = \frac{2.3026 \cdot RT}{zF} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[X]_i}{[X]_o}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate at body temperature (T = 310 K):**

$$
\frac{2.3026 \cdot R \cdot T}{F} = \frac{2.3026 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485} = 0.0615 \text{ V} = 61.5 \text{ mV}
$$

**Final Nernst Equation:**

$$
\boxed{E_X = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[X]_i}{[X]_o}\right) \text{ mV at 37 °C}}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Calculation of $E_{Cl}$ and EPSP Amplitude

**Given data (typical mammalian neuron):**
- Intracellular $[Cl^-]_i = 4$ mM
- Extracellular $[Cl^-]_o = 110$ mM
- Body temperature $T = 37$ °C
- $V_{rest} = -70$ mV
- Membrane capacitance $C_m = 1$ μF/cm²
- Membrane resistance $R_m = 1000$ Ω·cm²

**Calculation 1 — Chloride Reversal Potential:**

$$
E_{Cl} = \frac{61.5}{-1} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4}{110}\right)
$$

$$
\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4}{110}\right) = \log_{10}(0.03636) = -1.4393
$$

$$
E_{Cl} = (-61.5) \times (-1.4393) = +88.52 \text{ mV}
$$

Wait — note that since $z = -1$ for Cl⁻, the formula gives a *positive* value. The convention is to re-interpret: because the gradient is outward (Cl⁻ is much higher outside), the equilibrium potential is such that the inside would need to be **positive** to prevent further Cl⁻ entry. In conventional physiology, $E_{Cl}$ is reported as **−70 mV** when the formulation uses the opposite sign convention:

$$
E_{Cl} = \frac{61.5}{1} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[Cl^-]_o}{[Cl^-]_i}\right) = 61.5 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{110}{4}\right) = 61.5 \times 1.4393 = +88.5 \text{ mV}
$$

This means $E_{Cl}$ sits at **+88.5 mV** (using the *out/in* convention) or **−70 to −90 mV** depending on the [Cl⁻] gradient in mature vs. immature neurons. For *mature* neurons where $[Cl^-]_i \approx 4$ mM, **$E_{Cl} \approx -70$ mV** is a common textbook value when $[Cl^-]_o$ is slightly higher than the plasma value due to Donnan equilibrium.

**Calculation 2 — EPSP Amplitude for a Single Synaptic Event:**

Given a synaptic conductance transient $g_{syn}(t) = \bar{g}_{syn} \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$ with peak $\bar{g}_{syn} = 5$ nS, and $E_{rev} = 0$ mV (for AMPA receptor at $V_m = -70$ mV):

**Step A — Peak synaptic current:**

$$
I_{syn,peak} = \bar{g}_{syn} \cdot (V_m - E_{rev}) = 5 \text{ nS} \times (-70 \text{ mV} - 0 \text{ mV}) = -350 \text{ pA}
$$

**Step B — EPSP amplitude (peak depolarization):**
Using the steady-state approximation $\Delta V \approx I \cdot R_m$ (valid when $g_{syn} \ll 1/R_m$):

$$
\Delta V = -350 \text{ pA} \times 1 \text{ k}\Omega = -0.35 \text{ mV} \text{ (depolarization, sign negative = upward)}
$$

So a single EPSP produces a depolarization of about **+0.35 mV**, requiring **~ 20 simultaneous EPSPs** (spatial summation) to reach the typical **+7 mV** threshold for action potential initiation.

### 3.3 Derivation of Temporal and Spatial Summation

**Temporal Summation (same synapse firing repeatedly):**
If presynaptic spikes arrive at intervals shorter than the membrane time constant $\tau_m$, the resulting EPSPs add algebraically:

$$
V_{EPSP,n}(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} A \cdot e^{-(t - t_k)/\tau_m}
$$

where $A$ is the peak amplitude of a single EPSP and $t_k$ is the time of the $k$-th spike.

**Spatial Summation (multiple synapses firing simultaneously):**
When $N$ synapses fire at once, the currents sum in the postsynaptic neuron:

$$
I_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} g_{syn,i} \cdot (V_m - E_{rev,i})
$$

Because the dendrite behaves as a **leaky cable** (governed by the cable equation), EPSPs from distal synapses are attenuated by the factor $e^{-x/\lambda}$:

$$
V_{EPSP}(x) = V_0 \cdot e^{-x/\lambda}
$$

where $\lambda = \sqrt{r_m/r_i}$ is the **space constant** (typically **0.5 – 1.0 mm** in cortical dendrites).

### 3.4 Worked Example — Chloride Equilibrium in GABAergic Inhibition

A neuron has $[Cl^-]_i = 7$ mM, $[Cl^-]_o = 130$ mM, $T = 310$ K.

**Compute $E_{Cl}$:**

$$
E_{Cl} = -61.5 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[Cl^-]_i}{[Cl^-]_o}\right) = -61.5 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{7}{130}\right)
$$

$$
\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{7}{130}\right) = \log_{10}(0.0538) = -1.2695
$$

$$
E_{Cl} = -61.5 \times (-1.2695) = +78.1 \text{ mV}
$$

Since $V_{rest} = -70$ mV $< E_{Cl} = +78$ mV, opening Cl⁻ channels causes inward Cl⁻ flux, driving $V_m$ toward $E_{Cl}$. The neuron **hyperpolarizes toward −70 mV** (a small, stabilization effect), confirming **inhibitory** action.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table for KTU Answers

| Parameter | EPSP | IPSP |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Ionic basis | Na⁺ (and K⁺, Ca²⁺) influx | Cl⁻ influx or K⁺ efflux |
| $E_{rev}$ | **~ 0 mV** | **−70 to −90 mV** |
| Effect on $V_m$ | Depolarization | Hyperpolarization / Shunting |
| Conductance | $\uparrow$ for Na⁺ | $\uparrow$ for Cl⁻ or K⁺ |
| Threshold shift | Closer to AP threshold | Farther from AP threshold |
| Typical neurotransmitter | **Glutamate**, ACh | **GABA**, Glycine |
| Ionotropic receptor | **AMPA, NMDA, nAChR** | **GABA$_A$, Glycine-R** |
| Metabotropic counterpart | mGluR1-8 | GABA$_B$, mAChR (cardiac) |
| Reversal calculation | Nernst for non-selective cation | Nernst for Cl⁻ or K⁺ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Chemical Synapse Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Presynaptic Neuron Terminal] --> B[Action Potential Arrival]
    B --> C[Voltage-Gated Ca2+ Channels Open]
    C --> D[Ca2+ Influx]
    D --> E[Synaptic Vesicles Approach Active Zone]
    E --> F[SNARE-Mediated Membrane Fusion]
    F --> G[Neurotransmitter Released into Cleft]
    G --> H[Diffusion Across 20-40 nm Cleft]
    H --> I[Binding to Postsynaptic Receptors]
    I --> J{Ionotropic or Metabotropic?}
    J -->|Ionotropic| K[Fast EPSP or IPSP within 1 ms]
    J -->|Metabotropic| L[Slow Modulation via G-Proteins]
    K --> M[Reuptake and Degradation]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Signal Termination]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Electrical Synapse (Gap Junction)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Presynaptic Cell Membrane] --> B[Connexon Hemichannel]
    B --> C[Docked Connexon Pair]
    C --> D[Aqueous Pore 1-2 nm]
    D --> E[Postsynaptic Cell Membrane]
    F[Depolarization in Cell A] --> G{Voltage Gradient}
    G -->|Direct Ionic Flow| D
    D -->|Bidirectional| H[Depolarization in Cell B]
    H --> I[Synchronized Firing]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — EPSP and IPSP Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[Neurotransmitter Released] --> Bind{Binds to Receptor}
    Bind -->|AMPA, NMDA, nAChR| Exc[Excitatory Receptor]
    Bind -->|GABA-A, GABA-B, Glycine-R| Inh[Inhibitory Receptor]
    Exc --> Open1[Open Non-Selective Cation Channel]
    Open1 --> Na1[Na+ Influx]
    Na1 --> Depol[Membrane Depolarization]
    Depol --> EPSP[EPSP Generated]
    EPSP -->|If Threshold Reached| AP[Action Potential]
    Inh --> Open2[Open Cl- or K+ Channel]
    Open2 --> Cl1[Cl- Influx or K+ Efflux]
    Cl1 --> Hyper[Membrane Hyperpolarization or Shunting]
    Hyper --> IPSP[IPSP Generated]
    IPSP -->|AP Blocked| NoAP[Action Potential Suppressed]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Block Diagram — Integration at Axon Hillock

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Inputs
        E1[Excitatory Synapse 1]
        E2[Excitatory Synapse 2]
        E3[Excitatory Synapse 3]
        I1[Inhibitory Synapse 1]
        I2[Inhibitory Synapse 2]
    end
    S1[Temporal Summation] --> H
    S2[Spatial Summation] --> H
    E1 --> S1
    E1 --> S2
    E2 --> S1
    E2 --> S2
    E3 --> S2
    I1 --> S1
    I1 --> S2
    I2 --> S2
    H[Axon Hillock Integration] --> Decision{Threshold Reached?}
    Decision -->|Yes| AP[Action Potential Generated]
    Decision -->|No| NoFire[No Firing]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define an excitatory postsynaptic potential (EPSP). Mention any two features distinguishing it from an action potential.**

**Model Answer:**
An **Excitatory Postsynaptic Potential (EPSP)** is a transient, graded, sub-threshold depolarization of the postsynaptic membrane potential resulting from the opening of ligand-gated cation (mainly Na⁺) channels following neurotransmitter binding at an excitatory synapse. The amplitude of an EPSP typically ranges from **+0.1 mV to +5 mV**, in contrast to the all-or-none action potential of **~ +100 mV** amplitude.

| Feature | EPSP | Action Potential |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Amplitude | **Graded (0.1 – 5 mV)** | **All-or-none (~100 mV)** |
| Propagation | **Decremental** (passive) | **Non-decremental** (active) |
| Refractory Period | **Absent** | **Present** (absolute + relative) |
| Initiation | Postsynaptic receptors | Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels at axon hillock |

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a synaptic delay? State its typical value and the rate-limiting step responsible for it.**

**Model Answer:**
**Synaptic delay** is the brief time interval between the arrival of an action potential at the presynaptic terminal and the onset of the postsynaptic potential. Its typical value is **0.3 – 0.5 ms** (300 – 500 μs). The **rate-limiting step** is the **calcium-dependent fusion of synaptic vesicles** with the presynaptic membrane, mediated by SNARE proteins and triggered by Ca²⁺ entry through voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels. The other steps (Ca²⁺ influx, diffusion across the cleft, receptor binding) collectively contribute a much smaller fraction of the total delay.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare electrical and chemical synapses with respect to (i) structural features, (ii) mode of signal transmission, (iii) speed, (iv) directionality, and (v) plastic capability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the sequence of events involved in chemical synaptic transmission. Calculate the reversal potential for K⁺ given $[K^+]_i = 150$ mM and $[K^+]_o = 4.5$ mM at 37 °C. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Structural Features:**
- **Electrical synapse:** Formed by **gap junctions** — clusters of connexons (6 connexin subunits per hemichannel), bridging two cells across a narrow intercellular gap of **2 – 4 nm**.
- **Chemical synapse:** Comprises a **presynaptic terminal** (with mitochondria, synaptic vesicles, active zone with Ca²⁺ channels), a **synaptic cleft** of **20 – 40 nm**, and a **postsynaptic membrane** with receptor proteins.

**Mode of Signal Transmission:**
- Electrical: Direct ionic current flow through connexon pores (electrotonic).
- Chemical: Release of neurotransmitter (chemical messenger) into the cleft, binding to postsynaptic receptors.

**Speed:**
- Electrical: Virtually instantaneous, delay **< 0.1 ms**.
- Chemical: Delayed by **0.3 – 0.5 ms** (synaptic delay).

**Directionality:**
- Electrical: Usually **bidirectional** (no rectification gate).
- Chemical: **Unidirectional** — presynaptic to postsynaptic only.

**Plastic Capability:**
- Electrical: Limited plasticity; gap-junction conductance can be modulated by pH, Ca²⁺, phosphorylation, but no learning-related long-term changes.
- Chemical: High plasticity — supports **LTP (Long-Term Potentiation)**, **LTD (Long-Term Depression)**, **habituation, sensitization**, the cellular basis of learning and memory.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Sequence of Events in Chemical Synaptic Transmission:**

1. **Synthesis** of neurotransmitter in the presynaptic neuron.
2. **Storage** in synaptic vesicles (~ 40 nm diameter).
3. **Action potential** invasion of the presynaptic terminal.
4. **Depolarization** opens voltage-gated **Ca²⁺ channels**.
5. **Ca²⁺ influx** raises intracellular [Ca²⁺] from 100 nM to ~ 100 μM.
6. **Vesicle docking and fusion** at the active zone (SNARE complex, synaptotagmin as Ca²⁺ sensor).
7. **Exocytosis** releases neurotransmitter into the synaptic cleft.
8. **Diffusion** across the 20 – 40 nm cleft.
9. **Receptor binding** on the postsynaptic membrane (ionotropic or metabotropic).
10. **Postsynaptic potential** generated (EPSP or IPSP).
11. **Termination** of signal by reuptake, enzymatic degradation, or diffusion.

**Calculation of $E_K$:**

$$
E_K = \frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[K^+]_i}{[K^+]_o}\right) = 61.5 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{150}{4.5}\right)
$$

$$
\log_{10}(33.33) = 1.5229
$$

$$
E_K = 61.5 \times 1.5229 = 93.66 \text{ mV}
$$

The sign convention: since $[K^+]_i \gg [K^+]_o$, the K⁺ equilibrium potential is **negative** inside, so $E_K \approx -94$ mV (the convention takes the inside relative to outside).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with suitable diagrams the ionic basis of EPSP and IPSP. Mention the principal neurotransmitters and receptors involved in each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The reversal potential for Cl⁻ in a mammalian neuron is measured as $E_{Cl} = -70$ mV. Given $[Cl^-]_o = 110$ mM at 37 °C, calculate $[Cl^-]_i$. If the resting membrane potential is $-65$ mV, predict the direction of Cl⁻ current and the type of response (EPSP or IPSP) when GABA$_A$ channels open. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Ionic Basis of EPSP:**
- At an excitatory synapse, the neurotransmitter (e.g., **glutamate** acting on **AMPA/NMDA receptors**, or **acetylcholine** on **nicotinic AChR**) opens non-selective cation channels.
- These channels allow **Na⁺ influx** (and some K⁺ efflux, Ca²⁺ influx via NMDA).
- The reversal potential is ~ 0 mV, and since $V_{rest} = -70$ mV, the **driving force** is large and inward.
- Result: a **depolarizing EPSP** of amplitude +0.1 to +5 mV.

**Ionic Basis of IPSP:**
- At an inhibitory synapse, the neurotransmitter (e.g., **GABA** acting on **GABA$_A$** or **glycine** on **glycine receptor**) opens **Cl⁻-selective channels** (or K⁺ channels for GABA$_B$).
- Cl⁻ flows inward (since $E_{Cl} \approx -70$ mV, near rest), but the key effect is **stabilization of $V_m$** and **shunting** of excitatory currents by the lowered input resistance.
- For GABA$_B$ or muscarinic, K⁺ channels open, K⁺ efflux occurs, and $V_m$ is driven toward $E_K = -94$ mV (hyperpolarization).
- Result: an **IPSP** that hyperpolarizes or shunts the membrane.

| Synapse Type | Neurotransmitter | Receptor | Ion | Reversal Potential |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Excitatory | Glutamate | AMPA / NMDA | Na⁺ (K⁺, Ca²⁺) | ~ 0 mV |
| Excitatory | Acetylcholine | Nicotinic (N$_M$, N$_N$) | Na⁺, K⁺ | ~ 0 mV |
| Inhibitory | GABA | GABA$_A$ | Cl⁻ | ~ −70 mV |
| Inhibitory | GABA | GABA$_B$ | K⁺ | ~ −90 mV |
| Inhibitory | Glycine | Glycine-R | Cl⁻ | ~ −70 mV |

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Calculation of $[Cl^-]_i$:**

Using the Nernst equation with $z = -1$:

$$
E_{Cl} = \frac{61.5}{-1} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[Cl^-]_o}{[Cl^-]_i}\right)
$$

$$
-70 = -61.5 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{110}{[Cl^-]_i}\right)
$$

$$
\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{110}{[Cl^-]_i}\right) = \frac{70}{61.5} = 1.1382
$$

$$
\frac{110}{[Cl^-]_i} = 10^{1.1382} = 13.74
$$

$$
[Cl^-]_i = \frac{110}{13.74} = 8.00 \text{ mM}
$$

**Direction of Cl⁻ Current and Type of Response:**

The driving force on Cl⁻ is:

$$
DF_{Cl} = V_m - E_{Cl} = -65 - (-70) = +5 \text{ mV}
$$

Since the driving force is **positive** (inside is 5 mV more positive than $E_{Cl}$), the **electrochemical gradient** drives Cl⁻ **outward** (efflux) when GABA$_A$ channels open. This efflux removes negative charge from the cell, causing a small **depolarization**.

**Caveat:** In this specific numerical scenario, with $V_{rest} = -65$ mV and $E_{Cl} = -70$ mV, opening Cl⁻ channels would actually cause a **depolarizing response**. This means the synapse would function as **excitatory**, not inhibitory. In real neurons, the KCC2 cotransporter maintains $[Cl^-]_i$ low enough (4–8 mM) so that $E_{Cl}$ is more negative than $V_{rest}$, ensuring the inhibitory action. The numerical problem illustrates why **neuronal Cl⁻ homeostasis** is critical — failure of KCC2 (as occurs in epilepsy, neuropathic pain, and spinal cord injury) converts inhibitory GABA signaling into excitatory, contributing to disease pathology.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> 1. **Sign convention trap:** Many students lose marks by mishandling the sign in the Nernst equation. For anions (Cl⁻), $z = -1$, and the formula is often written both ways in textbooks. Always state the convention you are using.
> 2. **Confusing reversal potential with equilibrium potential:** They are the same thing, but students must explicitly state *which ion* the value refers to.
> 3. **Forgetting the synaptic delay in chemical synapses** is a frequent 1-mark loss in 14-mark questions. Always mention the **0.3–0.5 ms delay** and the **Ca²⁺-triggered vesicle fusion** as the rate-limiting step.
> 4. **EPSP vs. Action Potential:** Examiners expect students to clearly state that EPSPs are **graded and decremental**, while action potentials are **all-or-none and regenerative**.
> 5. **Reversal potential calculation:** Use the correct base of logarithm (**log₁₀**, not natural log), and include the **temperature dependence** (61.5 mV at 37 °C, 58 mV at 20 °C, 26.7 mV at 0 °C).

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Synapse** = junction between two neurons (or neuron and effector cell). Two structural classes: **electrical (gap junction)** and **chemical (vesicular)**.
- **Electrical synapse:** connexons, **bidirectional**, delay < 0.1 ms, used for synchronization.
- **Chemical synapse:** unidirectional, delay **0.3 – 0.5 ms**, dominated by **Ca²⁺-dependent vesicle fusion**.
- **EPSP** = depolarization, mediated by **Na⁺ influx** through AMPA/NMDA/nAChR; $E_{rev} \approx 0$ mV; neurotransmitters: **glutamate, ACh**.
- **IPSP** = hyperpolarization or shunting, mediated by **Cl⁻ influx (GABA$_A$, Gly-R)** or **K⁺ efflux (GABA$_B$)**; $E_{rev} \approx -70$ to $-90$ mV; neurotransmitters: **GABA, glycine**.
- **Nernst equation:** $E_X = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}([X]_i/[X]_o)$ mV at 37 °C — gives reversal potential of any single ion.
- **GHK equation:** predicts $V_{rest} \approx -70$ mV from multiple ion gradients and permeabilities.
- **Synaptic current:** $I_{syn} = g_{syn}(V_m - E_{rev})$ — identical in form to a FET's drain current equation.
- **Summation:** **Temporal** (same synapse, repeated firing) and **Spatial** (multiple synapses), both required to reach **~ +7 mV threshold** for AP initiation at axon hillock.
- **Cable properties:** **time constant $\tau_m = R_m C_m \approx 10$ ms** governs PSP decay; **space constant $\lambda \approx 0.5–1$ mm** governs dendritic attenuation.
- **Clinical relevance:** Failure of Cl⁻ extrusion (KCC2 dysfunction) flips GABA from inhibitory to excitatory — implicated in **epilepsy, neuropathic pain, spasticity**. NMDA receptor over-activation causes **excitotoxicity** in stroke. GABA$_A$ antagonists (e.g., **strychnine at glycine receptor, picrotoxin at GABA$_A$**) cause convulsions.
- **Numerical constants to memorize:**
  - Faraday's constant $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
  - $RT/F$ at 37 °C = **26.7 mV** (natural log) or **61.5 mV** (log₁₀)
  - Typical EPSP amplitude: **0.1 – 5 mV**
  - Typical IPSP amplitude: **-1 to -5 mV**
  - Synaptic cleft width: **20 – 40 nm**
  - Gap junction width: **2 – 4 nm**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EPSP & IPSP, Post Synaptic Integration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT303 - MEDICAL PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** EPSP & IPSP, Post Synaptic Integration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining Postsynaptic Potentials

A **Postsynaptic Potential (PSP)** is a graded, transient change in the membrane potential of the **postsynaptic neuron** that occurs in response to the binding of neurotransmitters released from the presynaptic terminal onto specific receptor sites on the postsynaptic membrane. Unlike the all-or-nothing action potential, PSPs are **graded potentials** — their amplitude is directly proportional to the intensity of the stimulus, and they decrement with distance (electrotonic conduction).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The two principal classifications of PSPs are **EPSPs (Excitatory Postsynaptic Potentials)** and **IPSPs (Inhibitory Postsynaptic Potentials)**. The algebraic sum of these potentials at the axon hillock determines whether a neuron will fire an action potential.

### 1.1.1 Excitatory Postsynaptic Potential (EPSP)

An **Excitatory Postsynaptic Potential (EPSP)** is a **transient depolarization** of the postsynaptic neuronal membrane that **increases** the probability of the postsynaptic neuron generating an action potential. The membrane potential becomes **less negative** (moves toward and may cross the threshold of approximately **−55 mV**).

- **Mechanism:** Binding of excitatory neurotransmitters (e.g., **Glutamate**, Acetylcholine at nicotinic receptors) opens ligand-gated Na⁺ (and to a lesser extent Ca²⁺) channels.
- **Ion Movement:** Net influx of **Na⁺** ions down their electrochemical gradient into the cell.
- **Typical Amplitude:** A single EPSP produces a depolarization of approximately **+0.1 mV to +1 mV** from the resting membrane potential (~**−70 mV**).

### 1.1.2 Inhibitory Postsynaptic Potential (IPSP)

An **Inhibitory Postsynaptic Potential (IPSP)** is a **transient hyperpolarization** (or stabilization) of the postsynaptic membrane potential that **decreases** the probability of an action potential being generated. The membrane potential becomes **more negative** (moves away from threshold).

- **Mechanism:** Binding of inhibitory neurotransmitters (e.g., **GABA**, Glycine) opens ligand-gated Cl⁻ or K⁺ channels.
- **Ion Movement:** Net influx of Cl⁻ (or efflux of K⁺) ions.
- **Typical Amplitude:** A single IPSP typically causes a hyperpolarization of approximately **−1 mV to −5 mV** from RMP.

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention used in KTU valuation:** EPSP is conventionally assigned a **positive sign (+)** and IPSP a **negative sign (−)**. A graded potential moving the membrane *toward* 0 mV is positive (excitatory); one moving it *away* from 0 mV (more negative) is negative (inhibitory).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Voting Booth" Model of a Neuron

Imagine a single neuron as a **town meeting** deciding whether to "fire the alarm" (generate an action potential). The decision happens at the **axon hillock** — the neuron's "voting booth."

| Component in the Analogy | Biological Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Each citizen shouting "FIRE!" at the booth | An EPSP arriving at the axon hillock |
| Each citizen shouting "STAY CALM" | An IPSP arriving at the axon hillock |
| The threshold to sound the alarm | The critical firing threshold (~−55 mV) |
| The final tally of votes | The net postsynaptic integration |
| The loud siren that sounds | The all-or-nothing action potential |

A single whisper (a single EPSP of 0.1 mV) is not enough — but if **many citizens shout simultaneously** (spatial summation) or **one person shouts repeatedly in quick succession** (temporal summation), the cumulative depolarization can cross the threshold. Conversely, if inhibitory voices shout louder, they can **cancel out** the excitatory votes, preventing the alarm entirely.

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this:** EPSP = **"Excite"** = **"E"** for **E**nter (Na⁺ enters) = Depolarization. IPSP = **"Inhibit"** = **"I"** for **I**n (Cl⁻ enters) = Hyperpolarization. This is one of the highest-yield mnemonics in KTU Medical Physics Module 1.

---

## 1.3 Defining Postsynaptic Integration

**Postsynaptic Integration** is the **continuous, real-time algebraic summation** of all excitatory and inhibitory graded potentials arriving at a neuron's dendrites and soma, ultimately determining the membrane potential at the **axon hillock (trigger zone)**. It is the fundamental computational mechanism by which a neuron "decides" whether to fire.

- **Two principal modes of integration:**
  1. **Spatial Summation** — simultaneous inputs from *multiple* presynaptic neurons.
  2. **Temporal Summation** — *rapid successive* inputs from a *single* presynaptic neuron.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ionic Basis of EPSP and IPSP

### 2.1.1 EPSP — Sodium-Driven Depolarization

When an excitatory neurotransmitter (e.g., Glutamate) binds to its ionotropic receptor:

1. The receptor undergoes a conformational change.
2. A non-selective **cation channel** opens, primarily permeable to **Na⁺**.
3. Na⁺ rushes **into** the cell down its electrochemical gradient (concentration: ~145 mM outside vs. ~12 mM inside; potential: −70 mV attracts cations).
4. The local membrane potential becomes **less negative** (e.g., from −70 mV to −69 mV).
5. This local depolarization spreads **passively (electrotonically)** to neighboring regions.

### 2.1.2 IPSP — Chloride-Driven Hyperpolarization

When an inhibitory neurotransmitter (e.g., GABA) binds to GABAₐ receptor:

1. The receptor opens a **Cl⁻-selective channel**.
2. Cl⁻ moves **into** the cell (equilibrium potential for Cl⁻ ≈ **−70 mV** in mature neurons).
3. The membrane potential becomes **more negative** (e.g., from −70 mV to −71 mV).
4. Simultaneously, the cell is stabilized — it becomes **harder to depolarize** even if EPSPs occur (shunting inhibition).

> [!NOTE]
> **Shunting Inhibition:** When Cl⁻ channels open, the local membrane conductance increases dramatically. Even if an EPSP tries to depolarize the membrane, the Cl⁻ conductance "short-circuits" the current, effectively dampening the EPSP amplitude. This is why IPSPs are powerful even with small amplitudes.

---

## 2.2 Modes of Postsynaptic Integration

### 2.2.1 Spatial Summation

- **Definition:** The algebraic summation of PSPs originating from **different presynaptic neurons** that arrive at the postsynaptic neuron **simultaneously** at different anatomical locations (different dendrites or the soma).
- **Mechanism:** Multiple EPSPs from separate synapses add together at the axon hillock.
- **KTU Key Point:** Spatial summation involves **parallel processing** of information from multiple input sources.

### 2.2.2 Temporal Summation

- **Definition:** The algebraic summation of PSPs from a **single presynaptic neuron** firing in **rapid succession** before the previous PSP has fully decayed.
- **Mechanism:** The second EPSP arrives while the first is still active, so their amplitudes add.
- **KTU Key Point:** Temporal summation requires a **high-frequency presynaptic firing rate** such that the inter-spike interval is shorter than the PSP time constant (τ).

### 2.2.3 The Trigger Zone — Axon Hillock

- The **axon hillock** has the **highest density of voltage-gated Na⁺ channels** in the neuron.
- It has the **lowest threshold** for action potential generation.
- All PSPs, regardless of where they originated on the dendrites, propagate electrotonically to this site, where final integration occurs.

---

## 2.3 Properties of Graded Potentials (PSPs) vs. Action Potentials

| Property | Graded Potential (PSP) | Action Potential |
|---|---|---|
| **Magnitude** | Variable (graded, ~0.1–5 mV) | All-or-nothing (~+30 mV peak) |
| **Sign of change** | Depolarizing (EPSP) or Hyperpolarizing (IPSP) | Always depolarizing then repolarizing |
| **Propagation** | Decremental (decreases with distance) | Non-decremental (constant amplitude) |
| **Refractory period** | **Absent** | Present (absolute & relative) |
| **Summation** | **Can be summed** (spatial & temporal) | Cannot be summed |
| **Threshold** | No fixed threshold | All-or-none at ~−55 mV |
| **Initiation site** | Dendrites / Soma (postsynaptic membrane) | Axon hillock (trigger zone) |
| **Duration** | ~5–20 ms | ~1–2 ms |
| **Ion basis** | Na⁺ influx (EPSP) / Cl⁻ influx (IPSP) | Sequential Na⁺ / K⁺ flow |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations and constants appear with high frequency in KTU 2024 Scheme Medical Physics examinations (Module 1). Master these for guaranteed marks in derivations and numericals.

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Formula | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $V_m$ | Membrane potential at any instant | mV |
| $V_{rest}$ | Resting Membrane Potential | $\approx -70$ **mV** |
| $V_{th}$ | Threshold potential for AP | $\approx -55$ **mV** |
| $E_{Na}$ | Nernst potential for Na⁺ | $+60$ **mV** |
| $E_K$ | Nernst potential for K⁺ | $-90$ **mV** |
| $E_{Cl}$ | Nernst potential for Cl⁻ | $-70$ **mV** |
| $E_{ion}$ | Nernst equation: $E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$ | Volts (or mV) |
| $V_{net}$ | Net postsynaptic potential: $V_{net} = \sum EPSP - \sum IPSP$ | mV |
| $\tau_m$ | Membrane time constant: $\tau_m = R_m \cdot C_m$ | ~5–50 **ms** |
| $\lambda$ | Space (length) constant | ~0.1–1.0 **mm** |
| $R$ | Universal gas constant | $8.314$ **J/(mol·K)** |
| $T$ | Absolute temperature | $310$ **K** (at 37°C body) |
| $F$ | Faraday constant | $96{,}485$ **C/mol** |
| $z$ | Ionic valence | $+1$ (Na⁺, K⁺), $-1$ (Cl⁻) |

### 2.4.1 The Summation Equations (Examiner's Favorites)

**Spatial Summation:**

$$V_{hillock} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} EPSP_i - \sum_{j=1}^{m} IPSP_j$$

**Temporal Summation (decaying residual + new input):**

$$V(t) = V_{peak} \cdot e^{-t / \tau_m} + V_{new}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Relevance:** Postsynaptic integration is the biological analogue of **operational amplifier (op-amp) summation circuits**. The dendrites function as a passive RC cable, the soma acts as a summing junction, and the axon hillock serves as a comparator with a fixed reference (threshold). This analogy is frequently tested in KTU viva voce and in 14-mark descriptive questions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Nernst Equation Applied to the EPSP Driving Force

The **driving force** for an ion is the difference between the actual membrane potential and that ion's equilibrium (Nernst) potential. The Nernst equation itself is derived from the thermodynamic condition where the electrical and chemical gradients exactly balance.

### Step 1: Set up the electrochemical equilibrium

At equilibrium, the Gibbs free energy change ($\Delta G$) for moving 1 mole of ions across the membrane is zero:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G_{chemical} + \Delta G_{electrical} = 0$$

### Step 2: Express the chemical free energy

For moving ions from outside (concentration $[ion]_{out}$) to inside ($[ion]_{in}$):

$$\Delta G_{chemical} = RT \ln \frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}$$

### Step 3: Express the electrical free energy

For an ion of valence $z$ moved across a potential difference $E_{ion} - E_{inside}$:

$$\Delta G_{electrical} = zF(E_{ion} - E_{inside})$$

### Step 4: Set the sum to zero and solve for $E_{ion}$

Setting $\Delta G = 0$ and taking the inside as reference ($E_{inside} = 0$):

$$0 = RT \ln \frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}} + zF \cdot E_{ion}$$

Solving for $E_{ion}$:

$$E_{ion} = -\frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Nernst Equation**. At body temperature ($T = 310$ K), the coefficient $\dfrac{RT}{F} \approx 26.71$ mV.

### Step 5: Numerical Example — Calculate $E_{Na}$ at 37°C

Given:
- $[Na^+]_{out} = 145$ mM
- $[Na^+]_{in} = 12$ mM
- $T = 310$ K
- $z = +1$

Substituting:

$$E_{Na} = \frac{(8.314)(310)}{(+1)(96485)} \ln \frac{145}{12}$$

$$E_{Na} = (0.02671) \cdot \ln(12.083)$$

$$E_{Na} = (0.02671) \cdot (2.4916)$$

$$E_{Na} \approx +0.0666 \text{ V} = +66.6 \text{ mV} \approx +60 \text{ mV (standard textbook value)}$$

### Step 6: Compute the EPSP Driving Force

The **driving force for Na⁺** during an EPSP is:

$$DF_{Na} = V_m - E_{Na} = (-70) - (+60) = -130 \text{ mV}$$

The **negative sign** indicates that Na⁺ is far from equilibrium and there is a strong inward electrochemical gradient. This is the **physical reason Na⁺ rushes into the cell** during an EPSP, causing depolarization.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Temporal Summation — The RC Decay Model

The postsynaptic membrane behaves as a **parallel RC circuit** (resistor = membrane resistance $R_m$; capacitor = membrane capacitance $C_m$). The membrane time constant $\tau_m$ governs how quickly a PSP decays.

### Step 1: Model the membrane electrically

$$C_m \frac{dV}{dt} + \frac{V}{R_m} = I_{synaptic}(t)$$

### Step 2: Solve the homogeneous equation

The decay portion follows:

$$C_m \frac{dV}{dt} = -\frac{V}{R_m}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dV}{V} = -\frac{dt}{R_m C_m} = -\frac{dt}{\tau_m}$$

Integrating from $V_0$ to $V$:

$$\int_{V_0}^{V} \frac{dV}{V} = -\frac{1}{\tau_m} \int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln \frac{V}{V_0} = -\frac{t}{\tau_m}$$

### Step 3: Final decay equation

$$V(t) = V_0 \cdot e^{-t / \tau_m}$$

### Step 4: Numerical Example — Temporal Summation

Suppose a single EPSP at a synapse has peak amplitude $V_0 = +1$ mV, and $\tau_m = 20$ ms.

**Scenario A:** Second EPSP arrives 5 ms later.

$$V_{residual} = 1 \cdot e^{-5/20} = 1 \cdot e^{-0.25} = 0.7788 \text{ mV}$$

Total membrane potential during second EPSP:

$$V_{total} = 0.7788 + 1.0 = 1.7788 \text{ mV}$$

**Scenario B:** Second EPSP arrives 30 ms later (after full decay):

$$V_{residual} = 1 \cdot e^{-30/20} = 1 \cdot e^{-1.5} = 0.223 \text{ mV}$$

$$V_{total} = 0.223 + 1.0 = 1.223 \text{ mV (less summation)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Temporal summation is *effective* only if the inter-spike interval is **less than ~3τ_m**. Beyond this, the residual EPSP is too small to contribute significantly.

---

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Problem — Combined Spatial & Temporal Summation

**Problem Statement (KTU style):**

A motor neuron receives simultaneous input from 3 excitatory synapses (each producing 0.8 mV EPSP) and 2 inhibitory synapses (each producing 0.5 mV IPSP). All excitatory synapses fire twice in rapid succession with a 4 ms inter-spike interval ($\tau_m = 20$ ms). Calculate the net membrane potential change and determine whether an action potential is generated.

### Step 1: Compute residual from 1st excitatory spike at the time of 2nd spike (4 ms later)

$$V_{residual} = 0.8 \cdot e^{-4/20} = 0.8 \cdot 0.8187 = 0.655 \text{ mV}$$

### Step 2: Compute effective amplitude of 2nd excitatory spike (temporal summation)

$$V_{excitatory\_per\_synapse\_total} = 0.655 + 0.8 = 1.455 \text{ mV}$$

### Step 3: Spatial summation across 3 excitatory synapses

$$V_{exc\_total} = 3 \times 1.455 = 4.365 \text{ mV}$$

### Step 4: Net IPSP contribution (no temporal component given for IPSPs)

$$V_{inh\_total} = 2 \times 0.5 = 1.0 \text{ mV}$$

### Step 5: Net postsynaptic potential at axon hillock

$$V_{net} = V_{exc\_total} - V_{inh\_total} = 4.365 - 1.0 = 3.365 \text{ mV}$$

### Step 6: Final membrane potential and decision

$$V_m = V_{rest} + V_{net} = -70 + 3.365 = -66.635 \text{ mV}$$
