T5 (now called P7) = 15% of head circumference from midline posterior - P3 = 25% from midline - Pz = on midline - P4 = 25% from midline (right side) - T6 (now called P8) = 15% from midline ### Step 3.1 Worked Example: Numerical Calculation > [!NOTE] > **Example Problem:** For a patient with Nasion-Inion distance of **35 cm** and pre-auricular-to-pre-auricular distance of **38 cm**, calculate the position of Cz and Fz. **Given:** $$ D_{AP} = 35 \text{ cm}, \quad D_{LR} = 38 \text{ cm} $$ **Step 1: Calculate Cz position from Nasion** $$ x_{Cz} = 0.50 \cdot D_{AP} = 0.50 \cdot 35 = 17.5 \text{ cm} $$ **Step 2: Calculate Cz position from A1** $$ x_{Cz} = 0.50 \cdot D_{LR} = 0.50 \cdot 38 = 19.0 \text{ cm} $$ **Step 3: Calculate Fz position from Nasion** $$ x_{Fz} = 0.30 \cdot D_{AP} = 0.30 \cdot 35 = 10.5 \text{ cm} $$ **Step 4: Calculate C3 position from A1** $$ x_{C3} = 0.30 \cdot D_{LR} = 0.30 \cdot 38 = 11.4 \text{ cm} $$ **Step 5: Verify total arc sum** $$ 0.10 + 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.10 = 1.00 = 100\% \quad \checkmark $$ ## 3.2 Derivation of EEG Wave Parameters The EEG signal can be mathematically modeled as a sum of sinusoids: $$ V_{EEG}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} A_i \sin(2\pi f_i t + \phi_i) $$ Where: - $A_i$ = amplitude of i-th component (typically 10-100 µV) - $f_i$ = frequency of i-th component (0.5-30 Hz) - $\phi_i$ = phase of i-th component - $N$ = number of frequency components For a typical alpha wave: $$ A = 50 \text{ µV}, \quad f = 10 \text{ Hz} $$ The peak-to-peak voltage: $$ V_{pp} = 2A = 100 \text{ µV} $$ The period of alpha wave: $$ T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.1 \text{ s} = 100 \text{ ms} $$ ## 3.3 Epilepsy Classification Analytical Matrix | Parameter | Generalized Tonic-Clonic | Absence (Petit Mal) | Myoclonic | Focal (Partial) | |-----------|--------------------------|---------------------|-----------|-----------------| | **Consciousness** | Lost | Lost briefly | Preserved | Variable | | **Frequency (Hz)** | Polyspike (15-25 Hz) | **3 Hz** | 4-6 Hz | Variable | | **Amplitude (µV)** | 200-1000+ | 200-500 | 100-300 | 50-200 | | **Pattern** | Polyspike → spike-wave | Spike-and-wave burst | Polyspike-wave | Focal spike | | **Duration** | 1-3 minutes | 5-10 seconds | <1 second | Seconds to minutes | | **Aura** | No | No | No | Often yes | | **Post-ictal** | Yes (confusion) | No | No | Variable | ## 3.4 Impedance Calculation for Electrode Contact For a typical EEG electrode, the contact impedance is: $$ Z = \frac{V}{I} $$ Where V is the voltage and I is the current. The recommended **inter-electrode impedance** is: $$ Z_{electrode} < 5 \text{ k}\Omega $$ For the contact paste (typically Ag/AgCl electrode), the half-cell potential is approximately **+0.223 V** at body temperature (37°C), following the Nernst equation: $$ E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\frac{[Ag^+]}{[Ag]}\right) $$ Where: - $E^0$ = standard electrode potential - $R$ = universal gas constant (**8.314 J/mol·K**) - $T$ = absolute temperature (**310 K**) - $n$ = number of electrons transferred - $F$ = Faraday's constant (**96485 C/mol**) ## 3.5 Analytical Verification of 10-20 System **Verification 1: Sum of percentage intervals** $$ \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{interval}_i = 10\% + 20\% + 20\% + 20\% + 20\% + 10\% = 100\% $$ **Verification 2: Symmetry check** - Left hemisphere: Fp1, F7, F3, T3, C3, T5, P3, O1 (odd numbers) - Right hemisphere: Fp2, F8, F4, T4, C4, T6, P4, O2 (even numbers) - Midline: Fpz, Fz, Cz, Pz, Oz (z suffix) **Verification 3: Coverage** - Total electrodes: **21** (including reference and ground) - Recording electrodes: **19** - Hemisphere ratio: 8 left + 8 right + 3 midline = 19 ✓ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Topographical Map of 10-20 Electrode System ```mermaid graph TB subgraph TopView["TOP VIEW OF HEAD - 10-20 ELECTRODE SYSTEM"] Fp1["Fp1
Frontopolar Left"] Fpz["Fpz
Frontopolar Midline"] Fp2["Fp2
Frontopolar Right"] F7["F7
Inferior Frontal Left"] F3["F3
Frontal Left"] Fz["Fz
Frontal Midline"] F4["F4
Frontal Right"] F8["F8
Inferior Frontal Right"] T3["T3
Temporal Left"] C3["C3
Central Left"] Cz["Cz
Vertex Midline"] C4["C4
Central Right"] T4["T4
Temporal Right"] T5["T5
Posterior Temporal Left"] P3["P3
Parietal Left"] Pz["Pz
Parietal Midline"] P4["P4
Parietal Right"] T6["T6
Posterior Temporal Right"] O1["O1
Occipital Left"] Oz["Oz
Occipital Midline"] O2["O2
Occipital Right"] end Fp1 --- Fpz Fpz --- Fp2 F7 --- F3 F3 --- Fz Fz --- F4 F4 --- F8 T3 --- C3 C3 --- Cz Cz --- C4 C4 --- T4 T5 --- P3 P3 --- Pz Pz --- P4 P4 --- T6 O1 --- Oz Oz --- O2 ``` ## 4.2 EEG Signal Acquisition Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR A["Brain
Neural Activity
10-100 microV"] --> B["Scalp Electrodes
10-20 System
21 Electrodes"] B --> C["Amplifier
Gain 10000-100000"] C --> D["Filter
0.5-30 Hz Bandpass"] D --> E["ADC
Sampling 256-512 Hz"] E --> F["Digital Storage
and Display"] F --> G["Analysis Software
Frequency and Pattern Recognition"] G --> H{"Normal or
Abnormal?"} H --> I["Normal EEG
Alpha Beta Theta Delta"] H --> J["Abnormal EEG
Spikes Sharp Waves"] J --> K["Epileptiform
Patterns"] K --> L["Seizure Type
Classification"] ``` ## 4.3 Epilepsy Classification Flowchart ```mermaid graph TB Start["Epileptic Seizure
Detection"] --> A1["Determine Onset"] A1 --> General["Generalized Onset
Both Hemispheres"] A1 --> Focal["Focal Onset
One Hemisphere"] A1 --> Unknown["Unknown Onset
Insufficient Data"] General --> G1["Tonic-Clonic
Polyspike Pattern"] General --> G2["Absence
3 Hz Spike-Wave"] General --> G3["Myoclonic
Polyspike-Wave"] General --> G4["Atonic
Slow Spike-Wave"] Focal --> F1["Focal Aware
Focal Spikes"] Focal --> F2["Focal Impaired
Temporal Spikes"] Focal --> F3["Focal to Bilateral
Evolution Pattern"] G2 --> Treat["Treatment Plan
Medication or Surgery"] F1 --> Treat F3 --> Treat ``` ## 4.4 EEG Signal Processing Pipeline ```mermaid graph TB Raw["Raw EEG
Multiple Channels"] --> Pre["Preprocessing
Artifact Removal"] Pre --> Filter["Bandpass Filter
0.5 to 70 Hz"] Filter --> Notch["Notch Filter
50 or 60 Hz"] Notch --> Epoch["Epoching
Time Windows"] Epoch --> Feature["Feature Extraction
Frequency and Amplitude"] Feature --> Classify["Classification
Normal vs Abnormal"] Classify --> Output["Diagnostic Output
Seizure Localization"] Output --> Clin["Clinical Decision
Support"] ``` ## 4.5 Electrode Placement Measurement Method ```mermaid graph TB Start["10-20 System
Placement Procedure"] --> L1["Step 1: Measure
Nasion to Inion
Total AP Distance"] Start --> L2["Step 2: Measure
Preauricular to Preauricular
Total Lateral Distance"] L1 --> P1["Mark Fpz at 10 percent
from Nasion"] L1 --> P2["Mark Fz at 30 percent
from Nasion"] L1 --> P3["Mark Cz at 50 percent
Vertex"] L1 --> P4["Mark Pz at 70 percent
from Nasion"] L1 --> P5["Mark Oz at 90 percent
from Nasion"] L2 --> Q1["Mark T3 at 10 percent
from Left Ear"] L2 --> Q2["Mark C3 at 30 percent
from Left Ear"] L2 --> Q3["Mark C4 at 70 percent
from Left Ear"] L2 --> Q4["Mark T4 at 90 percent
from Left Ear"] P3 --> Q2 P3 --> Q3 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **Define Epilepsy. List any four characteristic EEG patterns seen during an epileptic seizure.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Epilepsy is a chronic neurological disorder of the brain characterized by recurrent, unprovoked seizures resulting from abnormal, excessive, synchronous electrical activity of neurons. **Four characteristic EEG patterns (2 marks = 0.5 each):** 1. **Spike:** Sharp transient with duration **20-70 ms**, indicating synchronous neuronal discharge 2. **Sharp wave:** Transient of **70-200 ms** duration, similar to spike but slower 3. **Spike-and-wave complex:** A spike followed by a slow wave; **3 Hz pattern** is pathognomonic of absence seizures 4. **Polyspike discharge:** Multiple spikes in rapid succession, characteristic of myoclonic and generalized tonic-clonic seizures 5. **Hypsarrhythmia:** High-amplitude (>200 µV), chaotic, multifocal spikes seen in infantile spasms (West Syndrome) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse the duration of spike vs sharp wave. **Spike = 20-70 ms** (not 20-200 ms). **Sharp wave = 70-200 ms**. Wrong duration = -1 mark. --- ### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **What is the 10-20 Electrode System? Why is it called "10-20"?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** The 10-20 Electrode System is the international standard method for placing scalp electrodes for EEG recording, established by Herbert Jasper in 1958. It uses **21 electrodes** (19 recording + 2 reference) placed at standardized positions on the skull to ensure reproducibility across laboratories. **Why "10-20" (1.5 marks):** The system derives its name from the fact that electrodes are placed along the lines of the skull at intervals of **10% and 20%** of the total skull distance. The landmarks used are: - **Nasion (front)** and **Inion (back)** for the anteroposterior line - **Left and right pre-auricular points** for the lateral line The pattern of intervals along each line is: **10% – 20% – 20% – 20% – 20% – 10%** (totalling 100%). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "21 electrodes" but forget to mention **2 reference electrodes (A1, A2)**. Always specify: **19 recording + 2 reference = 21 total**. Missing reference clarification = -0.5 mark. --- ## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]** **(a) [7 marks] Explain the International 10-20 Electrode System for EEG recording in detail. With the help of a neat diagram, describe the placement of electrodes and the naming convention.** **(b) [7 marks] Classify epileptic seizures based on onset. Describe the EEG characteristics of generalized tonic-clonic and absence seizures.** --- ### **Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 marks** **1. Introduction (1 mark):** The 10-20 Electrode System, developed by **Herbert Jasper in 1958** and adopted by the **International Federation of Societies of Electroencephalography and Clinical Neurophysiology (IFSECN)**, is the standardized method for placing EEG electrodes. The "10-20" refers to the **10% and 20% intervals** along skull reference lines. **2. Skeletal Landmarks (1.5 marks):** - **Nasion (Nz):** Midpoint of the depression at the bridge of the nose - **Inion (Iz):** Bony protuberance at the back of the skull (occipital bone) - **Pre-auricular Points (A1, A2):** Depressions in front of the tragus of each ear - **Cz:** Vertex (top of the head) — the central reference **3. Reference Lines and Percentage Placement (2 marks):** - **Sagittal (AP) line:** Nz → Fpz → Fz → Cz → Pz → Oz → Iz - **Lateral (coronal) line:** A1 → T3 → C3 → Cz → C4 → T4 → A2 - **Frontal coronal:** Fp1 → F7 → F3 → Fz → F4 → F8 → Fp2 - **Posterior coronal:** T5 → P3 → Pz → P4 → T6 **4. Naming Convention (1.5 marks):** - **Letter prefix:** Indicates brain region (Fp = Frontopolar, F = Frontal, T = Temporal, C = Central, P = Parietal, O = Occipital, A = Auricular reference) - **Number:** Odd numbers = left hemisphere; Even numbers = right hemisphere - **Suffix "z":** Midline electrode (Fpz, Fz, Cz, Pz, Oz) **5. Total Electrodes (1 mark):** **21 electrodes** = 19 recording + 2 reference (A1, A2) --- ### **Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 marks** **1. ILAE 2017 Classification of Seizure Onset (2 marks):** - **Generalized Onset:** Both hemispheres involved from onset - **Focal Onset:** Begins in one hemisphere - **Unknown Onset:** Cannot be classified **2. Generalized Tonic-Clonic Seizure — EEG Characteristics (2.5 marks):** | Phase | EEG Pattern | Duration | |-------|-------------|----------| | **Tonic** | Low-amplitude fast activity (15-25 Hz), then generalized polyspike | 10-30 sec | | **Clonic** | High-amplitude spike-and-wave alternating with muscle artifact | 30-60 sec | | **Post-ictal** | Generalized slowing, then suppression | Minutes to hours | **Clinical features:** Loss of consciousness, body stiffening followed by rhythmic jerking, tongue biting, urinary incontinence, post-ictal confusion. **3. Absence Seizure (Petit Mal) — EEG Characteristics (2.5 marks):** - **Hallmark EEG:** **3 Hz generalized spike-and-wave complexes** (2.5-4 Hz) - **Onset/Offset:** Sudden onset and sudden termination - **Duration:** **5-10 seconds** (rarely >20 sec) - **Background:** Normal between episodes - **Provocation:** Hyperventilation, photic stimulation **Clinical features:** Brief staring spells, eye flickering, unresponsiveness, no post-ictal confusion. Common in children (4-10 years). --- ### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]** **(a) [7 marks] Describe the clinical features and EEG findings in different types of epileptic seizures. Compare the EEG patterns of absence seizures and complex partial seizures.** **(b) [7 marks] With a block diagram, explain the EEG signal acquisition and processing system. Discuss the various artifacts encountered during EEG recording.** --- ### **Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 marks** **1. Clinical Features of Common Epileptic Seizures (3 marks):** | Type | Clinical Feature | |------|------------------| | Generalized Tonic-Clonic | Loss of consciousness, tonic stiffening → clonic jerking | | Absence | Brief staring, unresponsiveness, no post-ictal phase | | Myoclonic | Sudden, brief shock-like muscle jerks | | Atonic | Sudden loss of muscle tone, "drop attacks" | | Focal Aware (Simple Partial) | No LOC, motor/sensory symptoms depending on focus | | Focal Impaired (Complex Partial) | Altered consciousness, automatisms, temporal origin | | Status Epilepticus | Continuous seizure > 5 minutes or recurrent without recovery | **2. EEG Findings in Epilepsy (2 marks):** - **Generalized seizures:** Bilateral, synchronous epileptiform discharges - **Focal seizures:** Localized spikes/sharp waves at the seizure focus - **Interictal spikes:** May appear between seizures - **Ictal patterns:** Continuous rhythmic discharges during seizure **3. Comparison: Absence vs Complex Partial Seizures (2 marks):** | Feature | Absence Seizure | Complex Partial Seizure | |---------|-----------------|-------------------------| | **EEG Pattern** | 3 Hz generalized spike-and-wave | Focal temporal spikes (1-3 Hz) | | **Location** | Generalized, bilateral synchronous | Unilateral, temporal or frontal | | **Duration** | 5-10 seconds | 30 seconds to 2-3 minutes | | **Consciousness** | Briefly impaired | Impaired, with automatisms | | **Post-ictal** | None, immediate recovery | Confusion, drowsiness | | **Age Group** | Children (4-10 yrs) | Adults | | **Prognosis** | Often resolves in adulthood | Chronic, may require surgery | --- ### **Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 marks** **1. EEG Signal Acquisition Block Diagram (3 marks):** ``` Brain → Electrodes (10-20 System, 21 electrodes) → Differential Amplifier (Gain 10,000-100,000) → Bandpass Filter (0.5-30 Hz) → Notch Filter (50/60 Hz) → ADC (Sampling 256-512 Hz) → Digital Storage and Display ``` **2. Description of Each Block (2 marks):** - **Electrodes:** Ag/AgCl disk electrodes placed per 10-20 system, impedance < 5 kΩ - **Amplifier:** Differential amplifier with high CMRR (> 80 dB) to reject common-mode noise - **Filters:** Bandpass (0.5-30 Hz) eliminates DC drift and high-frequency noise; notch removes powerline interference - **ADC:** Converts analog EEG to digital with at least 256 Hz sampling rate (Nyquist criterion) **3. Common Artifacts in EEG (2 marks):** | Artifact | Source | Characteristic | |----------|--------|----------------| | **Eye movement (EOG)** | Eye blinks, saccades | Slow high-amplitude waves in frontal leads (Fp1, Fp2) | | **Muscle (EMG)** | Scalp/neck muscle contraction | High-frequency (>20 Hz) spikes | | **Powerline** | 50/60 Hz AC interference | Regular sinusoidal at 50/60 Hz | | **Cardiac (ECG)** | Heart electrical activity | QRS complexes in EEG trace | | **Sweat artifact** | Skin perspiration | Very slow baseline drift | | **Electrode pop** | Loose electrode contact | Sharp transients | | **Movement** | Patient motion | Large baseline shifts | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Critical for 14-mark questions):** > 1. **Always draw the block diagram** in any EEG question worth 7+ marks — skipping diagram = **-2 marks minimum**. > 2. **Specify the ILAE 2017 classification** for seizure types — older "grand mal/petit mal" terminology may lose marks. > 3. **In calculations**, always show percentage intervals summing to 100% — failing to verify the **10-20-20-20-20-10** pattern loses 1 mark. > 4. **For 10-20 system**, mention **21 electrodes = 19 recording + 2 reference (A1, A2)** explicitly. > 5. **Differential amplifier CMRR** should be > 80 dB for medical-grade EEG. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam:** ### **EEG Fundamentals** - ✅ EEG records **brain electrical activity** with amplitude **10-100 µV** and frequency **0.5-30 Hz** - ✅ EEG is the **gold standard** for epilepsy diagnosis - ✅ Five frequency bands: **Delta (0.5-4), Theta (4-8), Alpha (8-13), Beta (13-30), Gamma (>30) Hz** ### **10-20 Electrode System — Key Facts** - ✅ **Inventor:** Herbert Jasper, **1958** - ✅ **Total electrodes:** **21** (19 recording + 2 reference) - ✅ **Pattern of intervals:** **10% – 20% – 20% – 20% – 20% – 10%** = 100% - ✅ **Skull landmarks:** Nasion, Inion, Pre-auricular points - ✅ **Odd numbers = Left hemisphere**, **Even numbers = Right hemisphere**, **"z" = Midline** - ✅ **Letter codes:** Fp (Frontopolar), F (Frontal), T (Temporal), C (Central), P (Parietal), O (Occipital), A (Aural reference) ### **Epilepsy — Key Facts** - ✅ **Definition:** Chronic disorder with recurrent, unprovoked seizures - ✅ **ILAE 2017 Classification:** Generalized, Focal, Unknown onset - ✅ **3 Hz spike-and-wave** = Absence seizures (pathognomonic) - ✅ **Polyspike pattern** = Generalized tonic-clonic - ✅ **Hypsarrhythmia** = West Syndrome (infantile spasms) - ✅ **Focal spikes** = Partial seizures ### **Epileptiform Patterns — Duration Rules** - ✅ **Spike:** **20-70 ms** (NOT 20-200) - ✅ **Sharp wave:** **70-200 ms** - ✅ **Spike-and-wave:** Spike + slow wave alternating - ✅ **Polyspike:** Multiple spikes in succession ### **EEG Acquisition — Block Diagram Order** - ✅ **Electrodes → Differential Amplifier → Bandpass Filter → Notch Filter → ADC → Display** ### **Key Impedance & Amplifier Specs** - ✅ **Electrode impedance:** < **5 kΩ** (preferred < 1 kΩ) - ✅ **Amplifier gain:** **10,000 to 100,000** - ✅ **CMRR:** > **80 dB** - ✅ **Sampling rate:** **256-512 Hz** (Nyquist requirement) ### **Common Artifacts to Identify** - ✅ **EOG** (eye), **EMG** (muscle), **Powerline (50/60 Hz)**, **ECG**, **Sweat**, **Electrode pop**, **Movement** > [!NOTE] > **One-Line Exam Memory Trick:** > *"From Nasion 10-20-20-20-20-10 to Inion; odd = left, even = right, z = midline; 3 Hz spike = absence."*

topics.title: Block Diagram of EEG Machine, Applications of EEG, Electromyogram: Measurement of EMG - Block Diagram of EMG Machine, Applications of EMG - Myoelectric Control System, Electrodes for Measurement of Bio Potentials - ECG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT303 - MEDICAL PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Block Diagram of EEG Machine, Applications of EEG, Electromyogram: Measurement of EMG - Block Diagram of EMG Machine, Applications of EMG - Myoelectric Control System, Electrodes for Measurement of Bio Potentials - ECG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electroencephalography (EEG)

### Formal Definition
**Electroencephalography (EEG)** is a non-invasive electrophysiological monitoring technique used to record the electrical activity of the brain. It measures the **summed synchronous postsynaptic dendritic potentials of cortical neurons** through electrodes placed on the scalp surface. The biopotential is in the range of **1 µV to 200 µV** with a frequency content of approximately **0.5 Hz to 100 Hz** (clinical band: **0.5 Hz to 30 Hz**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Bookmark:** EEG = measurement of spontaneous brain electrical activity using scalp electrodes, producing characteristic rhythms (Alpha, Beta, Delta, Theta) used for clinical neurological diagnosis.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of the brain as a huge **stadium full of people waving flashlights in patterns**. A single flashlight is too dim to see, but when millions wave in *synchrony*, you can detect patterns from outside the stadium. EEG is the "camera outside the stadium" that records the *combined* light. It cannot see individual neurons — only the macroscopic synchronization that leaks through the skull and scalp.

---

## 1.2 Electromyography (EMG)

### Formal Definition
**Electromyography (EMG)** is a diagnostic technique that records the **bioelectric activity associated with skeletal muscle contraction**. The signal represents the **superimposed action potentials of motor unit action potentials (MUAPs)** detected by surface or needle electrodes. The amplitude range is **50 µV to 5 mV** with a usable bandwidth of **2 Hz to 10 kHz**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a stadium crowd doing "the wave." EMG is like placing **microphones at each seat** — every time one motor neuron fires, it triggers a small "cheer" (an action potential). EMG captures the **summed applause of all active motor units** within a muscle, letting doctors hear whether muscles are firing normally, weakly, or chaotically.

---

## 1.3 Myoelectric Control System

### Formal Definition
A **Myoelectric Control System** is a bioengineering system that translates the **electrical activity of a residual or contracted muscle (EMG signal)** into a command signal for an external device such as a **prosthetic limb, exoskeleton, or assistive robot**.

### Conceptual Analogy
If your muscle's electrical chatter is "speaking," a myoelectric control system is the **interpreter/translator** that turns that chatter into instructions (e.g., "open hand," "close gripper"). It is the bridge between human intent (muscle firing) and machine action (motor actuation).

---

## 1.4 Bio-Potential Electrodes

### Formal Definition
A **bio-potential electrode** is a **transducer that converts ionic currents inside the body into electronic currents that can be processed by an external measurement system**. The most common type is the **Ag/AgCl (silver-silver chloride) electrode**, whose operation is governed by the **half-cell potential (Nernst Equation)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Focus:** "Electrodes for Measurement of Bio Potentials" specifically covers **Ag/AgCl electrodes, half-cell potential, polarization, motion artifacts, and electrode-skin interface** for ECG/EEG/EMG applications.

### Conceptual Analogy
The electrode is a **"bilingual diplomat"** standing at the border of two countries. On the body side, communication happens via **ions in electrolyte fluid**; on the instrument side, communication happens via **electrons in metal wire**. The electrode is the diplomat that *translates* between these two languages, but it sometimes slips up (artifacts, drift, polarization).

---

## 1.5 EEG Rhythms — Quick Reference

> [!NOTE]
> **Brainwave Bands (Must memorize for KTU 2024):**
> - **Delta (δ)** — 0.5–4 Hz — Deep sleep, infants
> - **Theta (θ)** — 4–8 Hz — Drowsiness, meditation
> - **Alpha (α)** — 8–13 Hz — Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed
> - **Beta (β)** — 13–30 Hz — Active thinking, alert state

---

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 EEG Machine — Block Diagram Analysis

An EEG machine amplifies a **microvolt-level** brain signal contaminated with noise. The architecture is carefully designed around **high CMRR, low noise, and patient safety (galvanic isolation)**.

### Block-by-Block Functional Logic

- **Electrodes (Ag/AgCl disc, 10–20 system placement):** Pick up scalp potentials. Typical impedance < 5 kΩ after skin prep.
- **Electrode Selector / Multiplexer:** Routes any pair of electrodes (e.g., Fp1–A1) to the amplifier. Modern digital EEG has 32–256 channels.
- **Differential Instrumentation Amplifier (IA):** Provides **high CMRR > 80 dB**, gain ~1000–10,000, and rejects common-mode 50/60 Hz mains noise.
- **High-Pass Filter (HPF, 0.5 Hz):** Removes DC offset and slow baseline wander.
- **Low-Pass Filter (LPF, 30–70 Hz):** Removes muscle (EMG) contamination and high-frequency RF interference.
- **Notch Filter (50/60 Hz):** Eliminates power-line interference.
- **Variable Gain Amplifier (VGA):** Adjusts signal to ADC input range.
- **Sample-and-Hold + ADC:** Converts analog to digital; sampling rate typically **256–512 Hz** per channel.
- **Microcontroller / DSP:** Performs digital filtering, artifact rejection, and FFT for spectral analysis.
- **Isolation Amplifier (Patient Safety):** Provides galvanic isolation (> 5 kV) to protect the patient from leakage currents; meets IEC 60601-1.
- **Display / Recorder / Storage:** Monitor, thermal printer, and hard disk.

---

## 2.2 EMG Machine — Block Diagram Analysis

The EMG signal has a much **wider bandwidth** (DC to 10 kHz) and **larger amplitude** (µV to mV) than EEG, so the architecture has subtle differences.

### Block-by-Block Functional Logic

- **Surface / Needle Electrodes:** Surface for global activity; concentric needle for single MUAP analysis.
- **Differential Preamplifier:** High CMRR (> 100 dB), high input impedance (> 10 MΩ), low noise (< 1 µV RMS).
- **High-Pass Filter (2–20 Hz):** Removes motion artifact and DC baseline.
- **Low-Pass Filter (5 kHz–10 kHz):** Anti-aliasing for needle EMG.
- **Notch Filter (50 Hz):** Power-line removal.
- **Main Amplifier (Gain 100–1000):** Brings signal to ±5 V range.
- **Rectifier + Smoother (Envelope Detector):** For "integrated EMG" (IEMG) used in prosthetics.
- **ADC + DSP:** Sampling at **2 kHz–20 kHz**, computes RMS, mean frequency, median frequency.
- **Audio Monitor + Display:** Plays EMG as audio (helps clinician hear MUAPs) and displays waveform.
- **Isolation Stage:** Patient protection per IEC 60601-1.

---

## 2.3 Myoelectric Control System — Functional Architecture

### Operational Stages
1. **Signal Acquisition:** Surface electrodes (typically a pair) over agonist/antagonist muscle pair (e.g., biceps/triceps).
2. **Pre-Processing:** Amplification, bandpass filtering (20–500 Hz), notch filter.
3. **Feature Extraction:** Common features are:
   - **Mean Absolute Value (MAV)**
   - **Root Mean Square (RMS)**
   - **Waveform Length (WL)**
   - **Zero Crossings (ZC)**
   - **Slope Sign Changes (SSC)**
4. **Classification:** Pattern recognition (LDA, SVM, Neural Networks) maps features to motion intent.
5. **Control Output:** Drives motor, valve, or microcontroller of prosthetic.

---

## 2.4 Electrode Theory — Half-Cell Potential

The **half-cell potential** of an electrode is the potential developed at the metal-electrolyte interface due to charge separation.

**Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** is the reference (0 V).

For the Ag/AgCl electrode (the workhorse of clinical biopotential recording), the half-reaction is:

$$AgCl_{(s)} + e^{-} \rightleftharpoons Ag_{(s)} + Cl^{-}_{(aq)}$$

The Nernst equation gives the half-cell potential:

$$E = E^{0} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{[Ox]}{[Red]} \right)$$

For Ag/AgCl:

$$E_{Ag/AgCl} = E^{0}_{Ag/AgCl} - \frac{RT}{F} \ln [Cl^{-}]$$

At **body temperature (T = 310 K)** and substituting numerical constants (2.303 RT/F ≈ 60 mV), this simplifies to:

$$E_{Ag/AgCl} \approx E^{0}_{Ag/AgCl} - 0.060 \, \log_{10} [Cl^{-}]$$

Where $E^{0}_{Ag/AgCl} \approx +0.222 \, V$ vs SHE.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Half-cell (Nernst) | $E = E^{0} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{[Ox]}{[Red]}$ | $R = 8.314 \, J/(mol \cdot K)$, $F = 96485 \, C/mol$, $T$ in K |
| 2 | Ag/AgCl @ 310 K | $E \approx E^{0} - 0.060 \log_{10}[Cl^{-}]$ | $E^{0} \approx 0.222 \, V$ |
| 3 | Standard potential (37 °C) | $E_{Ag/AgCl} \approx 0.245 \, V$ at body T | vs SHE |
| 4 | CMRR | $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left( \frac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}} \right)$ | $A_d$ = differential, $A_{cm}$ = common-mode gain |
| 5 | Noise figure | $NF = 10 \log_{10}\left( \frac{SNR_{in}}{SNR_{out}} \right)$ | dB |
| 6 | Skin-electrode impedance | $Z = \sqrt{R^{2} + X_{c}^{2}}$, $X_{c} = \frac{1}{2 \pi f C}$ | $C \approx 10–40 \, nF/cm^{2}$ |
| 7 | RMS EMG amplitude | $EMG_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_{i}^{2}}$ | dimensionless index, mV |
| 8 | Mean Absolute Value | $MAV = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert x_{i} \vert$ | use `\vert` for absolute value |
| 9 | Sampling theorem | $f_{s} \geq 2 f_{max}$ | EEG: $\geq 200$ Hz; EMG: $\geq 20$ kHz |
| 10 | Gain (cascaded) | $A_{T} = A_{1} \cdot A_{2} \cdot A_{3}$ | dB: $G_{dB} = \sum G_{i}$ |
| 11 | Power-line rejection | Notch Q-factor: $Q = \frac{f_{0}}{BW}$ | $f_{0} = 50$ Hz (India) |
| 12 | Motion artifact | $V_{artifact} = I_{motion} \cdot Z_{skin}$ | $Z_{skin} \approx 10$–$100$ kΩ |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Clinical diagnosis:** Epilepsy, sleep disorders, myopathies, neuropathy.
- **BCI (Brain-Computer Interface):** EEG-driven spellers, wheelchair control, gaming.
- **Prosthetics:** Myoelectric hands (e.g., Ottobock, COVVI) — multi-million-dollar industry.
- **Rehabilitation robotics:** Exoskeletons controlled by residual EMG signals.
- **Ergonomics & sports science:** Muscle fatigue analysis via median frequency drop.

---

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Nernst Equation for Ag/AgCl Electrode

The general Nernst equation for the half-cell reaction is:

$$aA + ne^{-} \rightleftharpoons bB$$

is given by:

$$E = E^{0} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

where $Q$ is the **reaction quotient**.

### Step 1: Write the half-reaction for Ag/AgCl

$$AgCl_{(s)} + e^{-} \rightleftharpoons Ag_{(s)} + Cl^{-}_{(aq)}$$

The solid phases have unit activity, so they vanish from Q:

$$Q = \frac{[Cl^{-}]}{1} = [Cl^{-}]$$

### Step 2: Substitute into the Nernst Equation

$$E_{Ag/AgCl} = E^{0}_{Ag/AgCl} - \frac{RT}{(1)F} \ln[Cl^{-}]$$

For a single electron, $n = 1$.

### Step 3: Convert natural log to base-10 for ease

Using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$E_{Ag/AgCl} = E^{0}_{Ag/AgCl} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \log_{10}[Cl^{-}]$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the constant at body temperature (T = 310 K)

$$\frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485}$$

$$= \frac{2.303 \times 2577.34}{96485} = \frac{5935.6}{96485} \approx 0.0615 \, V$$

$$E_{Ag/AgCl} \approx 0.222 - 0.0615 \log_{10}[Cl^{-}] \quad \text{(V vs SHE)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This explains why **Ag/AgCl** is preferred: low half-cell potential (~0.22 V) → small DC offset, low drift, and minimal polarization over time.

### Step 5: Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Typical Problem)

If the chloride concentration in the electrode gel is **0.1 M**, calculate $E_{Ag/AgCl}$ at 37 °C.

$$E = 0.222 - 0.0615 \log_{10}(0.1)$$

$$= 0.222 - 0.0615 \times (-1)$$

$$= 0.222 + 0.0615 = 0.2835 \, V \text{ vs SHE}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of CMRR in Differential Amplifier

The instrumentation amplifier ideally amplifies only the difference:

$$V_{out} = A_{d} V_{d} + A_{cm} V_{cm}$$

where $V_{d} = V_{1} - V_{2}$ and $V_{cm} = (V_{1}+V_{2})/2$.

### Step 1: Common-Mode Rejection Ratio Definition

$$\text{CMRR} = \frac{\vert A_{d} \vert}{\vert A_{cm} \vert}$$

### Step 2: Convert to decibels

$$\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left( \frac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}} \right)$$

### Step 3: Numerical Example

If $A_{d} = 1000$ and $A_{cm} = 0.1$:

$$\text{CMRR} = \frac{1000}{0.1} = 10{,}000$$

$$\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(10{,}000) = 20 \times 4 = 80 \, dB$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule of Thumb:** A clinical-grade biopotential amplifier must have **CMRR ≥ 80 dB** at 50 Hz to suppress 50 Hz mains interference by a factor of **10,000×**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Block-by-Block Comparison of EEG vs EMG vs ECG

| Stage | EEG | EMG | ECG |
|-------|-----|-----|-----|
| Amplitude | 1–200 µV | 50 µV – 5 mV | 0.5 – 5 mV |
| Bandwidth | 0.5 – 30 Hz | 2 Hz – 10 kHz | 0.05 – 150 Hz |
| Channels | 16 – 256 | 1 – 16 | 1 – 12 lead |
| Sampling | 256–512 Hz | 2 k–20 kHz | 250 – 1000 Hz |
| Electrode | Ag/AgCl disc (10–20 system) | Surface / concentric needle | Ag/AgCl disposable |
| Notch | 50 Hz | 50 Hz | 50 Hz |
| Gain (typ.) | 10,000–100,000 | 100–1000 | 1000 |
| Patient isolation | Mandatory | Mandatory | Mandatory |
| Artifact | Eye blink (EOG) | Movement, crosstalk | Motion, respiration |

---

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023] Define EEG. List the four major brainwave rhythms with their frequency bands.

**Model Answer:**
Electroencephalography (EEG) is a non-invasive technique that records the spontaneous electrical activity of the brain via scalp electrodes.

The four major rhythms are:
- **Delta (δ):** 0.5 – 4 Hz, deep sleep
- **Theta (θ):** 4 – 8 Hz, drowsiness
- **Alpha (α):** 8 – 13 Hz, relaxed wakefulness with eyes closed
- **Beta (β):** 13 – 30 Hz, active mental activity

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **upper limits** (mixing 30 Hz and 100 Hz). The clinical upper limit is **30 Hz**; higher frequencies are EMG contamination, not brain signals.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] Why is Ag/AgCl preferred over a pure silver or stainless-steel electrode for biopotential recording?

**Model Answer:**
Ag/AgCl is preferred because:
1. **Non-polarizable behavior:** It permits free current flow across the electrode-electrolyte interface with minimal polarization.
2. **Stable half-cell potential:** ~0.22 V vs SHE — much lower drift.
3. **Low offset voltage:** Reduces baseline wander in long recordings.
4. **Low noise:** Stable DC offset translates to lower 1/f and thermal noise.
5. **Reproducibility:** Manufacturing is standardized; performance is repeatable.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module 3 — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU Pattern:** Question 7 in the ESE paper offers Module 3 with internal choice. Each part is **7 + 7 = 14 marks**.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** Draw and explain the block diagram of an EEG machine. Why is a high CMRR essential in the preamplifier stage? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle and clinical applications of EEG. Mention any **four pathological conditions** diagnosed using EEG. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block Diagram of EEG Machine (7 marks):**

The EEG machine consists of the following blocks (refer Section 4.1):

1. **Electrodes (1 mark):** Ag/AgCl disc electrodes placed as per the 10-20 international system. Skin is abraded and gel applied to reduce impedance below 5 kΩ.

2. **Electrode Selector (0.5 mark):** A multiplexer allows the operator to choose any pair of electrodes for differential recording.

3. **Differential Instrumentation Amplifier (2 marks):** Provides high input impedance (> 10 MΩ), gain of ~10,000, and — most importantly — a **CMRR > 80 dB**. The EEG signal is **buried in 50 Hz mains interference** (which appears as common-mode voltage on the body). High CMRR rejects this interference; without it, the microvolt-level EEG would be lost.

4. **Filters (1 mark):** HPF (0.5 Hz) removes DC drift; LPF (30–70 Hz) removes EMG/RF noise; notch filter (50 Hz) cancels mains.

5. **Isolation Amplifier (0.5 mark):** Provides galvanic isolation (> 5 kV) per IEC 60601-1 to prevent microshock to patient.

6. **ADC, DSP, Display (1 mark):** 16-bit ADC, digital filtering, FFT for spectral analysis, monitor and storage.

7. **Why high CMRR essential (1 mark):** The body acts as an antenna picking up 50 Hz mains. This is a **common-mode signal** appearing equally on both inputs. The differential EEG is only a few microvolts — without high CMRR, the 50 Hz interference (~mV) would saturate the amplifier.

**(b) Principle and Clinical Applications (7 marks):**

**Principle (3 marks):**
Neurons of the cerebral cortex generate postsynaptic potentials that, when summed synchronously across millions of cells, produce potentials detectable on the scalp. These potentials are picked up by surface electrodes, amplified, filtered, and displayed as a multichannel waveform. The **International 10-20 electrode placement system** is used for standardized recording.

**Clinical Applications (4 marks):**
1. **Epilepsy diagnosis** — detection of spike-and-wave discharges.
2. **Sleep studies (polysomnography)** — sleep staging using delta, theta rhythms.
3. **Brain death confirmation** — electrocerebral silence.
4. **Brain tumors / lesions** — focal slow-wave activity (delta).
5. **BCI applications** — wheelchair, speller, robotic control.
6. **Anesthesia depth monitoring** — BIS (Bispectral Index).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of an EMG machine and explain the function of each block. Compare surface vs needle electrodes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a myoelectric control system? With a block diagram, explain how EMG signals are used to control a prosthetic limb. List the major signal features used for classification. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block Diagram of EMG (7 marks):**

(Refer to Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.2.)

- **Electrodes (1 mark):**
  - **Surface electrodes:** Non-invasive, pick up global muscle activity; used in prosthetics and ergonomics.
  - **Needle electrodes (concentric/ monopolar):** Invasive, pick up single Motor Unit Action Potentials (MUAPs); used in clinical neurophysiology to diagnose myopathies and neuropathies.

- **Preamplifier (1 mark):** High CMRR > 100 dB, gain ~100, low noise < 1 µV RMS.

- **Filters (1 mark):** HPF 2–20 Hz to remove motion artifact; LPF 5–10 kHz for anti-aliasing; notch for 50 Hz.

- **Main Amplifier (0.5 mark):** Additional gain to bring signal to ADC range.

- **Rectifier & Smoother (0.5 mark):** Produces Integrated EMG (IEMG) envelope for prosthetic control.

- **ADC + DSP (1 mark):** 12–16-bit, sampling ≥ 20 kHz; computes RMS, MAV, median frequency.

- **Display + Audio (0.5 mark):** Visual waveform + audio playback (clinicians "listen" to MUAPs).

- **Isolation (0.5 mark):** Patient safety per IEC 60601-1.

**Comparison (included within 7 marks):**

| Feature | Surface | Needle |
|---------|---------|--------|
| Invasiveness | Non-invasive | Invasive |
| Pickup volume | Large (global) | Small (local) |
| Signal | Superimposed MUAPs | Single MUAPs |
| Pain | None | Mild discomfort |
| Use | Prosthetics, sports | Clinical diagnosis |
| Bandwidth | DC–500 Hz | 20 Hz–10 kHz |

**(b) Myoelectric Control System (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A myoelectric control system is one in which the **EMG signal from a residual or contracted muscle is used as a control input** to drive an external device (prosthetic, exoskeleton, robot).

**Block Diagram Explanation (4 marks):**
(Refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid.)
1. **EMG Acquisition** — surface electrodes on agonist/antagonist pair.
2. **Pre-processing** — bandpass 20–500 Hz, notch 50 Hz, amplification.
3. **Feature Extraction** — MAV, RMS, WL, ZC, SSC.
4. **Classification** — pattern recognition (LDA, SVM, NN) maps features to motion classes (open, close, rotate).
5. **Actuation** — microcontroller drives DC motor in prosthetic hand.

**Major Features (2 marks):**
- **Time-domain:** MAV, RMS, Waveform Length, Zero Crossings, Slope Sign Changes.
- **Frequency-domain:** Mean Frequency, Median Frequency, Power Spectral Density.
- **Time-frequency:** Wavelet coefficients, Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT).
- **Auto-regressive (AR) coefficients** for parametric modeling.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting CMRR justification** in EEG/EMG preamp part → loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up frequency bands** (e.g., stating alpha = 4–8 Hz) → easy mark loss.
> 3. **Missing isolation amplifier** in the block diagram → mandatory per KTU 2024 syllabus (patient safety is a high-weight topic).
> 4. **Not mentioning IEC 60601-1** when explaining safety → explicitly asked in recent papers.
> 5. **No mention of 10-20 system** in EEG → loses definition marks.
> 6. **Wrong half-cell value for Ag/AgCl** — must be ~0.222 V (SHE), not 0.799 V (Ag⁺/Ag).
> 7. **Forgetting sampling theorem** ($f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$) in ADC choice → loses instrumentation mark.
> 8. **Confusing "polarizable" vs "non-polarizable"** electrodes — Ag/AgCl is non-polarizable; platinum is polarizable.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 (EEG / EMG / Electrodes)**

### EEG Essentials
- ✅ EEG = brain electrical recording; amplitude 1–200 µV; bandwidth 0.5–30 Hz.
- ✅ Rhythms: **δ (0.5–4)**, **θ (4–8)**, **α (8–13)**, **β (13–30)** Hz.
- ✅ 10-20 electrode placement system is **mandatory** for KTU answers.
- ✅ Preamplifier **CMRR > 80 dB** to reject 50 Hz mains.
- ✅ Patient isolation per **IEC 60601-1** is required.
- ✅ Applications: epilepsy, sleep study, BCI, anesthesia depth, brain death.

### EMG Essentials
- ✅ EMG = skeletal muscle electrical activity; amplitude 50 µV–5 mV; bandwidth 2 Hz–10 kHz.
- ✅ Surface electrode → global, non-invasive; needle → single MUAP, invasive.
- ✅ IEMG (rectified & smoothed) used in prosthetics.
- ✅ Applications: myopathy, neuropathy, prosthetics, sports science, ergonomics.

### Myoelectric Control Essentials
- ✅ Architecture: **Acquire → Filter → Feature → Classify → Actuate**.
- ✅ Features: MAV, RMS, WL, ZC, SSC, AR coefficients.
- ✅ Classifiers: LDA, SVM, Neural Networks.
- ✅ Used in prosthetic limbs (Ottobock, COVVI), exoskeletons, BCI.

### Bio-Potential Electrode Essentials
- ✅ **Ag/AgCl** is the workhorse — non-polarizable, stable.
- ✅ Half-cell potential from **Nernst equation**: $E = E^{0} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln[Cl^{-}]$.
- ✅ At body T (310 K): $E \approx 0.222 - 0.0615 \log_{10}[Cl^{-}]$ V.
- ✅ Half-cell potentials **subtract** in differential recording → why good matching matters.
- ✅ **Polarizable** electrodes (Pt, Au) → used for stimulation; **Non-polarizable** (Ag/AgCl) → used for recording.
- ✅ **Motion artifact** is the most common error source — minimized by proper skin prep and gel.
- ✅ **Impedance** of skin: 10–100 kΩ, lowered by abrasion to < 5 kΩ.

### Numericals to Practice
- ✅ Nernst potential at given [Cl⁻] and temperature.
- ✅ CMRR calculation in dB.
- ✅ Sampling frequency selection (Nyquist).
- ✅ Cascaded gain in dB.
- ✅ Time-constant of HPF (τ = RC) for given cutoff frequency.
- ✅ RMS / MAV of a sampled EMG segment.

### Cross-Module Links (KTU 2024)
- ✅ Module 1 (Biosignals) ↔ Module 3 (EEG/EMG/ECG) — biopotential origin.
- ✅ Module 2 (Electrodes) ↔ Module 3 — half-cell, polarization.
- ✅ Module 4 (Safety) ↔ Module 3 — IEC 60601-1, microshock, macroshock.

topics.title: EEG & EMG Electrodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT303 - MEDICAL PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** EEG & EMG Electrodes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electroencephalography (EEG) — Formal Definition

**Electroencephalography (EEG)** is a non-invasive electrophysiological monitoring technique used to record the electrical activity of the brain. It is performed using multiple **surface electrodes** placed on the scalp that detect the summed post-synaptic potentials of cortical pyramidal neurons, typically in the frequency range of **0.5 Hz to 100 Hz** with amplitudes between **1 µV to 100 µV**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** EEG electrodes are *transducers* that convert ionic currents (biological) into electronic currents (measurable). The interface between the electrode and skin is the most critical region for signal fidelity.

## 1.2 Electromyography (EMG) — Formal Definition

**Electromyography (EMG)** is a diagnostic procedure that records the electrical activity produced by skeletal muscles. The signal — known as the **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)** — is generated by the synchronous depolarization of muscle fibers within a motor unit. EMG signals typically have amplitudes of **50 µV to 5 mV** and frequency content spanning **20 Hz to 5000 Hz**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Listening to a Stadium Crowd"** 🎙️
>
> - **EEG** is like placing microphones outside a packed football stadium. You cannot hear individual conversations, but you can detect the *collective mood* — cheering, silence, or anxiety — represented by different frequency bands (delta, theta, alpha, beta).
> - **EMG** is like placing microphones *inside* the stadium where specific groups of fans are chanting. You can pick up a single chant clearly, but you also capture background noise from other groups.
> - The **electrodes** act as the microphones; the **skin/tissue** is the wall of the stadium filtering and distorting the sound; the **amplifier** boosts the tiny whispers; and the **display** shows the rhythmic patterns.

## 1.4 Standard Physiological Metrics (Bold Highlights)

| Parameter | EEG | EMG |
|---|---|---|
| **Amplitude Range** | **1 µV to 100 µV** | **50 µV to 5 mV** |
| **Frequency Range** | **0.5 Hz to 100 Hz** | **20 Hz to 5000 Hz** |
| **Source** | Cortical neurons (Pyramidal cells) | Skeletal muscle fibers |
| **Primary Electrode Type** | Surface (scalp) | Surface + Needle |
| **Impedance Target** | < **5 kΩ** | < **2 kΩ** |
| **Standard Reference** | Ag/AgCl (0.223 V) | Ag/AgCl (0.223 V) |

## 1.5 Functional Role of Bioelectrodes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **bioelectrode** is an *electrolyte-metal interface* that performs ionic-to-electronic current conversion. In the context of EEG/EMG, electrodes are essentially **electrochemical transducers** that obey the laws of electrochemistry, electrostatics, and bioimpedance simultaneously.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Electrode–Electrolyte Interface (E-S Interface)

When a metal electrode is placed in contact with an electrolyte (skin/sweat), a **charge double layer** forms at the interface. This region behaves as a **capacitor in parallel with a resistor**, the parameters of which determine the electrode's ability to faithfully record bioelectric signals.

### Layered Architecture of the E-S Interface:

1. **Metal Conductor Layer** – carries electrons.
2. **Helmholtz Double Layer (HDL)** – two oppositely charged ionic layers separated by ~10⁻¹⁰ m; behaves as a **parallel-plate capacitor**.
3. **Diffusion Layer (Gouy–Chapman)** – ions distributed by thermal motion; contributes a **resistive (Warburg) impedance**.
4. **Electrolyte/Tissue** – bulk ionic conductor (electrolyte gel + skin).

> [!NOTE]
> The total **electrode-skin impedance** $Z_{es}$ is modeled as:
> - $C_d$ (double-layer capacitance) in series with $R_d$ (DC resistance), all in parallel with the **half-cell potential source** $E_{hc}$.

## 2.2 Half-Cell Potential — The Origin of Electrode DC Offset

Every metal-electrolyte interface develops a **half-cell potential (HCP)** because of the unequal transfer of charge across the interface. This is the **DC offset** that must be cancelled by the differential amplifier.

### Nernst Equation (Governing Formula)

For the general redox reaction:

$$Ox + ne^- \rightleftharpoons Red$$

The Nernst equation gives the half-cell potential as:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\frac{a_{Red}}{a_{Ox}}\right)$$

Where:
- $E$ = electrode potential (V)
- $E^{\circ}$ = standard electrode potential (V)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96485 C/mol**
- $a_{Ox}, a_{Red}$ = activities of oxidized/reduced species

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At standard body temperature ($T = 310\ \text{K} = 37^{\circ}\text{C}$), the constant $RT/F \approx 0.0267\ \text{V}$. This is the value that should be used in **biomedical electrode** calculations — NOT the room temperature value 0.0257 V.

## 2.3 Polarization — A Critical Distinction

| Type | Mechanism | Behavior | Typical Material |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Perfectly Polarizable** | No charge crosses interface | Behaves as pure capacitor | Platinum, Stainless Steel |
| **Perfectly Non-Polarizable** | Charges cross freely | Behaves as pure resistor | Ag/AgCl (the gold standard) |
| **Intermediate** | Partial charge transfer | RC behavior | Most clinical electrodes |

> [!WARNING]
> Polarization in EEG/EMG electrodes introduces a **DC drift** and **low-frequency noise** that degrades signal quality. The Ag/AgCl electrode is preferred precisely because it is *non-polarizable* and hence has a *low and stable* half-cell potential.

## 2.4 EEG Rhythms / Frequency Bands — The Brain's Fingerprint

| Band | Frequency | Amplitude | Associated State |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Delta (δ)** | **0.5 – 4 Hz** | **20 – 200 µV** | Deep sleep (infants, coma) |
| **Theta (θ)** | **4 – 8 Hz** | **10 – 50 µV** | Drowsiness, light sleep |
| **Alpha (α)** | **8 – 13 Hz** | **20 – 60 µV** | Relaxed, eyes closed |
| **Beta (β)** | **13 – 30 Hz** | **5 – 20 µV** | Active thinking, alert |
| **Gamma (γ)** | **> 30 Hz** | **< 5 µV** | High-level cognitive processing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Significance:** Absence of delta waves in deep sleep suggests neurological pathology. Alpha rhythm suppression on eye opening is called **"Alpha Blocking"** or **"Berger Effect"** — a hallmark of a healthy brain.

## 2.5 Types of EEG & EMG Electrodes — Complete Classification

### A. By Application Depth

1. **Surface (Skin) Electrodes** — non-invasive, used for routine EEG/EMG.
2. **Needle Electrodes** — invasive; used for deep EMG recordings.
3. **Microelectrodes** — sub-cellular recording; tip diameter < 1 µm.
4. **Subcutaneous Electrodes** — fine wires inserted under skin.

### B. By Construction

1. **Metal Plate Electrodes** — solid metal disc; legacy EEG use.
2. **Silver/Silver Chloride (Ag/AgCl) Disc** — modern gold standard; low HCP (~**+0.223 V** vs SHE).
3. **Cup Electrodes** — for EEG; held by adhesive paste.
4. **Dry Electrodes** — no gel; used in wearable EEG.
5. **Active Electrodes** — incorporate pre-amplifier at electrode site.
6. **Concentric Needle Electrode** — for EMG; central wire inside cannula.
7. **Bipolar Needle Electrode** — two parallel wires; differential recording.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| S.No. | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Constants | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nernst Equation | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\dfrac{a_{Red}}{a_{Ox}}\right)$ | $R = 8.314$, $F = 96485$ | HCP calculation |
| 2 | Nernst (at 37 °C) | $E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{0.0615}{n} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{a_{Ox}}{a_{Red}}\right)$ | V, 310 K | Biomedical HCP |
| 3 | Warburg Impedance | $Z_W = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{\omega}} (1 - j)$ | Ω·cm² | Diffusion-controlled |
| 4 | Double Layer Capacitance | $C_d = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d}$ | Farads | Helmholtz model |
| 5 | Skin–Electrode Impedance | $Z_{es} = R_s \Vert \left(R_d + \dfrac{1}{j \omega C_d}\right)$ | Ohms (Ω) | Equivalent circuit |
| 6 | SNR (Electrode) | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{signal}}}{V_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | dB | Signal quality |
| 7 | EMG Peak Voltage (typical) | $V_p = N \cdot \text{MUAP}_{\text{peak}}$ | V (µV to mV) | Recruitment |
| 8 | Motor Unit Action Potential | $\text{MUAP} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{SFAP}_i$ | mV | EMG theory |
| 9 | Alpha Power (EEG) | $P_\alpha = \int_{8}^{13} \vert X(f)\vert^2 \, df$ | µV²/Hz | Spectral analysis |
| 10 | Noise Spectral Density (Ag/AgCl) | $e_n \approx 1 - 3\ \mu\text{V/}\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$ | V/√Hz | Amplifier design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always use **log base 10** form for biomedical Nernst equation problems and remember $T = 310\ \text{K}$ (body temperature), NOT 298 K.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Brain–Computer Interfaces (BCI):** EEG electrodes enable prosthetic control via motor-imagery signals.
- **Epilepsy Diagnosis:** 10-20 system EEG with spike detection.
- **Sleep Studies (Polysomnography):** Continuous EEG monitoring.
- **Rehabilitation Engineering:** EMG biofeedback for stroke patients.
- **Sports Medicine:** Surface EMG for muscle activation analysis.
- **Wearable Health Tech:** Dry electrode EEG headsets (Muse, Emotiv) and EMG armbands (Myo).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Nernst Equation (Thermodynamic Origin)

We begin with the **Gibbs free energy** of an electrochemical reaction, which combines chemical and electrical work:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln\left(\frac{a_{Red}}{a_{Ox}}\right)$$

The relationship between Gibbs free energy and the reversible cell potential is:

$$\Delta G = -nFE$$

And at standard conditions:

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$$

### Step 1: Substitute into the Gibbs Expression

Substituting $\Delta G$ and $\Delta G^{\circ}$:

$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln\left(\frac{a_{Red}}{a_{Ox}}\right)$$

### Step 2: Isolate the Potential $E$

Divide both sides by $-nF$:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\frac{a_{Red}}{a_{Ox}}\right)$$

### Step 3: Convert to Base-10 Logarithm

Using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log_{10}\left(\frac{a_{Red}}{a_{Ox}}\right)$$

### Step 4: Evaluate at Body Temperature (310 K)

Substituting $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$, $F = 96485\ \text{C/mol}$, $T = 310\ \text{K}$:

$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485} = 0.0615\ \text{V}$$

Therefore the **biomedical Nernst equation** becomes:

$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{0.0615}{n} \log_{10}\left(\frac{a_{Ox}}{a_{Red}}\right)$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Half-Cell Potential of Ag/AgCl Electrode

### Reaction at the Ag/AgCl Interface

The half-reaction is:

$$\text{AgCl(s)} + e^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Ag(s)} + \text{Cl}^-(aq)$$

Here, $n = 1$ (one electron), and the only variable species is $\text{Cl}^-$.

### Step 1: Apply the Nernst Equation

$$E = E^{\circ}_{\text{AgCl/Ag}} - \frac{0.0615}{1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{[\text{Cl}^-]}\right)$$

The numerator $a_{Red} = 1$ because the reduced species Ag is a solid (activity = 1).

### Step 2: Use the Standard Potential

The standard potential for the AgCl/Ag couple is:

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{AgCl/Ag}} = +0.2223\ \text{V (vs SHE)}$$

### Step 3: Simplify Using Logarithm Identity

$\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{1}{x}\right) = -\log_{10}(x)$, hence:

$$E = +0.2223 + 0.0615 \cdot \log_{10}\left([\text{Cl}^-]\right)$$

### Step 4: Plug in Physiological Chloride Concentration

The chloride concentration in body sweat/gel ≈ **0.1 M** (varies 0.05–0.15 M):

$$E = 0.2223 + 0.0615 \cdot \log_{10}(0.1)$$

$$\log_{10}(0.1) = -1$$

$$E = 0.2223 - 0.0615 = 0.1608\ \text{V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **actual in-vivo HCP of Ag/AgCl** under physiological conditions. The textbook value 0.223 V is the *standard* potential, not the *physiological* one. KTU examiners often test this distinction.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Skin–Electrode Equivalent Circuit Impedance

### Circuit Model

A standard model treats the skin–electrode interface as:

- $R_g$ (gel resistance) in series with
- $R_e$ (epidermis resistance) parallel to $C_e$ (epidermis capacitance).

### Step 1: Write the Series Combination

The total impedance is the gel resistance in series with the epidermis impedance:

$$Z_{total} = R_g + Z_{e}$$

### Step 2: Write Epidermis Impedance

The epidermis is a parallel RC combination:

$$Z_e = \frac{R_e \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_e}}{R_e + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_e}} = \frac{R_e}{1 + j\omega R_e C_e}$$

### Step 3: Combine

$$Z_{total} = R_g + \frac{R_e}{1 + j\omega R_e C_e}$$

### Step 4: Magnitude

$$\vert Z_{total}\vert = \sqrt{\left(R_g + \frac{R_e}{1 + (\omega R_e C_e)^2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\omega R_e^2 C_e}{1 + (\omega R_e C_e)^2}\right)^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight:** At low frequencies (DC), $C_e$ acts as an *open* circuit, and $\vert Z \vert \to R_g + R_e$ (large). At high frequencies, $C_e$ *shorts* the epidermis, so $\vert Z \vert \to R_g$ (small). This is why EEG signals (< 100 Hz) require good skin preparation to lower $R_e$.

### Step 5: Numerical Example

Given: $R_g = 100\ \Omega$, $R_e = 50\ \text{k}\Omega$, $C_e = 10\ \text{nF}$, at $f = 10\ \text{Hz}$ (alpha band):

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 62.83\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega R_e C_e = 62.83 \times 50000 \times 10^{-8} = 0.0314$$

$$Z_e = \frac{50000}{1 + j(0.0314)} \approx \frac{50000}{1.0005} \approx 49975\ \Omega$$

$$\vert Z_{total}\vert \approx 100 + 49975 = 50075\ \Omega \approx 50\ \text{k}\Omega$$

This high impedance explains the strict requirement for **input impedance of EEG amplifiers to be > 100 MΩ** (a *1000:1* ratio to minimize signal loss).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — EMG Signal Amplitude from Motor Unit Summation

### Background: The Motor Unit

A **motor unit** = 1 motor neuron + all muscle fibers it innervates. Each fiber produces a **Single Fiber Action Potential (SFAP)**; the sum is the **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)**.

### Step 1: Define the MUAP

If a motor unit has $N$ fibers, each contributing an SFAP $s_i(t)$:

$$\text{MUAP}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} s_i(t)$$

### Step 2: Peak Amplitude of MUAP

Assuming similar SFAP shapes, the peak MUAP voltage is approximately:

$$V_{MUAP, peak} = N \cdot V_{SFAP, peak}$$

For a typical SFAP peak of $V_{SFAP} \approx 50\ \mu\text{V}$ and $N = 100$ fibers per motor unit:

$$V_{MUAP, peak} = 100 \times 50\ \mu\text{V} = 5\ \text{mV}$$

### Step 3: Surface EMG (sEMG) with Multiple Motor Units

During voluntary contraction, $M$ motor units fire asynchronously. The total sEMG is:

$$\text{sEMG}(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{M} \text{MUAP}_j(t) + n(t)$$

where $n(t)$ is measurement noise.

### Step 4: RMS Amplitude (Time-Domain Feature)

$$V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_0^T [\text{sEMG}(t)]^2 \, dt}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The RMS amplitude is the most clinically used EMG feature because it correlates linearly with muscle force. A doubling of $V_{RMS}$ typically indicates a doubling of voluntary muscle force.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Frequency-Band Power in EEG

### Step 1: Take the Fourier Transform of the EEG Signal

$$X(f) = \mathcal{F}\{x(t)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j2\pi f t} \, dt$$

### Step 2: Power Spectral Density (PSD)

$$S_x(f) = \frac{\vert X(f)\vert^2}{T}$$

### Step 3: Band Power in Alpha Range (8–13 Hz)

$$P_\alpha = \int_{8}^{13} S_x(f) \, df$$

### Step 4: Relative Band Power (Normalized)

$$P_{\alpha, \text{rel}} = \frac{P_\alpha}{P_{\text{total}}} = \frac{\int_{8}^{13} S_x(f) \, df}{\int_{0.5}^{100} S_x(f) \, df}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clinical Use:** $P_{\alpha, \text{rel}}$ decreases during cognitive tasks ("alpha desynchronization"). This forms the basis of attention-monitoring EEG systems.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — EEG vs EMG Electrodes

| Parameter | EEG Electrodes | EMG Electrodes |
|---|---|---|
| **Typical Size** | 4–10 mm diameter | 5–20 mm (surface); 0.3–0.6 mm (needle) |
| **Site of Application** | Scalp (forehead, temporal, etc.) | Belly of the muscle |
| **HCP** | 0.16 V (Ag/AgCl physiological) | 0.16 V (Ag/AgCl physiological) |
| **Impedance Target** | < 5 kΩ | < 2 kΩ (surface); 1 MΩ (micro) |
| **Frequency Content** | 0.5–100 Hz | 20–5000 Hz |
| **Common-Mode Rejection** | > 100 dB (mains noise) | > 80 dB |
| **Reference Placement** | Ear lobes, mastoid, Fpz (10-20) | Tendon, bony landmark |
| **Special Concern** | Eye-blink artifacts (EOG) | Crosstalk from adjacent muscles |

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the half-cell potential. Why is the Ag/AgCl electrode preferred in EEG/EMG recordings?

`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** The half-cell potential (HCP) is the potential developed at the interface between a metal and an electrolyte due to unequal charge transfer across the boundary. It is governed by the Nernst equation:

$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{0.0615}{n} \log_{10}\left(\frac{a_{Ox}}{a_{Red}}\right)\ \text{V (at 37 °C)}$$

**Why Ag/AgCl is Preferred (2 Marks):**

1. **Low and stable half-cell potential** (~0.16 V in vivo), minimizing DC offset.
2. **Non-polarizable behavior** — current crosses the interface freely with minimal polarization.
3. **Low electrode-skin impedance** (< 5 kΩ with gel), improving signal transfer.
4. **Low noise** (~1–3 µV/√Hz), critical for µV-level EEG signals.
5. **Biocompatible and reusable.**

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between surface electrodes and needle electrodes used in EMG. Mention one clinical application of each.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Surface Electrode | Needle Electrode |
|---|---|---|
| **Invasiveness** | Non-invasive | Invasive (penetrates skin) |
| **Pickup Area** | Large (multiple motor units) | Small (single/few motor units) |
| **Pain** | Painless | Painful; risk of infection |
| **Signal Type** | Global muscle activity | Deep/specific MUAP |
| **Application** | Kinesiology, sports rehab, biofeedback | Diagnosis of myopathy, neuropathy, single-fiber EMG |

**Application (1 Mark):**
- Surface EMG → **Sports biomechanics / muscle fatigue studies**.
- Needle EMG → **Clinical diagnosis of ALS, myasthenia gravis, peripheral neuropathy**.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — `Part (a) 7 marks + Part (b) 7 marks`

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Explain with a neat diagram the equivalent circuit of the electrode–skin interface. Derive the expression for total impedance. Why must the amplifier input impedance be very high?

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Model Answer:**

**Diagram (2 Marks):**

The electrode–skin equivalent circuit contains:

- $R_g$ = gel/electrolyte resistance (in series)
- $R_d$ = tissue/dermis resistance (parallel)
- $C_d$ = double-layer capacitance at the metal-electrolyte boundary (parallel to $R_d$)
- $E_{hc}$ = half-cell potential DC source (in series with the rest)

A differential amplifier reads the signal between the two electrodes, both contributing these elements.

**Derivation (3 Marks):**

The series combination of gel and tissue impedances gives:

$$Z_{total} = R_g + \frac{R_d}{1 + j\omega R_d C_d}$$

The magnitude is:

$$\vert Z_{total}\vert = \sqrt{\left(R_g + \frac{R_d}{1 + (\omega R_d C_d)^2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\omega R_d^2 C_d}{1 + (\omega R_d C_d)^2}\right)^2}$$

At high frequency, $\vert Z_{total}\vert \to R_g$ (capacitor shorts the dermis).
At low frequency (DC), $\vert Z_{total}\vert \to R_g + R_d$ (capacitor blocks DC).

**Why high input impedance? (2 Marks):**

The EEG/EMG signal current $I_s$ is divided between the source impedance $Z_s$ and amplifier input impedance $Z_{in}$:

$$V_{measured} = V_{signal} \cdot \frac{Z_{in}}{Z_s + Z_{in}}$$

To capture > 99.9% of the signal, the standard rule is:

$$Z_{in} \geq 1000 \times Z_s$$

For $Z_s \approx 5\ \text{k}\Omega$ (typical EEG), we need $Z_{in} \geq 5\ \text{M}\Omega$. Commercial EEG amplifiers use $Z_{in} > 100\ \text{M}\Omega$ to allow for variable skin conditions.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: With a suitable diagram, explain the international 10-20 electrode placement system for EEG. List the various EEG rhythms with their frequency ranges and clinical significance.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**10-20 System (3 Marks):**

The 10-20 system is the international standard for EEG electrode placement, defined by the *International Federation of Clinical Neurophysiology (IFCN)*. The procedure:

1. Measure total skull distances: **nasion-to-inion** (front to back) and **preauricular-to-preauricular** (left to right ear).
2. Place electrodes at **10% or 20%** intervals along these lines.
3. Use anatomical landmarks: **Fpz** (frontopolar), **Cz** (vertex), **Pz** (midline parietal), **Oz** (occipital), and reference at **A1, A2** (ear lobes).
4. Total of **21 electrodes** in a clinical montage; reduced montages (e.g., 8 channels) for ambulatory use.

Odd-numbered electrodes are on the **left** (F3, C3, P3, O1, T3, T5, F7).
Even-numbered electrodes are on the **right** (F4, C4, P4, O2, T4, T6, F8).
"z" denotes **midline** (Fz, Cz, Pz, Oz).

**EEG Rhythms (4 Marks):**

| Rhythm | Frequency (Hz) | Amplitude (µV) | Clinical State |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Delta (δ)** | 0.5 – 4 | 20 – 200 | Deep sleep, coma, infants |
| **Theta (θ)** | 4 – 8 | 10 – 50 | Drowsiness, meditation |
| **Alpha (α)** | 8 – 13 | 20 – 60 | Relaxed wakefulness (eyes closed) |
| **Beta (β)** | 13 – 30 | 5 – 20 | Active thinking, alert |
| **Gamma (γ)** | > 30 | < 5 | Cognitive binding, perception |

**Clinical Significance:** Delta excess in awake adults suggests encephalopathy; alpha blocking on eye opening (Berger effect) is a normal response; theta excess may indicate drowsiness or focal lesions.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — `Part (a) 7 marks + Part (b) 7 marks`

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: What is a motor unit action potential (MUAP)? Derive an expression for the amplitude of the surface EMG signal. Explain the role of surface and needle electrodes in EMG.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**Model Answer:**

**MUAP Definition (1 Mark):**

A **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)** is the spatial and temporal summation of the **Single Fiber Action Potentials (SFAPs)** of all muscle fibers belonging to a single motor unit, recorded extracellularly.

**Derivation of EMG Amplitude (3 Marks):**

If a motor unit contains $N$ muscle fibers, and each SFAP has peak amplitude $V_{SFAP}$, the MUAP peak is:

$$V_{MUAP, peak} = N \cdot V_{SFAP}$$

For voluntary contraction involving $M$ active motor units, the surface EMG is:

$$\text{sEMG}(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{M} \text{MUAP}_j(t) + n(t)$$

The RMS amplitude (most clinically useful):

$$V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_0^T [\text{sEMG}(t)]^2 \, dt}$$

Typical values: $V_{SFAP} \approx 50\ \mu\text{V}$, $N \approx 100$ fibers, hence $V_{MUAP} \approx 5\ \text{mV}$. Surface EMG records a superposition of MUAPs and shows $V_{RMS}$ from 50 µV (rest) to 5 mV (max contraction).

**Role of Electrodes (3 Marks):**

- **Surface electrodes:** Non-invasive, placed on skin over muscle belly. Record summed activity from many motor units. Used in **kinesiology, biofeedback, sports science, prosthetic control**. Disadvantage: crosstalk and inability to isolate deep muscles.
- **Needle electrodes:** Invasive, inserted into the muscle. Record from a *localized* set of fibers. Used in **clinical neurophysiology** to diagnose myopathy, neuropathy, and neuromuscular junction disorders. Concentric needle is the gold standard for clinical EMG.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: Explain the origin of bioelectric potentials. Describe the equivalent circuit model of a bioelectrode and discuss the significance of half-cell potential and polarization in EEG/EMG measurements.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Origin of Bioelectric Potentials (2 Marks):**

Bioelectric potentials arise from the **unequal distribution of ions** (Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻, Ca²⁺) across the cell membrane, maintained by the **sodium-potassium pump**. This creates a **resting membrane potential** of approximately **−70 mV** (inside negative). When a stimulus arrives, ion channels open, ions flow, and the potential **depolarizes** to about **+30 mV**, then **repolarizes**. This action potential propagates along nerves and muscle fibers and can be measured externally using electrodes.

**Equivalent Circuit (2 Marks):**

The bioelectrode interface is modeled as:

- A DC voltage source $E_{hc}$ (half-cell potential).
- A resistance $R_d$ (charge transfer resistance).
- A capacitance $C_d$ (Helmholtz double layer) parallel to $R_d$.
- In series, the gel resistance $R_g$ and tissue resistance $R_t$.

The full model:

$$V_{measured} = E_{hc} + I(t) \cdot \left(R_g + R_t + \frac{R_d}{1 + j\omega R_d C_d}\right)$$

**Half-Cell Potential Significance (1.5 Marks):**

Each electrode develops its own DC potential. Since EEG/EMG use **differential amplifiers**, identical HCPs from both electrodes are **rejected as common-mode**. Any *mismatch* (different metals, gel drying) becomes a **differential DC offset** that can saturate the amplifier. Hence, matched Ag/AgCl pairs with stable HCP (~0.16 V) are essential.

**Polarization Significance (1.5 Marks):**

Polarization introduces a **non-linear, frequency-dependent** impedance. It:
- Causes **DC drift** and **low-frequency noise**.
- Reduces **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)**.
- Generates **motion artifacts** when the gel layer moves.

Ag/AgCl minimizes polarization because it behaves as a *non-polarizable* electrode, with current crossing the interface via reversible redox:

$$\text{AgCl} + e^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Ag} + \text{Cl}^-$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
>
> 1. **Using 25 °C instead of 37 °C** in the Nernst equation — KTU specifically tests the *biomedical* form. Always use $T = 310\ \text{K}$.
> 2. **Confusing standard HCP (0.223 V) with physiological HCP (0.16 V)** of Ag/AgCl.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention CMRR** when justifying the differential amplifier topology in EEG.
> 4. **Mixing up alpha and beta frequency ranges** — a classic trap. Alpha = 8–13 Hz, NOT 8–30 Hz.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in 10-20 system questions** — diagrams carry **2 to 3 marks** explicitly in KTU rubrics.
> 6. **Not mentioning gel/skin preparation** when asked to reduce impedance.
> 7. **Confusing "polarization" with "polarizability"** — they are *opposite* concepts. Polarization is the *effect*; polarizability is the *property*.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **EEG** records brain activity via scalp electrodes; amplitudes 1–100 µV; frequencies 0.5–100 Hz.
- ✅ **EMG** records muscle activity; amplitudes 50 µV to 5 mV; frequencies 20–5000 Hz.
- ✅ **Ag/AgCl** is the gold-standard electrode because it is *non-polarizable*, with a low in-vivo HCP of **~0.16 V** and noise of **1–3 µV/√Hz**.
- ✅ **Nernst equation at body temperature (310 K)** uses the constant **0.0615/n** with log base 10.
- ✅ **EEG frequency bands** (memorize): Delta (0.5–4), Theta (4–8), Alpha (8–13), Beta (13–30), Gamma (>30).
- ✅ **10-20 system**: 21 electrodes, 10%/20% spacing, odd = left, even = right, z = midline.
- ✅ **Berger effect**: alpha blocking on eye opening — a normal, healthy EEG response.
- ✅ **Skin–electrode impedance** ≈ 5 kΩ (well-prepared); amplifier input impedance must be **> 100 MΩ** for > 99.9% signal transfer.
- ✅ **MUAP** = sum of SFAPs; RMS amplitude correlates linearly with muscle force.
- ✅ **Motor unit** = 1 motor neuron + all fibers it innervates (typically 100–1000 fibers).
- ✅ **Polarization** = charge accumulation at interface (problem). **Non-polarizable** electrodes like Ag/AgCl are preferred because they *minimize* polarization.
- ✅ **Surface EMG** = global, non-invasive. **Needle EMG** = local, diagnostic, invasive.
- ✅ **Differential amplifier** in EEG/EMG has high **CMRR (> 100 dB)** to reject mains interference (50 Hz) and electrode common-mode noise.
- ✅ **MUAP duration**: 2–15 ms; **MUAP amplitude**: 100 µV to 5 mV depending on muscle and electrode.
- ✅ **Concentric needle** EMG uses an inner wire inside a hollow cannula — standard for clinical EMG.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Other Potentials - Evoked Potential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBMT303 - MEDICAL PHYSICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Basics of Other Potentials - Evoked Potential # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Evoked Potential (EP):** An evoked potential is a time-locked, stimulus-evoked electrical signal generated by the central nervous system (CNS) — the brain, spinal cord, or peripheral nerves — in response to a specific external sensory, motor, or cognitive stimulus. Because the raw cortical response is buried in much larger spontaneous EEG activity (typically **0.5 – 100 µV** in amplitude), the EP is extracted by **time-domain signal averaging** of many repeated, identical stimuli. In the KTU 2024 syllabus, "Other Potentials" refers to bioelectric signals that are *not* the standard ECG (cardiac) or EEG (spontaneous cortical rhythms). They are: - **Sensory Evoked Potentials (SEPs)** — elicited by stimulating a sense organ - **Cognitive / Endogenous Potentials** (e.g., P300, MMN) — elicited by mental events - **Motor Evoked Potentials (MEPs)** — elicited by cortical or spinal stimulation ## 1.2 The Three Clinically Important Sensory EPs | Acronym | Full Name | Stimulus | Recording Site | Key Waveform | |---|---|---|---|---| | **VEP** | Visual Evoked Potential | Checkerboard pattern reversal | Occipital scalp (Oz) | **P100** (positive peak at ~100 ms) | | **BAEP / AEP** | Brainstem Auditory Evoked Potential | Click sound (rarefaction/condensation) | Vertex / ear lobe (Cz–Ai) | **Jewett Roman Numerals I–VII** | | **SSEP** | Somatosensory Evoked Potential | Electrical pulse on peripheral nerve | Scalp & spine (Fz, C3'/C4') | **N20, P37** | > [!NOTE] > The **P100** is the single most clinically important wave in VEP testing. Its **latency** (delay from stimulus) and **interocular difference** are the diagnostic gold standard for **optic neuritis** and **multiple sclerosis (MS)**. ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you are standing next to a busy highway, trying to hear a single, soft whistle. The traffic noise (random, continuous) drowns out the whistle. Now, suppose you know *exactly when* a short whistle will blow, and you record 1000 successive 1-second windows starting at each whistle. If you **average all 1000 windows together**: - The whistle (signal) always appears at, say, 0.3 s — so it **adds up 1000 times coherently**. - The traffic noise (random) partly cancels — it adds up only as $\sqrt{1000} \approx 31.6$ times. - The whistle emerges from the noise. The brain's evoked potential is exactly that **whistle**, and the EEG background is the **traffic noise**. This is why the **averager** is the heart of every EP machine. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > - Amplitude range of cortical EPs: **0.1 – 20 µV** (vs. EEG of 10–100 µV) > - Latency range: **1 ms – 500 ms** (depending on pathway length) > - Stimulus repetition rate: **0.5 – 30 Hz** > - Number of sweeps averaged: **64 – 2000** (typically 100–500) > - Standard filter bandpass: **1 Hz – 3000 Hz** (BAEP uses 100–3000 Hz) > - Sampling rate: ≥ **10 kHz** for BAEP, ≥ **1 kHz** for VEP # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Averaging Is Necessary — The Mathematics of Noise The recorded voltage at any time $t$ is the sum of the true evoked response $s(t)$ and the ongoing EEG/EMG noise $n(t)$: $$V(t) = s(t) + n(t)$$ The signal-to-noise ratio before averaging is: $$SNR_{\text{in}} = \frac{A_s}{\sigma_n}$$ where $A_s$ is the signal peak amplitude and $\sigma_n$ is the RMS noise. For cortical EPs, $SNR_{\text{in}}$ is typically **< 1** (signal smaller than noise). After averaging $N$ identical, time-locked epochs: $$SNR_{\text{out}} = \sqrt{N} \cdot SNR_{\text{in}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Improvement in SNR** = $\sqrt{N}$. To improve SNR by a factor of 10, you must average **100 sweeps**. To improve by 100, you need **10,000 sweeps**. This square-root law is the central engineering trade-off in EP instrumentation. ## 2.2 Operational Steps of an EP Recording System 1. **Stimulus Generation** — Photic flash / checkerboard pattern / click / electrical pulse, triggered by a clock. 2. **Electrode Placement** — Disc (Ag/AgCl) electrodes on scalp per the **10–20 International System**. 3. **Amplification** — Differential amplifier with high **CMRR** (≥ 100 dB) and gain of **10⁵ – 10⁶**. 4. **Filtering** — Bandpass filter to remove DC drift and high-frequency EMG. 5. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** — 12–16 bit resolution. 6. **Averaging** — Time-locked summation across $N$ sweeps; the averaged waveform is displayed. 7. **Display & Analysis** — Latency (ms) and amplitude (µV) of labelled peaks are measured. ## 2.3 Waveform Nomenclature | Symbol | Meaning | Example | |---|---|---| | **P** | Positive-going peak | P100 | | **N** | Negative-going peak | N75, N145, N20 | | Subscript number | Nominal latency in ms | P100 → positive peak near 100 ms | | Roman numerals (I–VII) | Sequential brainstem generators (BAEP) | Wave V | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Recorded signal model | $V(t) = s(t) + n(t)$ | $s$ = EP (µV), $n$ = noise (µV) | | 2 | SNR improvement | $SNR_{\text{out}} = \sqrt{N} \cdot SNR_{\text{in}}$ | $N$ = number of sweeps | | 3 | Noise amplitude after N averages | $\sigma_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{\sigma_n}{\sqrt{N}}$ | — | | 4 | Signal amplitude after N averages | $A_{\text{out}} = A_s$ (preserved) | — | | 5 | Stimulus repetition period | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ (s) | $f$ = repetition rate (Hz) | | 6 | Total recording time | $t_{\text{total}} = N \cdot T$ (s) | — | | 7 | Interpeak latency (IPL) | $IPL_{xy} = L_y - L_x$ (ms) | $L$ = peak latency | | 8 | Interaural / Interocular difference | $\Delta L = \vert L_{\text{left}} - L_{\text{right}} \vert$ (ms) | — | | 9 | Amplitude peak-to-peak | $A_{pp} = \vert V_{\text{peak+}} - V_{\text{peak-}} \vert$ (µV) | — | | 10 | Nyquist sampling | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$ (Hz) | $f_{\text{max}}$ = signal bandwidth | | 11 | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | $CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ (dB) | $A_d$ = differential gain, $A_{cm}$ = common-mode gain | | 12 | Intensity scale: dB HL | $dB = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right)$ | $p$ = sound pressure (µPa) | > [!NOTE] > **Pitfall callout (from KTU past year scrutiny):** Students frequently confuse the SNR improvement law. Remember — **signal grows linearly (×N)**, but **noise grows as √N**, so the *ratio* improves as √N, not N.
