This is the single most-asked derivation in ESE Part B questions on EPs. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Neurology & Neurosurgery:** Intra-operative monitoring (IOM) using SSEP and BAEP during spinal/brainstem surgery. - **Ophthalmology:** Diagnosis of optic neuritis, MS, glaucoma. - **Audiology:** Newborn hearing screening (BAEP — objective test). - **ICU:** Neurological prognostication in comatose patients. - **Rehabilitation Engineering:** BCI (Brain–Computer Interface) systems use P300 evoked potentials to enable locked-in patients to type. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: SNR Improvement on Averaging (Full KTU-Style) Let the recorded signal in the $i$-th epoch be: $$V_i(t) = s(t) + n_i(t)$$ The averaged signal after $N$ epochs is: $$\bar{V}(t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i(t)$$ **Step 1** — Substitute the model into the sum: $$\bar{V}(t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ s(t) + n_i(t) \right]$$ **Step 2** — Split the sum (linearity): $$\bar{V}(t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} s(t) + \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i(t)$$ **Step 3** — Since $s(t)$ is identical in every epoch, the first term simplifies: $$\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} s(t) = \frac{1}{N} \cdot N \cdot s(t) = s(t)$$ **Step 4** — The noise term: each $n_i(t)$ is an independent sample from a zero-mean random process with variance $\sigma_n^2$. The variance of the average of $N$ independent samples is: $$\text{Var}\!\left[\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i(t)\right] = \frac{1}{N^2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{Var}[n_i(t)] = \frac{N \sigma_n^2}{N^2} = \frac{\sigma_n^2}{N}$$ **Step 5** — Standard deviation (RMS noise amplitude) of the averaged noise: $$\sigma_{\text{out}} = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_n^2}{N}} = \frac{\sigma_n}{\sqrt{N}}$$ **Step 6** — The signal amplitude is preserved ($A_{\text{out}} = A_s$), so the output SNR is: $$SNR_{\text{out}} = \frac{A_s}{\sigma_{\text{out}}} = \frac{A_s}{\sigma_n / \sqrt{N}} = \sqrt{N} \cdot \frac{A_s}{\sigma_n} = \sqrt{N} \cdot SNR_{\text{in}}$$ $$\boxed{\,SNR_{\text{out}} = \sqrt{N}\cdot SNR_{\text{in}}\,}$$ **Step 7 — Quantitative Example (KTU-style numerical):** A VEP has signal amplitude $10\,\mu V$ and noise RMS $30\,\mu V$. The input SNR is $10/30 = 0.33$. To achieve an output SNR of 5 (signal clearly visible), the required number of sweeps is: $$N = \left(\frac{SNR_{\text{out}}}{SNR_{\text{in}}}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{5}{0.33}\right)^{2} = (15.15)^{2} \approx 230 \text{ sweeps}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Sampling Rate (Nyquist) for BAEP The BAEP contains frequency components up to approximately $f_{\text{max}} \approx 3000$ Hz. By the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem: $$f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$$ $$f_s \geq 2 \times 3000 = 6000 \text{ Hz}$$ In practice, KTU/clinical systems oversample at $f_s = 10{,}000$ Hz or higher to allow for the sharp Jewett V peak (which has a duration of only ~0.5 ms). ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Three Sensory EPs | Parameter | **VEP** | **BAEP** | **SSEP** | |---|---|---|---| | Stimulus | Pattern reversal or flash | Click (1–4 kHz) | Median/Peroneal nerve pulse | | Stimulus rate | 1–2 Hz | 10–30 Hz | 2–5 Hz | | Typical sweeps | 100–200 | 1000–2000 | 200–500 | | Sweep duration | 250–300 ms | 10–15 ms | 50–100 ms | | Sampling rate | 1–2 kHz | 10–20 kHz | 2–5 kHz | | Bandpass filter | 1–100 Hz | 100–3000 Hz | 30–1500 Hz | | Diagnostic wave | P100 | Jewett V | N20 (upper) / P37 (lower) | | Clinical use | Optic nerve, MS | Acoustic neuroma, hearing screen | Spinal cord injury, cervical myelopathy | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Evoked Potential Recording System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Stimulus Generator
Pattern OR Click OR Pulse] B[Patient
Sensory Pathway] C[Electrodes
10-20 System] D[Differential Amplifier
Gain 10^5 to 10^6] E[Bandpass Filter
1 Hz to 3000 Hz] F[ADC
12 to 16 bit] G[Time-Locked Averager
N sweeps] H[Display and Marker
Latency Amplitude] I[Clinician Readout
P100 N20 Wave V] A -- Stimulus --> B B -- Evoked Response --> C C -- Microvolt signal --> D D -- Amplified --> E E -- Filtered --> F F -- Digital samples --> G A -- Trigger pulse --> G G -- Averaged waveform --> H H --> I ``` ## 4.2 Flowchart: Signal Processing Path Inside the Averager ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Acquire epoch V sub i of t] S2[Store in memory buffer] S3[Add sample to running sum] S4{Done N sweeps} S5[Divide total by N] S6[Display averaged waveform] S7[Auto-detect peaks P100 etc] S8[Compute latency and amplitude] S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 S4 -- No --> S1 S4 -- Yes --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 ``` ## 4.3 Hierarchy: Brainstem Auditory Pathway and Jewett Waves (BAEP) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Click delivered
to earphone] P2[Cochlea
Auditory nerve] P3[Pons
Cochlear nucleus] P4[Pons
Superior olive] P5[Midbrain
Lateral lemniscus] P6[Midbrain
Inferior colliculus] P7[Thalamus
Medial geniculate] P8[Auditory cortex] P1 -- Wave I --> P2 P2 -- Wave II --> P3 P3 -- Wave III --> P4 P4 -- Wave IV --> P5 P5 -- Wave V --> P6 P6 -- Wave VI --> P7 P7 -- Wave VII --> P8 ``` ## 4.4 Concept Map: Clinical Use of Latency ```mermaid flowchart TD R[EP Recording] R --> R1[VEP P100] R --> R2[BAEP Wave V] R --> R3[SSEP N20] R1 --> D1[Latency greater than 115 ms
suggests demyelination] R2 --> D2[Wave V absent or delayed
suggests acoustic neuroma] R3 --> D3[N20 absent bilaterally
suggests brain death or cord injury] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2024]** **Define an Evoked Potential. Why is signal averaging necessary for its recording?** **Model Answer:** > [!IMPORTANT] > An **Evoked Potential (EP)** is a small, time-locked electrical response of the nervous system to a specific external stimulus (visual, auditory, or somatosensory), with amplitude typically in the range of **0.1 to 20 µV**. > The EP is **buried in the much larger background EEG/EMG noise** (10–100 µV), making the signal-to-noise ratio **less than 1** in a single sweep. **Signal averaging** of $N$ time-locked epochs is required because: > - The **signal adds coherently** (linearly, $\times N$). > - The **random noise adds incoherently** (as $\sqrt{N}$). > - Hence the **SNR improves by a factor of $\sqrt{N}$** after averaging. *(Examiner's note: Mention of "time-locked", "amplitude range", and "√N law" is mandatory for full 3 marks.)* --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **List the three clinical sensory evoked potentials and state one diagnostic use of each.** **Model Answer:** | Type | Stimulus | Diagnostic Use | |---|---|---| | **VEP (Visual EP)** | Pattern-reversal checkerboard | Diagnosis of **optic neuritis** and **multiple sclerosis** (prolonged P100 latency) | | **BAEP (Brainstem AEP)** | Click sound | Detection of **acoustic neuroma** (delayed/absent Wave V) | | **SSEP (Somatosensory EP)** | Peripheral nerve electrical pulse | Intra-operative spinal cord monitoring and **brain death confirmation** | *(Examiner's note: 1 mark each for naming the EP, its stimulus, and its clinical use. Use the table format for clarity.)* --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of an Evoked Potential recording system**. Explain the role of the **differential amplifier** and the **CMRR**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the expression for the **improvement in signal-to-noise ratio** when $N$ time-locked epochs of an EP are averaged. A VEP has an input SNR of 0.5. How many sweeps are required to obtain an output SNR of 10? **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Block diagram & differential amplifier:** The system consists of the following blocks (in order): 1. **Stimulus generator** – produces a precisely timed pattern / click / pulse. 2. **Patient** – the sensory pathway generates the EP. 3. **Surface electrodes** – Ag/AgCl discs placed on the scalp as per the **10–20 International System** (e.g., Oz for VEP). 4. **Differential amplifier** – amplifies the difference between an **active** and a **reference** electrode; rejects any voltage common to both (such as 50 Hz mains interference). 5. **Bandpass filter** – removes low-frequency drift and high-frequency EMG. 6. **ADC** – digitizes the analog signal at sampling rate $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$. 7. **Time-locked averager** – triggered by the stimulus, it sums $N$ sweeps. 8. **Display** – shows the averaged waveform with cursor-based latency measurement. **Role of CMRR:** $$CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right) \text{ dB}$$ A high CMRR (≥ 100 dB in clinical EP machines) ensures that **mains hum (50/60 Hz)**, which appears equally on both electrodes as a common-mode voltage, is rejected, while the small differential EP signal is amplified. **(b) Derivation & Numerical:** From Section 3.1: $$SNR_{\text{out}} = \sqrt{N}\cdot SNR_{\text{in}}$$ Rearranging for $N$: $$N = \left(\frac{SNR_{\text{out}}}{SNR_{\text{in}}}\right)^{2}$$ Substituting $SNR_{\text{in}} = 0.5$ and $SNR_{\text{out}} = 10$: $$N = \left(\frac{10}{0.5}\right)^{2} = (20)^{2} = 400 \text{ sweeps}$$ If the stimulus repetition rate is $f = 2$ Hz, the total recording time is: $$t_{\text{total}} = N \cdot T = 400 \times 0.5 \text{ s} = 200 \text{ s} \approx 3.33 \text{ min}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Describe the **Visual Evoked Potential (VEP)** procedure. Explain the **origin of the P100 wave** and list **three pathological conditions** that prolong its latency. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the **Brainstem Auditory Evoked Potential (BAEP)**. Draw a labelled waveform and tabulate the **origin of Jewett waves I to V**. What is the clinical significance of the **interpeak latency I–V**? **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) VEP Procedure:** - **Stimulus:** High-contrast black-and-white checkerboard pattern that **reverses** (black ↔ white squares) at a rate of 1–2 reversals/second. (A **flash VEP** may be used for uncooperative patients, but the pattern-reversal VEP is the clinical gold standard.) - **Recording:** The active electrode is placed at **Oz** (occipital scalp, over the primary visual cortex), with a reference at **Fz** (frontal midline) and ground at the earlobe. - **Parameters:** 100–200 sweeps, sweep duration 300 ms, filter band 1–100 Hz, sampling rate ≥ 1 kHz. - **Waveform:** A characteristic triphasic waveform **N75 – P100 – N145** is recorded. **Origin of P100:** The P100 is a **positive peak at approximately 100 ms** post-stimulus, generated primarily in the **primary visual cortex (V1, striate cortex, Brodmann area 17)** of the occipital lobe, with contributions from the dorsal lateral geniculate nucleus (LGN). **Three conditions prolonging P100 latency:** 1. **Optic neuritis** (inflammation of the optic nerve). 2. **Multiple sclerosis (MS)** – demyelination of the optic pathway. 3. **Optic nerve compression** by tumor or aneurysm. *(Other acceptable: glaucoma, ischemic optic neuropathy, Leber's hereditary optic neuropathy.)* **(b) BAEP:** - **Stimulus:** Rarefaction **click** of 100 µs duration at **60–70 dB above hearing level (HL)**, delivered via headphones at 10–30 Hz. - **Recording:** Active electrode at **Cz** (vertex), reference at **Ai** (ipsilateral earlobe) or mastoid. - **Parameters:** 1000–2000 sweeps, sweep 10–15 ms, bandpass **100–3000 Hz**, sampling rate ≥ 10 kHz. - **Waveform:** Seven Jewett waves (I–VII) within the first 10 ms. **Origin of Jewett Waves:** | Wave | Approx. Latency (ms) | Anatomical Generator | |---|---|---| | **I** | 1.5 | Distal auditory nerve (VIII cranial nerve) | | **II** | 2.5 | Proximal auditory nerve / cochlear nucleus | | **III** | 3.5 | Pons — superior olivary complex | | **IV** | 4.5 | Pons — lateral lemniscus | | **V** | 5.5 | Midbrain — inferior colliculus (most clinically robust wave) | **Clinical significance of I–V interpeak latency (IPL):** The I–V IPL measures the **central brainstem conduction time**. A prolonged I–V IPL (> 4.5 ms) indicates **retrocochlear pathology**, most notably an **acoustic neuroma (vestibular schwannoma)** at the cerebellopontine angle, or demyelinating disease such as MS. An absent Wave V on the affected side (with intact Wave I) is pathognomonic. --- ## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps in EP Questions:** > 1. **Conflating ECG with EP** — students sometimes describe ECG leads instead of 10–20 EEG electrodes. EP electrodes follow the **10–20 International System**, not the Einthoven triangle. > 2. **Wrong SNR law** — writing "$N$" instead of "$\sqrt{N}$" for the SNR improvement **costs 2 marks instantly** in any Part B derivation. > 3. **Forgetting units** — latencies are in **milliseconds (ms)**, amplitudes in **microvolts (µV)**. Missing units in a table = -1 mark per row. > 4. **Mixing up wave polarities** — VEP's P100 is **positive-up** (P = Positive); SSEP's N20 is **negative-up** (N = Negative). Polarity convention must be explicit. > 5. **Skipping the trigger channel** — the averager requires a **synchronization trigger** from the stimulus generator; omitting this in the block diagram = -1 mark. > 6. **Writing "BAEP" without expansion** — KTU examiners deduct half a mark for unexplained acronyms. --- ## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Evoked Potentials (KTU Module 3):** > - **Definition:** Time-locked electrical CNS response to a specific stimulus; amplitude 0.1–20 µV. > - **Why averaging?** Because the signal is buried in EEG noise; averaging $N$ sweeps improves SNR by $\sqrt{N}$. > - **Three EPs:** VEP (P100), BAEP (Jewett I–VII), SSEP (N20, P37). > - **P100 latency:** Normal ≤ 115 ms; prolonged in MS, optic neuritis, glaucoma. > - **Wave V:** Most robust BAEP wave; absent/delayed in acoustic neuroma. > - **I–V IPL:** Normal ≤ 4.5 ms; prolonged in retrocochlear lesions. > - **10–20 System:** Standard electrode placement for all EPs. > - **CMRR:** ≥ 100 dB to reject mains interference. > - **Sampling rate:** ≥ 10 kHz for BAEP, ≥ 1 kHz for VEP (Nyquist criterion). > - **Filters:** 1–100 Hz (VEP), 100–3000 Hz (BAEP), 30–1500 Hz (SSEP). > - **Sweep count:** ~100 (VEP), ~1000–2000 (BAEP), ~200–500 (SSEP). > - **Key formula:** $\boxed{SNR_{\text{out}} = \sqrt{N}\cdot SNR_{\text{in}}}$. > - **Clinical uses:** Diagnosis of MS, optic neuritis, acoustic neuroma, spinal cord injury, brain death confirmation, and intra-operative monitoring. > - **Engineering applications:** Newborn hearing screening, BCI for locked-in patients (P300), IOM during surgery.

topics.title: Radioactivity: Units of Radioactivity - Law of Radioactive Decay
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT303 - MEDICAL PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Radioactivity: Units of Radioactivity - Law of Radioactive Decay

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Radioactivity — Formal KTU Definition

**Radioactivity** is the spontaneous, statistically random, and uncontrolled nuclear transformation (disintegration) of an unstable parent nuclide into a more stable daughter nuclide, accompanied by the emission of one or more ionizing radiations (alpha particles, beta particles, gamma photons, or a combination thereof). This phenomenon, discovered by **Henri Becquerel (1896)** and extensively characterized by **Marie and Pierre Curie (1898)**, is a property of the atomic nucleus and is independent of the chemical or physical state of the atom.

The **Activity (A)** of a radioactive sample is formally defined as the rate at which nuclear transformations occur within the sample. Mathematically:

$$A = -\frac{dN}{dt}$$

where $dN$ is the expected number of spontaneous nuclear transformations occurring in the time interval $dt$. By convention, $A$ is always taken as a **positive** quantity, hence the negative sign compensates for the fact that $N$ decreases with time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The activity depends *only* on the number of undecayed nuclei present and the decay constant. It does **NOT** depend on temperature, pressure, chemical combination, or external electromagnetic fields. This nuclear insensitivity is the very reason radioactivity forms the foundation of all medical imaging modalities (PET, SPECT) and radiotherapy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Population of Dying Candles"

Imagine a huge room containing **one million identical candles**, each of which has a small, random chance of extinguishing itself in any given second. If you walk back into the room after one hour:

- Some candles will be alive, some will have gone out.
- The number of candles that die **per second** at that moment is the **activity**.
- The fraction of candles dying per second is the **decay constant**.

Crucially, **a single candle does not "know" when it will go out** — its behavior is purely probabilistic. However, when you look at the *entire population*, the decay follows a beautifully predictable exponential curve. This is the central paradox of radioactivity: **individual events are random, but the population obeys a strict law**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Physical Constants You Must Memorize:**
> - **1 Curie (Ci)** = **3.7 × 10¹⁰ disintegrations per second (dps)** — originally tied to the activity of 1 gram of pure Ra-226.
> - **1 Becquerel (Bq)** = **1 disintegration per second (dps)** — the SI unit.
> - **1 Rutherford (Rd)** = **10⁶ dps** = **1 MBq**.
> - **ln(2)** = **0.693147** (the natural log of 2).
> - **Avogadro's Number** $N_A$ = **6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Units of Radioactivity — Engineering-Level Breakdown

The choice of unit depends on whether you are dealing with **trace medical tracers** (nano-Curies in a thyroid scan) or **massive industrial teletherapy sources** (kilo-Curies of Co-60 in a Gamma Knife / cobalt bomb).

| **Unit Name** | **Symbol** | **Equivalent in dps** | **Equivalent in SI** | **Typical KTU Context** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Becquerel | Bq | 1 dps | 1 Bq | SI unit; used in modern nuclear medicine dosimetry |
| Curie | Ci | 3.7 × 10¹⁰ dps | 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq | Legacy/clinical unit for therapy sources |
| Millicurie | mCi | 3.7 × 10⁷ dps | 3.7 × 10⁷ Bq | Typical diagnostic tracer dose (e.g., Tc-99m) |
| Microcurie | µCi | 3.7 × 10⁴ dps | 3.7 × 10⁴ Bq | Brachytherapy seed calibration |
| Rutherford | Rd | 10⁶ dps | 1 MBq | Historical British unit |

### 2.2 Derived/Related Quantities Frequently Tested

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Definition** | **SI Unit** |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| Decay Constant | $\lambda$ | Probability of decay per nucleus per unit time | s⁻¹ |
| Half-Life | $T_{1/2}$ | Time for activity (or N) to fall to 50% of original value | s |
| Mean (Average) Life | $\tau$ | Average lifetime of a single radioactive atom | s |
| Specific Activity | $S_A$ | Activity per unit mass of the radionuclide | Bq/kg or Ci/g |
| Exposure Rate Constant | $\Gamma$ | Exposure rate per unit activity at 1 m | R·cm²/(mCi·hr) |

### 2.3 The Radioactive Decay Law — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1: Statistical Foundation.** For a large population of identical unstable nuclei, the *fraction* of nuclei decaying per unit time is a constant, denoted $\lambda$ (the decay constant). Because each nucleus is independent and identically distributed, the rate of decay is **proportional to the number of nuclei present**.

$$-\frac{dN}{dt} \propto N \quad \Longrightarrow \quad -\frac{dN}{dt} = \lambda N$$

**Step 2: Separation of Variables.** Rearranging gives us a first-order linear ordinary differential equation.

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \, dt$$

**Step 3: Integration with Initial Condition.** Integrate from $t = 0$ (where $N = N_0$) to a generic time $t$ (where $N = N(t)$):

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \ln(N) - \ln(N_0) = -\lambda t$$

**Step 4: Exponentiation.** This yields the **canonical radioactive decay equation**:

$$N(t) = N_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 5: Expression in Terms of Activity.** Since $A = \lambda N$, multiply both sides by $\lambda$ to obtain the activity version of the same law:

$$A(t) = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 6: The Half-Life Connection.** By definition, when $t = T_{1/2}$, we have $A = A_0 / 2$. Substituting:

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}$$

**Step 7: The Mean Life.** The average lifetime of a single nucleus is found by computing the expectation value of $t$ over the exponential distribution:

$$\tau = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \, p(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \, \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

Hence $\tau = 1/\lambda = T_{1/2}/0.693 \approx 1.4427 \, T_{1/2}$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Mathematical Form** | **Typical KTU Application** |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Fundamental decay law | $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | Find remaining atoms after time $t$ |
| Activity form | $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | Find activity after storage/transport |
| Decay constant | $\lambda = -\frac{1}{N}\frac{dN}{dt}$ | Theoretical/derivation problems |
| Half-life | $T_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}$ | Convert between $\lambda$ and $T_{1/2}$ |
| Mean life | $\tau = \frac{1}{\lambda} = 1.443 \, T_{1/2}$ | Statistical lifetime problems |
| Activity–atom relation | $A = \lambda N$ | Calculate number of atoms in a source |
| Number from mass | $N = \frac{m \, N_A}{M}$ | Combine with $A = \lambda N$ for specific activity |
| Specific activity | $S_A = \frac{\lambda N_A}{M}$ | Compare medical isotopes |
| Number of half-lives | $n = \frac{t}{T_{1/2}}$ | Quick mental calculation: $A = A_0 / 2^n$ |
| Curie–Becquerel | $1 \, \text{Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \, \text{Bq}$ | Unit conversion problems |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In medical physics, these equations are not abstract — they govern **daily clinical decisions**:

- **Radiopharmaceutical preparation:** A Tc-99m dose (T₁/₂ = 6 hours) eluted in the morning must be recalibrated every few hours using the decay equation.
- **HDR Brachytherapy:** Iridium-192 sources (T₁/₂ = 73.8 days) are decay-corrected weekly.
- **Patient dose calculation:** Knowing $A = \lambda N$ allows the medical physicist to convert the calibrated source activity (in Ci or Bq) into the actual number of radioactive atoms present, which feeds into dosimetry models.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Radioactive Decay Law

We start from the fundamental postulate that for an isolated population of $N$ identical unstable nuclei, the *probability* that any single nucleus decays in an infinitesimal time interval $dt$ is a constant, $\lambda \, dt$, independent of time and independent of any prior history (memoryless process).

**Step 1:** The expected number of decays in time $dt$ is therefore:

$$dN = -\lambda N \, dt$$

The negative sign indicates that $N$ is a *decreasing* function of time.

**Step 2:** Separate variables:

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \, dt$$

**Step 3:** Apply the integral on both sides. The left integral is the standard logarithm; the right is trivial:

$$\int_{N_0}^{N(t)} \frac{dN'}{N'} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt'$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate:

$$\left[ \ln N' \right]_{N_0}^{N(t)} = -\lambda \, t$$

$$\ln(N(t)) - \ln(N_0) = -\lambda t$$

**Step 5:** Use the logarithm identity $\ln(a) - \ln(b) = \ln(a/b)$:

$$\ln\left(\frac{N(t)}{N_0}\right) = -\lambda t$$

**Step 6:** Exponentiate both sides:

$$\frac{N(t)}{N_0} = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 7:** Solve for $N(t)$ — the canonical decay equation:

$$N(t) = N_0 \, e^{-\lambda t} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Half-Life from the Decay Equation

**Step 1:** Begin with the decay law:

$$N(t) = N_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the *definition* of half-life: at $t = T_{1/2}$, exactly half the original nuclei remain:

$$N(T_{1/2}) = \frac{N_0}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$\frac{N_0}{2} = N_0 \, e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $N_0$ (assuming $N_0 \neq 0$):

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$$

**Step 5:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\lambda T_{1/2}$$

**Step 6:** Recall that $\ln(1/2) = -\ln(2) = -0.693147...$ :

$$-0.693 = -\lambda T_{1/2}$$

**Step 7:** Cancel the negatives:

$$T_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of Mean (Average) Life $\tau$

**Step 1:** The probability density function for a single nucleus to decay at time $t$ is:

$$p(t) \, dt = \lambda \, e^{-\lambda t} \, dt \quad (\text{for } t \geq 0)$$

This is a normalized exponential distribution.

**Step 2:** The mean life is the expectation value of $t$:

$$\tau = \langle t \rangle = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \, p(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \, \lambda \, e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 3:** Use integration by parts. Let $u = t$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt$. Then $du = dt$ and $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$.

$$\tau = \left[ t \cdot (-e^{-\lambda t}) \right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} (-e^{-\lambda t}) \, dt$$

**Step 4:** The boundary term vanishes: at $t \to \infty$, $t e^{-\lambda t} \to 0$; at $t = 0$, the term is zero.

$$\tau = 0 + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the remaining integral:

$$\tau = \left[ \frac{-e^{-\lambda t}}{\lambda} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left(\frac{-1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $\lambda = 0.693 / T_{1/2}$:

$$\tau = \frac{T_{1/2}}{0.693} \approx 1.4427 \, T_{1/2} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked KTU-Style Numerical Problem

**Problem:** A radiopharmaceutical contains **15 mCi** of Technetium-99m (T₁/₂ = 6.05 hours). Find:
**(a)** The decay constant $\lambda$ in s⁻¹.
**(b)** The activity remaining after **18 hours**.
**(c)** The number of radioactive atoms present initially.
**(d)** The mean life $\tau$.

---

#### Solution (a): Decay constant

**Step 1:** Convert half-life to seconds:

$$T_{1/2} = 6.05 \, \text{hr} \times 3600 \, \text{s/hr} = 21{,}780 \, \text{s}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the relation $T_{1/2} = 0.693 / \lambda$:

$$\lambda = \frac{0.693}{21{,}780 \, \text{s}} = 3.183 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{s}^{-1}$$

---

#### Solution (b): Activity after 18 hours

**Step 1:** Number of half-lives elapsed:

$$n = \frac{t}{T_{1/2}} = \frac{18 \, \text{hr}}{6.05 \, \text{hr}} = 2.975$$

**Step 2:** Compute the residual fraction $1/2^n$:

$$\frac{A}{A_0} = \frac{1}{2^{2.975}} = \frac{1}{7.892} = 0.1267$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by initial activity:

$$A = 0.1267 \times 15 \, \text{mCi} = 1.901 \, \text{mCi} \approx 1.90 \, \text{mCi}$$

**Verification using $e^{-\lambda t}$:** $\lambda t = (3.183 \times 10^{-5}) \times (18 \times 3600) = 2.062$. Then $e^{-2.062} = 0.127$, confirming $A \approx 1.90$ mCi. ✓

---

#### Solution (c): Initial number of atoms

**Step 1:** Convert 15 mCi to dps:

$$A_0 = 15 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Ci} \times 3.7 \times 10^{10} \, \text{dps/Ci} = 5.55 \times 10^{8} \, \text{dps} = 5.55 \times 10^{8} \, \text{Bq}$$

**Step 2:** Use $A_0 = \lambda N_0$:

$$N_0 = \frac{A_0}{\lambda} = \frac{5.55 \times 10^{8}}{3.183 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.744 \times 10^{13} \, \text{atoms}$$

---

#### Solution (d): Mean life

$$\tau = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{3.183 \times 10^{-5}} = 31{,}420 \, \text{s} \approx 8.73 \, \text{hours}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\tau = 1.4427 \times T_{1/2} = 1.4427 \times 6.05 = 8.73 \, \text{hours} \quad \checkmark$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Conceptual Logic of the Decay Law

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **logical architecture** of the radioactive decay process — from a microscopic random event to the macroscopic deterministic population-level exponential behavior. This block-level flow is the standard KTU mental model.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Population of N₀ Identical Unstable Nuclei"] --> B["Each Nucleus Has Constant Decay Probability lambda per Unit Time"]
    B --> C["Individual Decay Event is Random and Memoryless"]
    C --> D{"Has Nucleus Decayed in Interval dt?"}
    D -->|"Yes Probability lambda dt"| E["Daughter Nuclide + Emitted Radiation"]
    D -->|"No Probability 1 minus lambda dt"| F["Parent Nucleus Survives to Next Instant"]
    F --> B
    E --> G["dN Equals Negative lambda N dt"]
    G --> H["Integrate over Time 0 to t"]
    H --> I["N of t Equals N₀ times e raised to Negative lambda t"]
    I --> J["Multiply by lambda to Get Activity A of t"]
    J --> K["A of t Equals A₀ times e raised to Negative lambda t"]
    K --> L["Special Case at t Equals T half"]
    L --> M["A Equals A₀ divided by 2"]
    M --> N["T half Equals 0.693 divided by lambda"]
    N --> O["Mean Life tau Equals 1 divided by lambda Equals 1.443 T half"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Unit Conversion Flow

This second diagram depicts the **engineering decision flow** for unit conversion — essential for any KTU numerical problem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Given Activity in Ci or mCi or microCi"] --> B["Identify Numerical Magnitude"]
    B --> C["Multiply by 3.7 times 10 raised to 10 dps per Ci"]
    C --> D["Convert dps to Bq Using 1 Bq Equals 1 dps"]
    D --> E["If Rutherford needed Divide by 10 raised to 6"]
    E --> F["Final SI Activity in Bq or Derived in Ci"]
```

### 4.3 Graphical Schematic — Exponential Activity Decay Curve

A textual reproduction of the canonical exponential decay curve is presented below as a block-schematic; in your exam, you should sketch the actual graph with clearly labeled axes and the standard features highlighted.

```
Activity A (Ci or Bq)
^
|  *
|   *
|    * *
|       *  *
|          *  *
|             *   *
|                *    *  *
|                   *        *  *
|                      *            *  *
|__________________________*__________________*__________*________> Time t
0            T_half          2T_half         3T_half
            (50%)            (25%)           (12.5%)
```

Key features a KTU examiner expects on this graph:
- The **y-intercept** is $A_0$ (initial activity).
- The curve is **purely exponential** — concave-up everywhere (second derivative > 0).
- At each successive half-life, the curve halves in height.
- The **tangent line at $t = 0$** extrapolated to zero intersects the time axis at $t = \tau$ (mean life).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Define the term "activity" of a radioactive sample. Mention its SI unit and the relation between activity and decay constant.**

**Model Answer:**
The activity $A$ of a radioactive sample is defined as the number of nuclear disintegrations occurring per unit time in the sample. Mathematically:

$$A = -\frac{dN}{dt} = \lambda N$$

where $\lambda$ is the decay constant and $N$ is the number of undecayed nuclei. The **SI unit** of activity is the **becquerel (Bq)**, defined as 1 disintegration per second. The traditional/clinical unit is the **curie (Ci)**, where $1 \, \text{Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10}$ Bq.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Distinguish between half-life and mean life of a radioactive nuclide. Establish their mathematical relationship.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Half-life ($T_{1/2}$)** is the time required for the activity (or number of undecayed nuclei) of a sample to reduce to *half* of its initial value.
- **Mean life ($\tau$)** is the *average lifetime* of a single radioactive atom before it decays — it is the expectation value of the decay time over the entire population.

Mathematical relationship:

$$T_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}$$

$$\tau = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

Combining:

$$\tau = \frac{T_{1/2}}{0.693} \approx 1.443 \, T_{1/2}$$

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**
**(a)** State and derive the **law of radioactive decay**. Obtain the relation between decay constant and half-life. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** A radioactive source has a half-life of **30 years**. Calculate:
  (i) The decay constant in s⁻¹.
  (ii) The fraction of atoms that will remain undecayed after **100 years**.
  (iii) The mean life in years. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution (a):

**Statement:** The rate of decay of a radioactive substance at any instant is directly proportional to the number of undecayed atoms present at that instant.

**Derivation:**
For a population of $N$ unstable nuclei, the expected number decaying in time $dt$ is:

$$-dN = \lambda N \, dt$$

(where $\lambda$ is the decay constant per nucleus per unit time).

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \, dt$$

Integrating from $t = 0$ (when $N = N_0$) to time $t$ (when $N = N(t)$):

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN'}{N'} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt'$$

$$\ln(N) - \ln(N_0) = -\lambda t$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\lambda t$$

$$\frac{N}{N_0} = e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$\boxed{N(t) = N_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}}$$

**Relation between $\lambda$ and $T_{1/2}$:**
At $t = T_{1/2}$, $N = N_0/2$:

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$$

Taking natural logarithm:

$$\ln(1/2) = -\lambda T_{1/2}$$

$$-\ln 2 = -\lambda T_{1/2}$$

$$\boxed{T_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}}$$

---

#### Solution (b):

**(i) Decay constant in s⁻¹:**

Convert half-life to seconds:

$$T_{1/2} = 30 \, \text{yr} \times 365.25 \times 24 \times 3600 \, \text{s/yr} = 9.467 \times 10^8 \, \text{s}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{0.693}{9.467 \times 10^8} = 7.32 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{s}^{-1}$$

**(ii) Fraction remaining after 100 years:**

Number of half-lives:

$$n = \frac{100}{30} = 3.333$$

Fraction remaining:

$$\frac{N}{N_0} = \frac{1}{2^{3.333}} = \frac{1}{10.079} = 0.0992 \approx 9.92\%$$

**(iii) Mean life:**

$$\tau = 1.443 \times T_{1/2} = 1.443 \times 30 = 43.3 \, \text{years}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\tau = \frac{T_{1/2}}{0.693} = \frac{30}{0.693} = 43.3 \, \text{years} \quad \checkmark$$

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam — Alternative Module Question]**
**(a)** Explain the various **units of radioactivity** (Becquerel, Curie, Rutherford) with their inter-conversion relations. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** A medical physicist receives a shipment of **Iridium-192** ($T_{1/2} = 73.8$ days) with a calibrated activity of **500 mCi** on the calibration date. Calculate:
  (i) The number of radioactive Ir-192 atoms in the source.
  (ii) The activity remaining after **45 days**.
  (iii) After how many days will the activity drop to **50 mCi** (the safety-handling threshold)? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution (a):

**Becquerel (Bq):** The SI unit of activity, defined as exactly one disintegration per second. $1 \, \text{Bq} = 1 \, \text{dps}$.

**Curie (Ci):** The historical/clinical unit, originally defined as the activity of 1 gram of pure Radium-226. $1 \, \text{Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \, \text{dps} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \, \text{Bq}$.

**Rutherford (Rd):** An older unit used in early British literature. $1 \, \text{Rd} = 10^6 \, \text{dps} = 1 \, \text{MBq}$.

**Inter-conversion:**

$$1 \, \text{Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \, \text{Bq} = 3.7 \times 10^4 \, \text{Rd}$$

$$1 \, \text{Bq} = 1 \, \text{dps}$$

$$1 \, \text{mCi} = 3.7 \times 10^7 \, \text{Bq}$$

**Sub-units:** For medical applications, the milliCurie (mCi = 10⁻³ Ci), microCurie (µCi = 10⁻⁶ Ci), and the kiloBecquerel (kBq = 10³ Bq) and MegaBecquerel (MBq = 10⁶ Bq) are most commonly used.

---

#### Solution (b):

**(i) Number of atoms:**

Convert 500 mCi to Bq:

$$A_0 = 500 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.7 \times 10^{10} = 1.85 \times 10^{10} \, \text{Bq}$$

Convert half-life to seconds:

$$T_{1/2} = 73.8 \times 24 \times 3600 = 6.376 \times 10^6 \, \text{s}$$

Decay constant:

$$\lambda = \frac{0.693}{6.376 \times 10^6} = 1.087 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{s}^{-1}$$

Number of atoms:

$$N_0 = \frac{A_0}{\lambda} = \frac{1.85 \times 10^{10}}{1.087 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.702 \times 10^{17} \, \text{atoms}$$

**(ii) Activity after 45 days:**

$$A(45) = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda \times 45 \times 24 \times 3600}$$

$$\lambda t = 1.087 \times 10^{-7} \times 3.888 \times 10^6 = 0.4226$$

$$A(45) = 500 \times e^{-0.4226} = 500 \times 0.6554 = 327.7 \, \text{mCi}$$

**(iii) Time for activity to fall to 50 mCi:**

$$\frac{A}{A_0} = \frac{50}{500} = 0.1 = e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$\ln(0.1) = -\lambda t$$

$$t = \frac{\ln 10}{\lambda} = \frac{2.3026}{1.087 \times 10^{-7}} = 2.118 \times 10^7 \, \text{s}$$

Convert to days:

$$t = \frac{2.118 \times 10^7}{86400} = 245.1 \, \text{days}$$

**Equivalently** (using half-life formula): $t = T_{1/2} \times \log_2(A_0/A) = 73.8 \times \log_2(10) = 73.8 \times 3.322 = 245.2 \, \text{days} \, \checkmark$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert half-life units** — many students plug $T_{1/2}$ in hours when $\lambda$ is required in s⁻¹. Always convert to the same time base before applying $T_{1/2} = 0.693/\lambda$.
> 2. **Sign confusion in the decay equation** — the exponent is **always** $-\lambda t$ (negative). A positive exponent would indicate *growth*, which is never the case for radioactive decay (except for daughter nuclides being produced).
> 3. **Mixing up mean life and half-life** — these are *not* the same. Mean life is **larger** than half-life by a factor of 1.443. A common slip is writing $\tau = 0.693 \, T_{1/2}$ instead of $\tau = T_{1/2}/0.693$.
> 4. **Unit-conversion slip-ups** — $1 \, \text{Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10}$ Bq, **not** $3.7 \times 10^6$ Bq (the latter would be 1 Rd). Examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 5. **Not stating the assumption** — the decay law assumes a *constant* $\lambda$ and *no* re-feeding from a longer-lived parent. If secular equilibrium applies, the simple exponential form does not hold.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **activity** $A = -dN/dt$ is the *rate* of disintegration; it is a positive quantity.
- The **SI unit** of activity is the **becquerel (Bq)** = 1 dps; the clinical unit is the **curie (Ci)** = $3.7 \times 10^{10}$ Bq; the **rutherford (Rd)** = $10^6$ dps = 1 MBq.
- The **radioactive decay law** is $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$, or equivalently $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$.
- The **decay constant** $\lambda$ has units of s⁻¹ and represents the *probability per unit time* that a given nucleus will decay.
- **Half-life**: $T_{1/2} = 0.693 / \lambda$.
- **Mean life**: $\tau = 1/\lambda = 1.443 \, T_{1/2}$.
- **Activity–atoms relation**: $A = \lambda N$ — this is the most-used equation in clinical medical physics.
- **Quick rule of thumb**: After $n$ half-lives, the activity is $A_0 / 2^n$. After 10 half-lives, less than 0.1% remains — this is the engineering rule for "safe decay storage."
- Radioactivity is a **statistical, not deterministic, phenomenon** — the law governs populations, not individual atoms.
- The decay law is **independent** of temperature, pressure, chemical state, and electromagnetic fields — this is why radioactive isotopes are ideal as biological tracers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Half Life Period
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT303 - MEDICAL PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Half Life Period

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Half-Life Period (T₁/₂ or t₁/₂)** of a radioactive nuclide is defined as the time required for the activity of a radioactive sample to decay to exactly one-half of its initial value, or equivalently, the time required for the number of radioactive nuclei present in the sample to reduce to **50%** of its original value.

Mathematically, it represents the time interval during which:

$$N(t) = \frac{N_0}{2}$$

where $N_0$ is the initial number of radioactive parent nuclei and $N(t)$ is the number of remaining radioactive nuclei after time $t = T_{1/2}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCBMT303 - Module 4):** The half-life period is treated as a critical radiological parameter that governs patient dosimetry, radiopharmaceutical shelf-life, radioactive waste management, and the design of brachytherapy sources.

### 1.2 Types of Half-Life Encountered in Medical Physics

In Medical Physics, the term "half-life" is not a single monolithic concept. It is classified into three operationally distinct categories:

| Sub-Type | Symbol | Governing Phenomenon |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Physical Half-Life** | $T_p$ | Intrinsic nuclear instability / radioactive decay |
| **Biological Half-Life** | $T_b$ | Metabolic clearance / excretion of nuclide by the body |
| **Effective Half-Life** | $T_{eff}$ | Combined effect of physical decay + biological elimination |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Melting Ice Cube"**
> Imagine an ice cube placed in a warm room. It does not disappear in one giant step; instead, it shrinks gradually. If we measure the time it takes for the cube to shrink to **half its current mass**, that interval is its "half-life." Now suppose we also periodically scoop out small pieces of the cube with a spoon (biological removal). The cube now disappears *faster*. The total disappearance rate is a combination of *melting* (physics) and *scooping* (biology). The combined rate gives us the **effective half-life**. This is precisely what happens to a radiopharmaceutical inside a human patient.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants

- **Decay Constant:** $\lambda$ (unit: $\text{s}^{-1}$ or $\text{h}^{-1}$)
- **Standard Conversion Factor:** $\ln(2) = 0.693$
- **Avogadro's Number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}$
- **Unit of Activity:** Becquerel ($\text{Bq}$) = 1 disintegration per second; legacy unit: **Curie (Ci)** = $3.7 \times 10^{10}\ \text{Bq}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic Flow of Radioactive Decay

1. **Step 1 — Fundamental Decay Law:** The rate of disintegration of radioactive nuclei is directly proportional to the number of undecayed nuclei present at that instant. This gives the differential form:

$$-\frac{dN}{dt} \propto N \quad \Longrightarrow \quad -\frac{dN}{dt} = \lambda N$$

2. **Step 2 — Solution of the Differential Equation:** Integrating the above first-order ODE yields the exponential decay law:

$$N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

3. **Step 3 — Activity Definition:** The activity $A(t)$ is defined as the number of disintegrations per unit time, which is mathematically $\lambda N$:

$$A(t) = \lambda N(t) = \lambda N_0 e^{-\lambda t} = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

4. **Step 4 — Definition of Half-Life:** At $t = T_{1/2}$, the surviving fraction is exactly $\frac{1}{2}$. Substituting:

$$\frac{N_0}{2} = N_0 e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$$

5. **Step 5 — Solving for T₁/₂:** Taking the natural logarithm of both sides and isolating $T_{1/2}$:

$$T_{1/2} = \frac{\ln(2)}{\lambda} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}$$

6. **Step 6 — Mean (Average) Life ($\tau$):** Although individual nuclei decay randomly, the *average* lifetime of a radioactive nucleus before decay is given by the reciprocal of the decay constant. The half-life is always **shorter** than the mean life:

$$\tau = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{T_{1/2}}{0.693} \approx 1.44 \times T_{1/2}$$

### 2.2 Effective Half-Life (Medical Physics Specific)

When a radiopharmaceutical is administered to a patient, two parallel processes reduce the radioactivity inside the body:

- **Process A:** Radioactive decay of the nuclei (governed by $T_p$)
- **Process B:** Biological excretion/metabolism of the nuclide (governed by $T_b$)

Since both processes act **simultaneously and independently**, the effective decay constant $\lambda_{eff}$ is the **sum** of the individual decay constants:

$$\lambda_{eff} = \lambda_p + \lambda_b$$

Converting to half-lives using $T = 0.693 / \lambda$, the universally memorized formula is:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{T_{eff}} = \frac{1}{T_p} + \frac{1}{T_b}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility in Medicine:** $T_{eff}$ determines the actual radiation dose delivered to a target organ (e.g., thyroid in $^{131}$I therapy). Misestimating $T_{eff}$ is a direct cause of dosimetry errors in radionuclide therapy.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | Radioactive decay law | dimensionless ratio |
| 2 | $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | Activity as a function of time | Bq or Ci |
| 3 | $T_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{\lambda}$ | Half-life ↔ Decay constant | s, min, h, days, yr |
| 4 | $\lambda = \dfrac{0.693}{T_{1/2}}$ | Decay constant ↔ Half-life | s⁻¹ |
| 5 | $A = \lambda N$ | Activity relation | Bq |
| 6 | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = 1.44\, T_{1/2}$ | Mean (average) life | s |
| 7 | $\dfrac{1}{T_{eff}} = \dfrac{1}{T_p} + \dfrac{1}{T_b}$ | Combined biological + physical | s, days |
| 8 | $m(t) = m_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | Mass of parent nuclide remaining | g, mg |
| 9 | $N = \dfrac{m \cdot N_A}{M}$ | Number of atoms in mass $m$ | atoms |
| 10 | $n = \dfrac{T_{1/2}}{t}$ | Number of half-lives elapsed | dimensionless |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Medical Utility

- **Diagnostic Imaging (SPECT/PET):** Choice of radionuclide ($^{99m}$Tc with $T_{1/2} = 6$ h, $^{18}$F with $T_{1/2} = 110$ min) is dictated by imaging duration.
- **Radiotherapy:** Permanent vs. temporary implants (brachytherapy) depend heavily on $T_p$ of $^{103}$Pd, $^{125}$I, $^{192}$Ir.
- **Radiation Protection:** Storage of $^{60}$Co sources uses half-life to predict source strength after years of shielding.
- **Pharmacy:** Determining the calibration time of a Tc-99m generator relies on parent-daughter $T_{1/2}$ relationships.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Linking Half-Life to Decay Constant

**Statement to Prove:**
$$T_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}$$

**Full Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Starting with the fundamental first-order decay differential equation:

$$-\frac{dN}{dt} = \lambda N$$

Rearranging variables to separate $N$ and $t$:

$$-\frac{dN}{N} = \lambda\, dt$$

Integrating both sides from initial state $(N_0, t=0)$ to final state $(N, t)$:

$$-\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN'}{N'} = \lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt'$$

The left side evaluates to a natural logarithm:

$$-[\ln N' - \ln N_0] = \lambda t \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \ln\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \lambda t$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$\frac{N_0}{N} = e^{\lambda t} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

Applying the **half-life boundary condition** $N = \dfrac{N_0}{2}$ at $t = T_{1/2}$:

$$\frac{N_0}{2} = N_0 e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$$

Cancelling $N_0$ from both sides:

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\lambda T_{1/2}$$

Using the identity $\ln(1/2) = -\ln(2) = -0.693$:

$$-0.693 = -\lambda T_{1/2}$$

Solving for $T_{1/2}$:

$$\boxed{\,T_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}\,}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The numerical value $0.693$ arises solely from the natural logarithm of $2$. It is **universal** for all radionuclides, regardless of their chemical identity or decay mode.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Effective Half-Life Formula

**Given:** A radiopharmaceutical with physical half-life $T_p$ is cleared biologically with half-life $T_b$. Prove that:

$$\frac{1}{T_{eff}} = \frac{1}{T_p} + \frac{1}{T_b}$$

**Derivation:**

Step A — The total rate of disappearance of radioactive atoms from the body is the **sum** of two independent first-order loss rates:

$$-\frac{dN}{dt} = \lambda_p N + \lambda_b N = (\lambda_p + \lambda_b) N$$

Step B — This composite equation is itself a first-order decay equation with an **effective decay constant**:

$$\lambda_{eff} = \lambda_p + \lambda_b$$

Step C — Using the established relationship $\lambda = 0.693 / T$:

$$\frac{0.693}{T_{eff}} = \frac{0.693}{T_p} + \frac{0.693}{T_b}$$

Step D — The factor $0.693$ cancels from every term on both sides, leaving:

$$\boxed{\,\frac{1}{T_{eff}} = \frac{1}{T_p} + \frac{1}{T_b}\,}$$

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Medical Physics Standard Problem

**Problem:** A patient is administered a dose of $^{131}$I (used in thyroid therapy). The physical half-life of $^{131}$I is $T_p = 8.04$ days, and the biological half-life of iodine in the thyroid is $T_b = 64$ days. Calculate the effective half-life.

**Solution:**

Using the effective half-life formula:

$$\frac{1}{T_{eff}} = \frac{1}{8.04} + \frac{1}{64}$$

Evaluating each reciprocal:

$$\frac{1}{T_{eff}} = 0.1244 + 0.0156 = 0.1400\ \text{day}^{-1}$$

Inverting to obtain $T_{eff}$:

$$T_{eff} = \frac{1}{0.1400} \approx 7.14\ \text{days}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** Although iodine atoms naturally leave the body slowly (long $T_b$), the rapid nuclear decay ($T_p = 8.04$ d) dominates the kinetics. The effective half-life (7.14 d) is therefore **shorter** than both individual half-lives, but **closer** to the physical half-life because $\lambda_p \gg \lambda_b$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Three Types of Half-Life in Medical Physics

The following Mermaid flowchart maps how physical, biological, and effective half-lives interact inside a human patient after radiopharmaceutical administration.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["RADIOPHARMACEUTICAL INJECTED INTO PATIENT"] --> B["RADIOACTIVE NUCLEI INSIDE BODY"]
    B --> C1["PROCESS 1: PHYSICAL DECAY"]
    B --> C2["PROCESS 2: BIOLOGICAL EXCRETION"]
    C1 --> D1["Physical Half-Life Tp"]
    C2 --> D2["Biological Half-Life Tb"]
    D1 --> E["COMBINED EFFECTIVE LOSS"]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F["Effective Half-Life Teff"]
    F --> G["DECISION: RADIATION DOSE DELIVERED"]
    G --> G1["Imaging Scan Timing"]
    G --> G2["Therapy Dose Calculation"]
    G --> G3["Patient Release Criteria"]
    G --> G4["Waste Management Storage"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Decay Cascade and Parameter Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N0["Initial Nuclei N0"] -->|decay over time| Nt["Remaining Nuclei Nt"]
    A0["Initial Activity A0"] -->|decay over time| At["Current Activity At"]
    Lambda["Decay Constant lambda"] -->|inverse relation| Thalf["Half-Life T1/2"]
    Thalf -->|T1/2 = 0.693 by lambda| Lambda
    Tp["Physical Tp"] -->|parallel| Combine["1 by Teff = 1 by Tp + 1 by Tb"]
    Tb["Biological Tb"] -->|parallel| Combine
    Combine --> Teff["Effective Teff"]
    Thalf --> Tau["Mean Life tau = T1/2 by 0.693"]
```

### 4.3 Concept Map — Decay Equation Variables

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Law["Radioactive Decay Law: N = N0 exp(-lambda t)"] --> V1["N: Surviving nuclei"]
    Law --> V2["N0: Initial nuclei"]
    Law --> V3["lambda: Decay constant s^-1"]
    Law --> V4["t: Elapsed time"]
    V3 -->|lambda = 0.693 / T1/2| V5["T1/2: Half-life"]
    V1 -->|multiply by lambda| A["Activity A = lambda N"]
    V2 -->|multiply by lambda| A0["Initial Activity A0"]
    A -->|differentiate| Rate["Decay rate: -dN by dt = lambda N"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** All three block diagrams emphasize a single engineering truth — the half-life is a *derived* parameter; the fundamental physical quantity is the **decay constant $\lambda$**. Every operational formula in medical physics ultimately traces back to $\lambda$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
Define **half-life period** of a radioactive nuclide. Derive the relationship between half-life and decay constant.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** Half-life is the time required for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) of a sample to reduce to half of its initial value.
- **Equation (1 Mark):** From $N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ and applying $N = N_0/2$ at $t = T_{1/2}$:

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$$

- **Final Result (1 Mark):** $T_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{\lambda}$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
Distinguish between **physical half-life**, **biological half-life**, and **effective half-life** of a radiopharmaceutical.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Physical Half-Life ($T_p$) (1 Mark):** Time for radioactive nuclei to decay to half purely due to nuclear instability, independent of any external environment.
- **Biological Half-Life ($T_b$) (1 Mark):** Time for the body to biologically eliminate half of the administered nuclide through metabolism and excretion.
- **Effective Half-Life ($T_{eff}$) (1 Mark):** Combined decay in a living system, given by $\dfrac{1}{T_{eff}} = \dfrac{1}{T_p} + \dfrac{1}{T_b}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the fundamental radioactive decay law and obtain the relationship between the decay constant and the half-life period. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A radioisotope has a half-life of 30 days. Calculate:
  (i) The decay constant. (2 Marks)
  (ii) The fraction of nuclei remaining after 90 days. (2 Marks)
  (iii) The mean life of the nuclide. (3 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**
- Starting equation: $-\dfrac{dN}{dt} = \lambda N$ (1 Mark)
- Rearrangement: $-\dfrac{dN}{N} = \lambda\, dt$ (1 Mark)
- Integration limits: $-\displaystyle\int_{N_0}^{N}\dfrac{dN'}{N'} = \lambda \displaystyle\int_0^t dt'$ (1 Mark)
- Result: $\ln(N_0/N) = \lambda t \Rightarrow N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ (1 Mark)
- Half-life condition: $N = N_0/2$ at $t = T_{1/2}$ (1 Mark)
- Substitution: $\frac{1}{2} = e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$ (1 Mark)
- Final result: $T_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{\lambda}$ (1 Mark)

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**(i) Decay constant (2 Marks):**

$$\lambda = \frac{0.693}{T_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{30} = 0.0231\ \text{day}^{-1}$$

**(ii) Fraction remaining after 90 days (2 Marks):**

Number of half-lives: $n = 90/30 = 3$

$$\text{Fraction remaining} = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^3 = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125$$

**(iii) Mean life (3 Marks):**

$$\tau = \frac{T_{1/2}}{0.693} = \frac{30}{0.693} \approx 43.29\ \text{days}$$

Alternatively using $\tau = 1/\lambda$:

$$\tau = \frac{1}{0.0231} \approx 43.29\ \text{days}\ \checkmark$$

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** What is meant by the **effective half-life** of a radiopharmaceutical? Derive the relation connecting physical, biological, and effective half-lives. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A patient is administered $^{131}$I for thyroid treatment. The physical half-life of $^{131}$I is 8.04 days, and the biological half-life of iodine in the thyroid gland is 64 days. Calculate:
  (i) The effective half-life. (3 Marks)
  (ii) The time required for the activity to fall to 10% of its initial value. (4 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Effective Half-Life (7 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 Marks):** Effective half-life is the time required for the activity of a radiopharmaceutical inside a living system to reduce to half of its initial value, accounting for both radioactive decay and biological clearance.
- **Statement of combined rate (1 Mark):**

$$-\frac{dN}{dt} = \lambda_p N + \lambda_b N = (\lambda_p + \lambda_b)N$$

- **Identification of $\lambda_{eff}$ (1 Mark):** $\lambda_{eff} = \lambda_p + \lambda_b$
- **Substitution using $\lambda = 0.693/T$ (2 Marks):**

$$\frac{0.693}{T_{eff}} = \frac{0.693}{T_p} + \frac{0.693}{T_b} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{T_{eff}} = \frac{1}{T_p} + \frac{1}{T_b}$$

- **Final Result (1 Mark):** Effective half-life is **shorter** than the smaller of $T_p$ and $T_b$.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**(i) Effective half-life (3 Marks):**

$$\frac{1}{T_{eff}} = \frac{1}{8.04} + \frac{1}{64} = 0.1244 + 0.0156 = 0.1400\ \text{day}^{-1}$$

$$T_{eff} = \frac{1}{0.1400} \approx 7.14\ \text{days}$$

**(ii) Time to reach 10% activity (4 Marks):**

Using $A = A_0 e^{-\lambda_{eff} t}$ with $A/A_0 = 0.10$:

$$0.10 = e^{-\lambda_{eff} t}$$

$$\ln(0.10) = -\lambda_{eff} t \Rightarrow t = \frac{-\ln(0.10)}{\lambda_{eff}} = \frac{2.3026}{0.1400}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 16.45\ \text{days}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in mean-life formula:** Students often write $\tau = 0.693 \times T_{1/2}$ instead of $\tau = T_{1/2} / 0.693$. The correct relationship is **division**, not multiplication. This single sign-direction error costs 1 mark consistently.
> 2. **Effective half-life arithmetic:** When computing $1/T_{eff}$, students forget to find a **common denominator** and add numerical reciprocals. Always convert to common denominator first.
> 3. **Forgetting the natural log:** For non-half-fraction problems (e.g., 10% remaining), the equation $\ln(N/N_0) = -\lambda t$ **must** be used, not the half-life shortcut $\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n$.
> 4. **Unit mismatch:** $\lambda$ must be in inverse seconds if $T_{1/2}$ is in seconds. Mixing days with seconds is a **silent killer** that costs 2 marks.
> 5. **Conceptual confusion:** "Half-life" without context is ambiguous in medical physics — always clarify whether it is **physical**, **biological**, or **effective**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Half-Life Period**

- ✅ Half-life ($T_{1/2}$) is the time for activity (or nuclei) to fall to **50%** of initial value.
- ✅ The constant $0.693$ is $\ln(2)$ — memorize it as a **universal constant**, not a nuclide-specific value.
- ✅ Core link: $T_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{\lambda}$ and $\lambda = \dfrac{0.693}{T_{1/2}}$.
- ✅ **Mean life** $\tau$ is **always longer** than half-life: $\tau = 1.44 \times T_{1/2}$.
- ✅ Three categories: **Physical** ($T_p$), **Biological** ($T_b$), **Effective** ($T_{eff}$).
- ✅ **Effective half-life formula:** $\dfrac{1}{T_{eff}} = \dfrac{1}{T_p} + \dfrac{1}{T_b}$ — this is a *reciprocal sum*, not a *direct sum*.
- ✅ $T_{eff}$ is always **less than** the smaller of $T_p$ and $T_b$.
- ✅ For $n$ half-lives elapsed, surviving fraction $= (1/2)^n$.
- ✅ Activity $A$ is **directly proportional** to $N$: $A = \lambda N$.
- ✅ Units check: $T_{1/2}$ and $t$ **must share the same unit** before applying decay law.
- ✅ For any non-half-fraction calculation, always use $N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ with $\ln$.
- ✅ Medical applications: Tc-99m generator elution timing, I-131 thyroid dosimetry, brachytherapy source calibration, and radioactive waste storage all depend on $T_{1/2}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement Methods - Interaction of Radiation with Matter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT303 - MEDICAL PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Measurement Methods - Interaction of Radiation with Matter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Interaction of Radiation with Matter** refers to the physical processes by which ionizing radiations (charged particles, neutral particles, and electromagnetic photons) transfer their energy to the atoms and molecules of the medium they traverse. The mode and efficiency of this energy transfer depend fundamentally on the **type of radiation**, its **kinetic energy**, and the **atomic number (Z) and density (ρ) of the absorber**.

In the KTU 2024 framework, this topic is divided into three primary radiation classes:

1. **Heavy Charged Particles** (alpha particles, protons, deuterons) — interact primarily via **Coulombic (electrostatic) interactions** with atomic electrons and nuclei.
2. **Light Charged Particles** (electrons, positrons, beta particles) — interact via **Coulombic forces, radiative losses (Bremsstrahlung)**, and **annihilation** (positrons only).
3. **Neutral Radiations** (X-rays, gamma rays, neutrons) — interact via discrete **quantum events** such as photoelectric absorption, Compton scattering, pair production, or nuclear interactions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** Every medical imaging modality (CT, PET, SPECT) and radiotherapy technique (linac, proton therapy, brachytherapy) is engineered around controlling and exploiting these specific interaction mechanisms. The choice of radiation type in a clinical protocol is dictated entirely by its interaction physics with biological tissue.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Billiard Ball on a Pool Table" Model

Imagine throwing different objects onto a snooker table covered with billiard balls (representing atoms):

- A **bowling ball (heavy charged particle)** moves in an almost **straight line**, pushing every ball it touches slightly aside. It loses energy in **many small, continuous steps** and stops after a well-defined distance (the **range**).
- A **table tennis ball (electron)** bounces around **wildly**, ricocheting off balls and occasionally flying off in a new direction. Its path is **erratic**, and it loses energy through both collisions and radiation (light emission).
- A **flashlight beam (photon)** does not push the balls directly. Instead, it can be **absorbed whole by one ball** (photoelectric effect), **bounce off** (Compton scattering), or even **vanish and create two heavier balls** (pair production) — but most of the beam passes straight through.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** The "heavier" or "more charged" the radiation, the shorter and more localized its interaction range inside matter. Photons are most penetrating; alpha particles are least penetrating.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants (Required for KTU Numericals)

| Symbol | Quantity | Value | Unit |
|--------|----------|-------|------|
| $c$ | Speed of light in vacuum | $2.998 \times 10^8$ | m/s |
| $m_e$ | Electron rest mass | $9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ | kg |
| $e$ | Elementary charge | $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ | C |
| $N_A$ | Avogadro's number | $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ | mol$^{-1}$ |
| $r_e$ | Classical electron radius | $2.818 \times 10^{-15}$ | m |
| $h$ | Planck's constant | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ | J·s |
| $\varepsilon_0$ | Vacuum permittivity | $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ | F/m |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Interaction of Heavy Charged Particles (α, p, d)

### Mechanism: Coulombic Stopping

A heavy charged particle of charge $ze$ and velocity $v$ traversing a medium interacts via the **Coulomb force** with:

- **Atomic electrons** → dominant energy-loss channel → **electronic (ionization) stopping**
- **Atomic nuclei** → infrequent but violent → **nuclear stopping** (small contribution for protons/alphas)

### Key Quantities

**Linear Stopping Power** ($S_{lin}$): Energy lost per unit path length.

$$S_{lin} = -\frac{dE}{dx} \quad [\text{units: MeV/cm}]$$

**Mass Stopping Power** ($S_{mass}$): Normalized by density to remove medium dependence.

$$S_{mass} = \frac{S_{lin}}{\rho} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{dE}{dx} \quad [\text{units: MeV·cm}^2/\text{g}]$$

### The Bethe-Bloch Formula (Non-Relativistic Regime)

For a heavy charged particle of charge $ze$, mass $M$, and velocity $v$ in a medium of atomic number $Z$ and atomic mass $A$:

$$-\frac{dE}{dx} = \frac{4\pi z^2 e^4}{m_e v^2} \cdot N Z \left[ \ln\left(\frac{2 m_e v^2}{I}\right) \right]$$

where:
- $N$ = number of atoms per unit volume = $\rho N_A / A$
- $I$ = mean ionization potential of the medium (≈ $10 \cdot Z$ eV for $Z > 13$)
- $m_e$ = electron rest mass

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** $S_{mass} \propto \dfrac{z^2}{v^2} \cdot Z$. **Doubling the charge** quadruples the stopping power. **Doubling the velocity** reduces stopping power to one-fourth. This is why alpha particles lose energy so rapidly and have a short range!

### Range of Heavy Charged Particles

The **range $R$** is the straight-line path length traveled before coming to rest:

$$R = \int_0^{E_0} \left(-\frac{dE}{dx}\right)^{-1} dE$$

A useful empirical approximation (Geiger's rule for alphas in air at STP):

$$R_{air} \approx 0.318 \cdot E^{3/2}$$

where $R$ is in cm and $E$ is in MeV (valid for 4–7 MeV alphas).

**Bragg Curve:** Plot of $-(dE/dx)$ versus residual range $R'$. Characteristic shape — energy deposition is low at entry, peaks sharply near the end (the **Bragg peak**), then falls to zero. This is the **physico-chemical basis for proton therapy's superior dose localization**.

## 2.2 Interaction of Electrons (Light Charged Particles)

Electrons undergo **three** energy-loss mechanisms simultaneously:

| Mechanism | Description | Importance |
|-----------|-------------|------------|
| **Collisional (Ionization)** | Coulombic scattering off atomic electrons | Dominant at low E |
| **Radiative (Bremsstrahlung)** | Deceleration in nuclear Coulomb field emits X-ray photon | Dominant at high E, high Z |
| **Nuclear Scattering** | Elastic scattering off nuclei (large-angle deflections) | Causes path tortuosity |

### Bremsstrahlung Yield

The fraction of energy radiated as Bremsstrahlung is approximated by:

$$\frac{(dE/dx)_{rad}}{(dE/dx)_{coll}} \approx \frac{E \cdot Z}{800}$$

where $E$ is in MeV. For a 10 MeV electron in lead (Z = 82): ratio ≈ 1 (equal contributions).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Energy ($E_c$):** The electron energy at which radiative and collisional losses are equal. $E_c \approx 800/Z$ (in MeV) for solids; $\approx 710/Z$ for gases.

### Range of Electrons (CSDA Range)

Electrons don't travel in straight lines due to large-angle scattering. The **Continuous Slowing Down Approximation (CSDA)** range is:

$$R_{CSDA} = \int_0^{E_0} \left(-\frac{dE}{dx}\right)^{-1} dE$$

Empirical formula (in g/cm²) for electrons in water/plastic:

$$R \approx 0.412 \cdot E^{1.265 - 0.0954 \ln E}$$

(where $E$ in MeV, valid for 0.01–3 MeV)

## 2.3 Interaction of Photons (X-rays, γ-rays)

Photons are uncharged; they interact via **discrete quantum events**. The probability per unit length is governed by the **linear attenuation coefficient μ**.

### Beer-Lambert Exponential Attenuation

$$I(x) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$$

where:
- $I_0$ = incident intensity
- $I(x)$ = intensity after thickness $x$
- $\mu$ = linear attenuation coefficient [cm⁻¹]

### Mass Attenuation Coefficient

$$\mu_m = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \quad [\text{cm}^2/\text{g}]$$

Independent of physical density — better for tabulation and inter-material comparison.

### Half-Value Layer (HVL)

$$HVL = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{\mu}$$

The thickness that reduces intensity by **50%**.

### Tenth-Value Layer (TVL)

$$TVL = \frac{\ln 10}{\mu} = \frac{2.303}{\mu}$$

### Mean Free Path

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{N \sigma_{tot}}$$

where $\sigma_{tot}$ is the total interaction cross-section per atom.

### The Five Primary Photon Interaction Channels

| Process | Mechanism | Energy Range | Z-Dependence |
|---------|-----------|--------------|--------------|
| **Coherent (Rayleigh) Scattering** | Photon scatters off bound electron; no energy loss | $< 30$ keV, high Z | $\propto Z^2$ |
| **Photoelectric Effect** | Photon absorbed → ejects orbital electron | $< 100$ keV (diagnostic) | $\propto Z^3 / E^3$ |
| **Compton Scattering** | Photon scatters off quasi-free electron | 100 keV – 10 MeV | $\propto Z$ (per electron) |
| **Pair Production** | Photon → e⁺e⁻ pair in nuclear field | $> 1.022$ MeV | $\propto Z^2$ |
| **Photodisintegration** | Photon ejects nuclear particle (n, p) | $> 10$ MeV | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Concept:** $\mu_{total} = \mu_{photo} + \mu_{Compton} + \mu_{pair} + \mu_{coh}$ — attenuation is the **sum** of all interaction probabilities because any interaction removes the photon from the beam.

### The Photoelectric Effect — K-Shell Approximation

The cross-section (K-shell dominant) is:

$$\sigma_{photo} \propto \frac{Z^{n}}{E_\gamma^3}$$

where $n \approx 4$ to $5$ (typically $n \approx 3$ to $4.5$ across the diagnostic energy range). It exhibits **sharp discontinuities (K-edge, L-edge)** when $E_\gamma$ matches a shell binding energy.

### Compton Scattering — Klein-Nishina Cross-Section

The differential cross-section per electron:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{d\Omega} = \frac{r_e^2}{2} \left(\frac{E'}{E}\right)^2 \left(\frac{E'}{E} + \frac{E}{E'} - \sin^2\theta\right)$$

where the scattered photon energy is:

$$E' = \frac{E}{1 + \frac{E}{m_e c^2}(1 - \cos\theta)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Compton formula shows the scattered photon is **always less energetic** than the incident (wavelength shift, demonstrated by Arthur Compton, Nobel 1927). This is the basis for **image noise** in CT and the **gamma-ray backscatter** in industrial gauging.

### Pair Production Threshold

$$E_{threshold} = 2 m_e c^2 = 1.022 \text{ MeV}$$

Required to conserve energy and create an electron-positron pair (rest mass + kinetic). The positron subsequently annihilates producing two 511 keV photons.

## 2.4 Interaction of Neutrons

Neutrons interact **only with nuclei** (no Coulomb force). Two main processes:

| Process | Description | Application |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| **Elastic Scattering (n, n)** | Bounces off nucleus; transfers kinetic energy | Most important in tissue (H nucleus) |
| **Inelastic Scattering (n, n′γ)** | Nucleus excited, emits γ | High-E neutrons |
| **Capture (n, γ)** | Neutron absorbed; compound nucleus emits γ | Thermal neutrons in BNC |
| **Activation (n, p) or (n, α)** | Transmutations: $^{14}N(n,p)^{14}C$ | Tissue damage, BNCT |

**Elastic Energy Transfer (max) to nucleus of mass $A$:**

$$\frac{\Delta E}{E} = \frac{4 A}{(1+A)^2}$$

Maximum transfer to hydrogen (A=1) = **100%** — this is why **hydrogen-rich tissue (water, fat) attenuates neutrons strongly** and why neutron shielding uses **hydrogenous materials** (polyethylene, water) rather than high-Z materials.

## 2.5 📋 KTU Formula Sheet (Comprehensive Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ | Photon attenuation (Beer-Lambert) | — |
| 2 | $\mu_m = \mu / \rho$ | Mass attenuation coefficient | cm²/g |
| 3 | $HVL = 0.693 / \mu$ | Half-value layer | cm |
| 4 | $TVL = 2.303 / \mu$ | Tenth-value layer | cm |
| 5 | $\lambda = 1/\mu$ | Mean free path | cm |
| 6 | $S_{mass} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{dE}{dx}$ | Mass stopping power | MeV·cm²/g |
| 7 | $S_{mass} \propto z^2 Z / v^2$ | Bethe-Bloch scaling (qualitative) | — |
| 8 | $R_{air} \approx 0.318 E^{3/2}$ | Alpha range in air (Geiger) | cm |
| 9 | $E_{threshold} = 1.022$ MeV | Pair production threshold | MeV |
| 10 | $E' = E / [1 + (E/m_ec^2)(1-\cos\theta)]$ | Compton scattered photon energy | MeV |
| 11 | $\sigma_{photo} \propto Z^n / E^3$ | Photoelectric cross-section | — |
| 12 | $\frac{\Delta E}{E} = \frac{4A}{(1+A)^2}$ | Max elastic energy transfer to nucleus | — |
| 13 | $E_c \approx 800 / Z$ (MeV) | Critical energy (rad = collision) | MeV |
| 14 | $\lambda_{Compton} = h / (m_e c) = 2.43$ pm | Reduced Compton wavelength | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** The attenuation equation $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ is the operational backbone of every **diagnostic X-ray system, CT scanner, gamma camera, and PET scanner**. Stopping power determines the **design of ionization chambers, dosimeters, and radiotherapy beams**. Pair production is the very mechanism that **creates the 511 keV photons detected in PET imaging**.
